### TheKid — 2013-02-04T14:33:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74059>

Hydro. Cortisone. Is. Magic.

Repeat that with me please.

Not sure about anyone else's issues, but I have raging adrenal fatigue apparently. Bascially, I make NO cortisol from about noon onwards, and it just ravages me. My doc wanted to narrow in on things, so we tested all over the map and looked at support supplementation first. So we took a swing with DHEA and T4 to no avail from a symptomological perspective. Although not optimal, my T3 and DHEA levels are now normal and my Reverse T3 also went from being sky high to normal. And my Vitamin D is stellar. So that left the elephant in the room, hypothalamic adreanl fatigue.

But don't you know, within 30 minutes of taking my first dose, I swear the chest pain, tightness, anxiety, coughing and whole bunch of mess simply disa-FREAKING-ppeared. It was like damn magic. Hope this continues.

Anyone else have experience with hydrocortisone? How did things go after the honeymoon? Thanks.

### ashryn — 2013-02-04T15:52:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74067>

The blog.. Which I just finished summarising says most people who have a rapid response find it short lived.. And low and slow is the way to go. I only know this because I literally just posted a summary on my journal!
Awesome to hear you've had good results

### Martin — 2013-02-04T16:20:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74071>

Oh, yeah.

Been there, Kid.

Back in '08, after two years of researching HC and finally getting over the "terror" of taking steroids, I bought some online from England (cheap, I might add).

Took a breath, and took a little.

O. M. G.

The "Eternal Ache" I'd had for ages melted away. My poor brain was less lost in fog, and I literally sprinted out to the workshop to take on my long-lost love of creating things in my shop. I started singing again - my wife noticed me outside chirping away - and I noticed my legs were really diggin' into each step instead of me dragging two meat-stumps around.

But I had to progressively take more and more until I was up to 70 mgs a day just to avoid this swirling, super-tired feeling that would come on when I was coming down. I was concerned, but it's what my body need to reach some level of homeostasis. In an illness, the bod can require like 200 mgs a day, so I rolled with it.

I came down with a sore throat one morning and had contractors arriving, so I gulped some down, knocked it out! I was up diggin' out with those legs in less than 45 minutes! I was a happy guy!

I did it for three months and towards the end, I could tell it was getting weird. A little like caffeine buzzing, slight aggression, I can see how trying to get in the middle of the body's natural mix of determining what it needs and when, was getting to be cumbersome.

I eschew taking pills, so I tapered off very slowly and successfully.

After that, I just continued to eat right, think right, and in a few months, I felt the best I'd felt in nearly two decades. I was flying high!

Then the "Great Sickness" came upon me, but that's another whole thread. But I will say, I've often wondered if I'd had enough HC to take at that time, I might have done better? I'll never know.

Hope this gives you some perspective. Best of luck!

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-04T18:57:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74090>

Oh yes - HC. I still have a bunch on hand. On the days after a night where I don't sleep very well, I battle higher heart rates more, and an uneasiness feeing in my chest. I save it for those days, and I usually find 5-10mg, in the am is all it takes to take the edge off. I use it about once every 3 weeks or so, and I don't worry about it.

I will also mention that the acetyl-l-carnitine had a similar impact on me when I started taking it. After a few days on it, I was cleaning out closets, the basement, stuff was getting done! 10 days in though, I crashed, had so little energy I felt like I couldn't lift my arms, or even talk. I read that it can add some thyroid resistance (used for treating Graves sometimes), and so I decided to discontinue it. Now I am back to my regular, non-energetic, wish I could sit around doing nothing self... I was considering trying a lower dose though, because of the good result I had.

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-04T19:16:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74092>

That's really interesting about the acetyl -l-carnitine! I'm wondering if it blocked the thyroid I've been taking and then caused the lower temps which I've been noticing. JK referred me to the EMF4 blog to come, so I'm waiting to read that too. What a wondrous web of complexity

I used HC for years on itching skin rashes on my hands...I'm sure it contributed to adrenal issues later... I gave up sugar a number of years ago, my skin settled down.

Kid...I hope the magic continues and your brain learns to make its own....I guess that's what we're all after isn't it??

### TheKid — 2013-02-04T19:53:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74098>

@Seahorse, thanks for the encouragement. Indeed I am trying to get my brain to make its own. But I'll be damned if Jack and other practitioners weren't correct about adrenal fatigue being a DEEP hole to get out of. If I remember correctly, in one of the blogs Jack said it takes 3-18 months to recover. I imagine the 3 months if for folks who either don't have it so bad or who are able to really tightly control all factors related to healing. For me, I can see how, living where I live, it might take 18 months. Daunting task; because I am so bad off that I am unable to withstand driving in traffic or having a meeting at work. If I had a trust fund, I'd retire to the family estate in a place like the Hamptons and CT in the ocean and go clamming daily. Don't have that option so it's sweet, sweet, hydrocortisone for now.

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-04T20:46:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74112>

The other thing that started happening to me while taking the carnitine at the LEF doses (2000-3000mg/day) was that I started being able to have "discussion" type conversations, even while in bed, before sleeping. I am normally the kind who can't have ANY disruption to my evening life, or I won't sleep until after 1am, and wake up extra early (5-6am) - a horrible night! I don't go anywhere in the evenings, because I can't handle it. I can't watch TV (pretty much anytime - the story stays with me too much), and especially not in the evening. Even picking up my dd one night at 9pm messed me up. So to find myself in a debate right in the bed (normally I completely avoid this), and then be able to let it go and have a normal night's sleep - I was really taken aback! I've got to try that stuff again.. I continued taking the ribose, and found no such effect.

MamaG I know exactly how you feel. Jack is super great in a lot of ways...but he is frustrating with give out information of which could be argued that since it sounds so 'complex' people won't argue against it and/or don't KNOW one way or another. Then he leaves you freaking hanging with no further information (or actionablity). Very frustrating and something about it rubs me the wrong way. The only thing I like what he says is about question everything and I guess know your own body. So I try to take a mix of Jack and a mix of Barry's approach. Obviously I feel much benefit from visiting this site in the last year, but I still make errors and the information is sometimes not enough for me.

Also The Kid posted on Gretchen's personal blog about wondering if trying to do all these protocols in the midst of neolithic life is in itself a mismatch (b/c short of COMPLETELY isolating yourself you cannot eliminate neolithic life completely). I have some testing and experimenting coming up (well not even until Fall and next winter) that will be different from this winter. It should be interesting. Also, health is dynamic so what was a problem for you in 2011 might not exist and instead something else happens that you have to fight through to figure out....but o well.

Good luck MamaG!!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-04T22:12:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74133>

But p.s.. I DO think taking Mg as isolated supplementation is at least worthy and valuable. Basics in my opinion. Mg + D3. I know someone in the comments of GNOLLS.ORG blog post (done in August 2011) said that they thought the best remedy/help for them personally was simply Mg+D3. Sometimes it might be the 'go back to basics most simple approach'. However I must say for myself, I thought I could get away with ZERO supplements and only rely on epi-paleo...but I was wrong. Sadly, I was so so wrong. I found trouble at end of November into December. So I learned my lesson (and also analyzed certain supps that seemed to help me in the past summer). I combine the two situations to help me in this 'new' situation.

Oi...lots to wrap my head around. hehe.

Pffft. Let's see about Jack:

He has super loads of money and is in position of power so that he can force his lifestyle (on others/his family) and be the one in control. He has oodles of resources etc. So for him what he does is different b/c basically he's a manic of sorts. For the rest of normal human beings who a. don't have all the resources like him and are b. not in control like he is....what do you do? ### Glamazon — 2013-02-06T05:01:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74386>

Jack earned what he got with hard work. Don't try and take that away. No one handed his success to him. His clean lifestyle is what makes him seem manic to average folks. I remember when he was getting shit from the paleo dogma crowd accusing him of being manic last year. No one seems to think that he has such high energy because he lives what he writes about.

It's a sad commentary on society when people who want to be optimal and live healthy are thought to be antisocial and crazy. Fortunately, my mom is on these protocols and she has seen significant changes in the last year in her fibromyalgia. Pain is almost gone. Flexibility is returning. She doesn't take sleeping pills anymore. She started grounding recently and her BP has come down before she takes her meds in the AM. Jack even asked her to write a guest blog about her experiences. She is 78. She doesn't even CT. Imagine what might happen if she did. She's also on Medicare, retired, and lives on Social Security. If she can do it, anyone can.

It took you years to get in bad shape and it is going to take some time to get out of it. The doc never promised over night results. He often said 2-3 years. This is not a Weight Watchers program or a Doctor Oz plan written for mainstream public who want to be spoonfed. These are major lifestyle changes required that are complex and tough. But, we can do it.

The changes that you have had should give you hope that the light at the end of the tunnel is not a train. I haven't felt this hopeful in all my life about having success.

What makes people successful is when they want other people to be successful. You get your dreams by helping others achieve theirs.

Have hope and stay strong.  Never give up.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-06T05:32:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74390>

Glamazon I am very happy to hear about your mother! That is inspiring to say the least.

Also, thanks for your words of encouragement. Some of what you wrote I was not sure if it was a little harsh but then it was followed up with more support or encouragement and like you are just meaning well. It's much appreciated.

Of course we are all only human at the end of the day sometimes, so frustrations WILL be vented from time to time. It's not possible to always agree and/or see the other person's perspective and so therefore (for me) I will end up 'questioning everything'.

\*Btw I did not know that 'being manic' is what JK got accused of last year b/c a. he never really told what happened (and is often quite vague in most things he says) and b. I don't read the other blogs really (lol) too lazy. So this was just how I was feeling based on my time spent here over the last year. That is all. It does not dismiss his 'hard work' or 'effort' but it should be noted that his reality is still quite different than many (most) people's realities and I think it is important to understand this fact.

Do I still follow so much of what I learned from this website, YES I do. Also I am very comfortable with grounding and connecting with the earth. I have always been naturally inclined this way anyway. (And I prefer a CT that is outdoors versus being stuck in some bath tub etc).

So please I hope it's known that I am not trying to bash anyone here that is not my intention in the slightest, instead it is to raise concern if I have a concern and just to question everything as is \[supposed\] to be encouraged by all of us.

What a great post.

I think many use their left brain way too much.. because that is the common way in todays world.

Jack wrote recent blogposts about thinking more with our "heart". That is it all about. Children live quite a lot in this way of thinking. We need to step out of our ego, we need to expand our awareness, humbleness, love..

Now, it all belongs to life, the rainy days and the sunshine. It is all so beautiful...!

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-06T14:41:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74409>

The other dynamic that is easy to fall into is how we project our own frustrations, misgivings and unknowings on those who are shaking up our world....and that can be a positive shake up or a negative shake up. It is human nature to project our own stuff onto others...a useful survival technique that can be very destructive without awareness. So whenever JK becomes a target of anger or frustration you can bet that the person feeling it is in need of clarity.

No one is making us do anything suggested on this site. We do not HAVE to experiment and learn what is presented here. Many of us are because good things are happening. When good things either stop happening or stall or never start the way we would like them to....that is not because JK is leading us down the garden path...it's because we don't have all the information yet, or maybe because we aren't willing to change enough, or who knows...the point is JK can easily become the target of our own frustrations. All leaders in all fields live with this dynamic. It can be very useful to understand and reflect on how easy it is to strike outward instead of looking inward.

It's also easy to try to "catch" somebody out who is presenting such a vast spread of knowledge. So Zorica...the fact that the Vit D message may be different one time from the next is all about context...recovering from an injury may change what supplements he's taking despite the seasons...etc. etc. So it is good to notice these things, but remembering context can prevent you from resenting the inconsistencies....and of course there will be inconsistencies in some details...but the message is very simple.

We can navigate and help each other in the process by staying open in the heart as Ingar spoke about above...the bad stuff and the good stuff. Remembering that what we say to each other here can potentially reach out to so many people. Processing issues with grace is part of living in a new way. Let's take the high road! all the best, SeaHorse

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-06T15:21:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74421>

And we all get frustrated sometimes. When I'm in a great place, I can put up with all kinds of idiosyncrasies from all kinds of people (and yep, Jack's no different than the rest of us; he's full of them, too). When I'm desperately frustrated with my struggles, that comes bubbling up and spewing out from time to time, IRL, here, & everywhere else.

I try to keep my trash to myself, but am not always successful.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-06T15:27:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74422>

> [Zorica Vuletic said:](/goto/post?id=74152)
>
> Pffft. Let's see about Jack:
>
> He has super loads of money and is in position of power so that he can force his lifestyle (on others/his family) and be the one in control. He has oodles of resources etc. So for him what he does is different b/c basically he's a manic of sorts. For the rest of normal human beings who a. don't have all the resources like him and are b. not in control like he is....what do you do? Isolate yourself away from family in order to 'get all protocols right'? Or...stress about the impact of said isolation b/c it distances you away from family and friends?
>
> Sorry to sound so bitter, but honestly it gets very ridiculous sometimes. Am frustrated to see also that so many people here are doing ALL THESE PROTOCOLS and supplements etc. and are still suffering so bad or that their health really is not improved. One aspect might have been improved only to have something else weird be uncovered...and for what? And for what of all the 'weirdness' and isolation we/you have to go through? I question that quite often. Like OK do I go out in the woods and live in a log cabin BY MYSELF just so that I can 'get all these things right'? \*Btw that doesn't sound like too bad of an idea sometimes lol. But I don't have the eccentric gene in me...at least I don't think I do to that level. ....

Pretty good time for me to chime in........Here is why some folks will never reach Optimal. Bad Thinking is your biggest obstacle. The mindset we are a collection of our past experiences.........That the past equals our future…….NEWS FLASH: Our biography is not our destiny, but our decisions however do 100% shape our destiny. Moreover, our decisions are what determines ultimate achievement and fulfillment in life. And Zorica this one is particularly just for you. **Lack of resources do not limit us EVER, but our lack of resourcefulness is the the only issue behind our failures.**

You don't know **SH!T** about me, or my history, but I love how you default to that belief. It tells me more about you and less about me. If you did, you might be awfully red in the face to see where I have come from. I promise you it far below your current situation. Your attitiude is your personal speed bump to Optimal.

**_No Rx, resource, money, situation can change that._**

Only your resourcefulness can, and it is clear to me that you are saying loudly you may not have enough........if that is the case, cowboy up, and get some, before life passes you by.

I would be quite careful to tell others that they cant do something............**focus on what you can do instead of what you can't.** You really need to go somewhere and think about this post...........you need to stew in this for a while but I bet you get more pissed off then see the silver lining........and again that tells me how you see the world through your eyes. I hope this hits you squarely between the eyes, because you and I do not see life the same way and I think this is **what really separates us** and puts us in different paths in life. I am just glad their are others here that do not feel as you do...........they would rather create their life than wait for somebody to hand it to them.

Are you truthful with yourself? Do you keep promises you make yourself? Are you quick to forgive yourself? Do you accept responsibility for your actions? Are you consistent with your personal goals? Do you genuinely care and respect the life you have been given?

Integrity is the welcome mat at your front door......... Integrity is telling ourself the truth about our life. And honesty is telling the truth to other people when they may not see it. It's not what we eat, but what we digest that makes us strong; It's not what we gain but what we save that makes us rich, and not what we read but what we remember that makes us learn, and not what we profess but what we practice that gives us integrity.

### Glamazon — 2013-02-06T15:34:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74425>

Inger, you are spot on about it becomes our new nature.

In Eastern Wisdom the goal is to become one with the Tao (the Way). When people ask me what that means (I teach meditation) I say: We learn to spontaneously dance in harmony with Nature. We no longer live in reaction with life.

That is difficult to do when you are not optimal. You are living your epigenetics and not in harmony with your ancestors.

Beginning to live and practice being optimal means choice has been given back to me what neolithic thinking has stolen.

In you and Jack I routinely see comments and behaviors that are characteristic of those who have learned to live in harmony with The Way. Good on you!

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=74405)
>
> What a great post.
>
> I think many use their left brain way too much.. because that is the common way in todays world.
>
> Jack wrote recent blogposts about thinking more with our "heart". That is it all about. Children live quite a lot in this way of thinking. We need to step out of our ego, we need to expand our awareness, humbleness, love..
> And yeah.. it all requires hard work. At least in the beginning. In the end it becomes our new nature and we get transformed and then it is not hard anymore. But we have gotten spoiled and that is hard work to change that. Exept.. if we see where we are going it is not hard anymore.
> Now, it all belongs to life, the rainy days and the sunshine. It is all so beautiful...!

### TheKid — 2013-02-07T11:39:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74608>

Wow, and I thought we were just going to talk about the wonders of hydrocortisone. So much for that.

@Jack, something occurred to me that might be helpful here. At least for me, I feel like I know quite a bit about your theories, philosophies, expertise and protocols. However, I don't know so much about you and your personal journey. I am wondering if you might share some of the more personal stuff so that people can possibly better relate, if that's not too personal for you.

On a number of occasions you've talked about how far below many of us you were before you started your journey. I'd be interested in knowing about that experience and how you overcame obstacles in a more detailed manner other than generally changing your thought process. This would be helpful, because it might dispel any preconceptions that "Jack doesn't understand my pain."

For instance, you know my case fairly well and some of the rather extreme challenges I have been facing. So for me personally, I would be interested in knowing if you experienced something similar or at least on par with the magnitude/intensity of my challenges. And more importantly, I would like to know what it was that helped you maintain your faith in the process, maintain your sanity and maintain your focus so that you were able to pick yourself up off the ground and keep at it.

In the absence of this narrative, I think it's easy for people, when frustrated with their own challenges, to attribute your success to whatever material resources you have. Indeed, you have some level of material success that gives you options that might not be available to others. But there are plenty of people with wealth and possessions who suffer from poor health and neolithic disease. So wealth and opportunity is only a secondary factor in the change process. And with your "3 labs + a detailed personal history" approach to consultation, I think you have shown that cost can be managed. Heck, just eating real food will save you money in the long run. So money is indeed not necessarily a limiting factor in this process.

I have read much of your motivational writing; however, I am not sure that that conveys the same notion of solidarity with the masses that I'm talking about.

Some of this might boil down to your writing style, which surely conveys your enthusiasm and depth of knowledge, but does not always, in my opinion, connect as well as you might like. Language is as magical as the complex and confounding science you explain. Master your words and I think everything else will follow.

survivorship does not require connecting with the masses.......in fact it requires the opposite. You need to make the critical decisions early to save yourself first. That is rule number one in all survivorship training........it is the same in biology. If you can make the correct first choice you pay the biologic toll. Biology requires us to embrace paradox and ambiguity even when it seems completely opposite to what you have been told your whole life............when you sit in that stew long enough you realize all life is based upon randomness..........And then you see other people who see the world as you do (Black Swans).......they just do not realize what is written about those random events pertains to their modern life.........To gain from chaos you need to make sense of it.......because evolution gains from its randomness........the gains are in future generations and not your life........I believe you even asked me about this very topic once we spoke...........Well, its true............when you understand the rules of engagement in life it makes decisions easy if you choose to survive over choosing the luxuries of the modern world instead..........Loaded answer and has nuclear implications.........but that is where we are headed........and I know you know it..........I am not sure how many others here do.

The Kid, good thinking. I do agree with what you're saying. I think I'll throw in 'context' here in that I don't think I was complaining about 'lack of resources' or 'wealth' per se but my written word certainly reflected that as the main point for sure. I believe I've come a long way with the resources I DO have and I am actually very grateful for what I have. That should be clear. I think I was getting upset about something else entirely, but was using the wrong words which ended up talking about something else (that is only somewhat related).

Yes, language is a powerful tool that can convey or confuse. I know I given the wrong message through simple wording. I don't pretend to have all the answers and I'm stumbling and feeling my way through just like everyone else (yes even the ones who gets labs are still stumbling and feeling away too). It's all trial and error. Health is dynamic, so what works now may not work in the future, or what didn't work in the past may work for you now....even tests and labs can't help with that. Life. It's why I want to enjoy my journey now instead of focusing so heavily on some end point destination. I do not want to have any regrets when I'm 90+ w/ 0 disease (lol). I want meaning and value in my life and I want to SHARE my experiences with those who are close to me.

Hydro cortisone....yes, so do tell how it's Magic. LOL

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-07T16:28:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74639>

while I think its interesting to know what Jack's journey was like, his journey has no bearing upon mine or anyone else's in my opinion. Why? N=1. How Jack did x,y & z, may not be what's going to help me. I may have to do a different combination of different things.

I have come to discover, that while my health challenges may difficult, they are surmountable. They are also no more challenging than what others are going through here, we all have something to fix, and how we get to optimal is entirely different than how Jack got optimal, even though we're using the same tools. I may have setbacks due to things I cannot control at this time, but knowing that, I can still make changes to mitigate the negative.

the ability to conquer adversity, is how you perceive the adversity. adversity, chaos, risk and change make us stronger and better in the long run. this is no easy road we're walking/running, and each person's progress is different and we'll all get to optimal in our own time, not on anyone else's. This is why Jack keeps harping N=1. Trust your N=1, your body knows what its doing. We all may not be where Inger is with regards to trusting our instincts and n=1, but we're all on the path to get there. We all may not have had success like weight loss, or reversal of disease - but we're on our way, its going to take a while as many of us have been broken for a long time. Apply the tools, trust your n=1, advocate for yourself with your doctors, and embrace that which is holding you back and change your paradigm.

I wrote the following on my FB wall this morning: When you pay attention to the world around you, you'll anticipate which doors will open & when. Your opportunities are no longer surprises they're known entities

Think about what that means for you on this journey.

### SeaJane — 2013-02-07T16:57:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74644>

> [TheKid said:](/goto/post?id=74608)
>
> Wow, and I thought we were just going to talk about the wonders of hydrocortisone. So much for that.
>
> @Jack, something occurred to me that might be helpful here. At least for me, I feel like I know quite a bit about your theories, philosophies, expertise and protocols. However, I don't know so much about you and your personal journey. I am wondering if you might share some of the more personal stuff so that people can possibly better relate, if that's not too personal for you.
>
> On a number of occasions you've talked about how far below many of us you were before you started your journey. I'd be interested in knowing about that experience and how you overcame obstacles in a more detailed manner other than generally changing your thought process. This would be helpful, because it might dispel any preconceptions that "Jack doesn't understand my pain."

In my experience, the inspirational level of personal success stories in motivating me is often a function of how open I am to the possibility of change for me. If I believe (even deep inside) in the possibility of change for me, then I tend to see inspiration and motivation in many places. If I am in a lower life condition, then my mind will twist even the most relevant success story to show me why I can't change. My mind will find ways to show how it won't work for me (e.g., oh, that isn't as bad as me, oh they have more money to do things, oh they don't have children, etc). So while I would honor hearing Jack's story as a way to know more of his journey, I am not letting that get in my way of what I want for me. It is easy to become distracted from attending to what needs to be done. I am focusing on me and what is so for me and working from that place forward. I feel that is the expedient path for me.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-02-07T21:43:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74680>

I'm jumping into this late, but felt I could add a coupla cents.

> [Zorica Vuletic said:](/goto/post?id=74152)
>
> ... so that he can force his lifestyle (on others/his family) and be the one in control. He has oodles of resources etc. So for him what he does is different b/c basically he's a manic of sorts.

Having just had the pleasure of hanging out with Jack in a variety of natural environments and temperatures, I can say without a doubt that I did not find him to be manic at all. Jazzed? Pumped up? Excited? Willing to share? Yeah, all that. I also found Dr. K to be contemplative, willing to listen, and humble when conditions lead to that. I find it completely off-base to suggest that Dr. K _**is forcing**_ his lifestyle on anyone here on his website, or within his own family. He offers up a tremendous amount of resources and information that has been filtered and condensed through his own understanding and education, and presents some possible paths forward for people to take or leave as they will.

> [Zorica Vuletic said:](/goto/post?id=74152)
>
> ... For the rest of normal human beings who a. don't have all the resources like him and are b. not in control like he is....what do you do? Isolate yourself away from family in order to 'get all protocols right'? Or...stress about the impact of said isolation b/c it distances you away from family and friends?

Perspective is key with these questions. What is someone to do who does not have oodles of money. Well, I don't currently have oodles of money, I am working on that, but for now I go with what I do have...and what is that? Access to the mountains, rocks I can ground on conveniently located out my office door, access to cold water both naturally and in my house, the ability to meditate, my own knowledge of the outdoors, medicinal herbs, intuition, and my willingness to be very, very aware of my self and my place. I don't have access to tons of fish since I'm so far inland, but I find sardines, scallops, shrimp, and salmon whenever I can. Would I like fresh oysters every week, you betcha! Am I complaining? No.

The only one in control of you is you. I am the only one in control of me. When you have kids, you quickly realize that you cannot control another human being. So it's absurd to think that unless you can control others with regards to the information presented here that you'll never get to optimum. I take what I've digested here and made changes in my own life. My husband, out of curiosity has tried some of the things I've suggested. Your statements are basically an either/or situation. Either you need resources and control, or you won't get to optimal. Either you isolate yourself or you stress about it. Neither of those things is in line with what I hear Jack saying.

Listen to yourself. Be aware of how things affect your body, your emotions, your labs. Tweak things when necessary. Add, remove, tweak some more.

With that, I'm going to go walk barefoot across a canyon so that I can go sit on a rock and meditate for a while.

### DarleenMB — 2013-02-07T21:57:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-magic.5733/post-74683>

Holy crap. I'm not sure I'm glad I wandered in here or not.

First of all Jack shared enough of his story on his blog (see his About Me) to convince me he sure as hell knows what he's talking about. I don't need any more personal info than that. I'll write my own story, with a little of his guidance, thank you very much.

Secondly, the more I read here in the forum the more amazed I am at how broken most people really are and how very very lucky I am to not be in worse shape than I already am. Actually I'm stunned by the fact I am still walking around.

finally, my take on "victimology." It is a colossal waste of time. If you want to have a pity party then go have one and be done with it. Then pick yourself back up and keep on truckin'. Life's too damned short. Better to keep moving than lie in the dirt and get trampled by the horde.

### TheKid — 2013-02-08T06:31:00+0200

So anyway, hydrocortisone is an interesting thing.

My very first dose was indeed like magic. Subsequent doses? Not so much.

This is my first week, so I'm giving it time. It was strange though, I actually felt worse the next 2 days. Before I starting taking it, I would crash physically and emotionally by the end of the day. That would come and go. I assumed hydrocortisone would end that rollercoaster. Unfortunately, I crashed harder for 2 days and continued with reactive hypoglycemia after dinner.

Things have settled down more the last 2 days, so I am waiting to see what the 7 and then 14 day trend look like.

# Thread: Help.  Cravings beating me down.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 89

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-04T17:58:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74082>

It's back. It's back!

Ugh.

6 months trying to get to where I could comply with the LR, finally got it down and completely, 6 months of absolutely NO CRAVINGS at all. Then 6 months of them coming very very slowly back. Now I'm being assaulted every day around 4pm, although that time is getting earlier and earlier each day. It's driving me bananas; I can thinking of nothing but how to get more sweet food all afternoon and evening. The only sweet food in my house is whole yogurt & raw honey & whole organic apples, so that's what I'm hitting. And the result is HUGE belly, sweating at 2am every night, hard stomach, evening misery & dire evening fatigue.

Sigh.

I'm still eating around 80gm protein every morning (at least 7oz of it is seafood) & a huge blob (close to 1/4C) of bacon grease. Still taking ox bile & HCL & Mg, D3, fCLO (think I should up this), and much more.

(All else is the same or improved compared to this time last year - more CT, less EMF, less artificial light, etc.)

Help, prayers, advice, all is welcome.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-04T18:09:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74085>

Hi MamaGrok, you have my sympathies.
In your situation, I would take about 100mg 5-HTP around 1-2pm then. You should notice an effect in a few hours. You may be getting low in tryptophan/serotonin, and your body knows that carbs will raise it quickly. Or maybe your dopamine is high and it is out of balance with your serotonin.

### kathylu — 2013-02-04T18:19:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74087>

Somewhere recently, JK made the comment that when cravings hit "you are not craving food, you are craving electrons". So maybe walk outside barefoot with a cup of hot tea with 10 g d-ribose and coconut oil in it? Take a 500mg capsule of acetyl L carnitine too. I have also used 1/2 tsp of L-glutamine powder directly on my tongue. That stops sugar cravings for me. Good luck MG!

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-05T03:18:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74218>

MamaGrok - sorry to hear you are having a hard time. When I had a lot of cravings my first stop was the l-glutamine and about 1/8 of a teaspoon under the tongue often worked wonders. I also found that 5 HTP also made a big difference. However these did need to be taken prior to the cravings kicking in badly, the 5-HTP especially. To start with I needed 3 a day, slowly dropping it to one at night before being able to drop it in November last. I think I read somewhere that Jack isn't a fan of 5-HTP, but you don't want to binge either. One was not a substitute for the other. if I needed 5-HTP then l-glutamine wouldn't work and vice versa.

I do find it heartening, though I don't like you suffering, to find that others find it not smooth sailing too even when they are doing everything right. It means we can take such hiccoughs in our stride and not give up.

My own pet theory is that as we go downhill our bodies choose the best of the bunch of hormonal cascades they could use. And these keep changing as we go further and further down. When we start coming up we can't go straight to optimal but have to come up through those same pathways. My theory is that on the upward journey some of these hormonal cascades, while being physiologically better than the previous one my body had been using, might have undesirable qualities from my experiential point of view. What I have to do is to hang on in there as my body continues to adapt and my body is able to step up to the next physiologically better hormonal cascade and hopefully out of those unhelpful symptoms. Not that the idea helped when the cravings were uncontrollable, though it did help with the guilt afterwards.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-05T03:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74221>

melatonin, or Ca AEP work for cravings in an emergency.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-05T03:57:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74225>

I am so screwed. I am in a setting where I have to eat so freaking late!! It's really driving me up the wall. I'm so afraid for all the bad things it will do hormonally after all my efforts to try to at least stay afloat. But it was choose to go back to Ottawa and basically not afford proper food...or come back home and a. eat foods I really shouldn't and b. eat so freaking late!! :S

Short of 'isolating' myself away from family and acting 'weird' I don't know what to do. Maybe I'll get a rude response, but I am serious and I am panicking over this which causes stress which is obviously not good either. So either way my body is getting extra stress for this. :-(

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T05:34:53+0200

I think that the cravings are coming from my taste buds in the case of d-ribose. Like artificial sweeteners, once it gets to the body, it doesn't act like a true sugar, but in the mouth, it perpetuates the cravings that are already there.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=74221)

Ca = Calcium
AEP = ?

I'm glad for help for emergencies, but they're happening every day and I need to hit the root cause!

Zorica, for me, I pick the most dire sounding health problem I have and use it - "I need to eat dinner a lot earlier b/c having it later is doing some unexpected things to my Hashimoto's disease / whatever. ### ColdBren — 2013-02-05T06:51:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74251>

I think its the yogurt....totally throws off all progress for me and causes bloating too. Also, I dont' know if this is approved or not, but when I go through these periods of cravings I put tons of truvia on anything and everything. It seems to be the only artificial sweetner that satisfies that sweet tooth but ironically makes the cravings go away...unlike splenda etc. An apple would also feed the cravings monster in me. Sorry you are goiong through this, the anxiety also drives it so hopefully you can let go of the frustration, no one can be cravings free 100% of the time.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-05T10:13:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74258>

I'm not much help - I was taking 5-HTP for about 15 months to deal with both cravings and depression. It was better for the depression than anything any doctor ever prescribed over 6 decades. I was depressed from the age of 3 till November last year. At the same time I was mostly good on a paleo diet 20/21 meals per week. But fruit was my downfall. Despite cutting wayyyy back, any really sweet fruit would set me off, so I cut right back. At Christmas I decided I would try Jack's stuff seriously so went on a mussels and fish binge and things changed hormonally in lots of tiny ways. Amongst other things the cravings dropped right back and they don't bother me at the moment, except for the one day I ate too many grapes about 3 weeks ago. The good thing about the l-glutamine is that an eighth of a teaspoon would work for 4-12 hours so it wasn't expensive. It isn't emotionally satisfying like sugar is, but after the first few minutes I forget about it as long as I find something else to do so I don't dwell on the lack of the sugar hit.

Not sure I notice anything from that yet but I am excited to see what happens.

I am hesitant to add the acetyl l-carnitine due to the thyroid suppression which I don't need. Any experience with that?
Sometimes when I take d-ribose, I feel that I could skip my thyroid dose but I don't.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-05T15:25:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74280>

True measure of personal leadership is done by the quality of the leaders they leave behind as their legacy, not by the number of followers they garnered. Great leaders are like great conductors who demonstrate their ability to create an environment where the artistry of others may emerge so that their teams can perform in excellence! The conductor is the only person in the orchestra who “doesn’t make a sound." Like musical notes, they have their specific place and timing; their part is to play through the sharps and flats to produce a symphony of excellence to the ear. Conducting does not demand or control. Conducting well aligns people according to their unique talents and gifts and guides them in order that their whole life is in harmony. A well-conducted life perceives, believes, and receives its own dreams, while helping others reach their own dreams. A well-orchestrated life delivers a high yield. Today begin your own masterpiece, and ditch the yogurt.......

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T16:06:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74284>

Let me be clear.

I ATE THE YOGURT AND FRUIT BECAUSE OF THE CRAVINGS.

THE YOGURT AND FRUIT DID NOT CAUSE THE CRAVINGS.

> I think its the yogurt. An apple would also feed the cravings monster in me.

I'm not a fan of caps lock, but I need to emphasize this somehow. I have eaten yogurt on three days in the past year, all because cravings were driving me to the point of insanity, and I decided that whole plain yogurt, and/or a piece of whole fruit would be a better idea than driving to the store and buying and eating an entire cookie cake.

The cravings have driven me to food madness again, and in the midst of it, I am trying to make "better" choices rather than horrible ones. The question is, why are the cravings back?

(And I don't mean normal wishfulness for tasty foods I can't have. I can overcome those. I mean insane cravings that won't let me think about anything else other than "don't indulge. don't indulge." Also, there is the reality that I was 100% cravings free for 6 months, not even those normal "well, it would have been nice if I could have had that" kind of feelings.)

### diane — 2013-02-05T16:56:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74286>

Mamagrok - I totally get where you're coming from. I have the same thing - drives me crazy. i can control them for short periods of time, but they come roaring back.

### PaulaRichards — 2013-02-05T18:35:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74294>

Wish I had words of advise. I was craving free for almost 3 months and, coincidence or not, they were back with a vengence the first day I took the d-ribose. I know, I know, it isn't metabolized like sugar, etc but something triggered the craving insanity that day. I will try the d-ribose again but am waiting until I'm back to being craving-free for another month or so.

I know you want to get to the root cause but what happens if you drink some olive oil or eat some pure fat when you have the cravings, will they go away? Since I had my consult with Jack that tidbit has saved me from insanity a few times!

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T18:48:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74295>

That used to work. It hasn't the last week. The first thing I do when a craving hits is eat a full big meal of protein and tons of fat. Doesn't stop 'em.

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-05T20:00:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74299>

I wish I could help you. I keep returning to this thread. My problem is similar, in that I never feel satisfied/full. If I could be eating all the time, that would be just fine with me. Of course carbs answer it the most, but plenty of other options fit the bill. I remember when I used to eat a meal, and then not feel like eating when it was done. I would love to return to just that - carbs/starches/LC/epi-paleo/whatever.

### vkiernan — 2013-02-05T20:13:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74301>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=74295)
>
> That used to work. It hasn't the last week. The first thing I do when a craving hits is eat a full big meal of protein and tons of fat. Doesn't stop 'em.

Just wondering if this is a hormonal thing. We women are forevering changing and sometimes I get the horrendous urges too. Sometimes not and that is the weird part. You know your thyroid is messed up a little now so this might also have something to do with it if the t3 can't get into your cells. My sugar cravings came back at x-mas and hasn't left me yet. I fed it once and that is all it takes! Mabye you need more sunshine. Spring is on its way.

Jack told me once that my brain is programmed to like sweet and I have to retrain it to like salt. So everytime I want something sweet, go for the salt. I can tell you I don't always practice this but I eat kale chips a lot now with my meals and I am starting to like salt. Sometimes even more than sugar.

I wish I had an answer for you but unfortunetly I am the same as you. We will pull ourselves out though all in good time.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T20:52:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74309>

Yes, we will. There's certainly no going back or giving up.

I love salty. I already salt everything (highest quality salt) and love salty foods. I don't even really like sweet foods anymore, which is oh so frustrating. I'm pawing pawing digging for something sweet, shove it down, and don't even like it, but a desperate "need" for more.

I think it's notable that I don't have these cravings at all until at least 2pm. I'm sure that means something.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-05T20:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74310>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=74295)
>
> That used to work. It hasn't the last week. The first thing I do when a craving hits is eat a full big meal of protein and tons of fat. Doesn't stop 'em.

This may be stupid to suggest, but what if the cravings are not pathological. Why is everything that doesn't perfectly fit the epi-paleo template pathological? (Just questioning everything here). If you have totally sorted your eating and killed your cravings for months and months now and suddenly they rear up, maybe there is wisdom in that. I can relate in that I get what I would loosely call 'cravings' but are actually like un-fightable signals for certain food sometimes. About a month ago, it was yogurt (and sometimes, during that same period, cheese). I realise I'm not 'supposed' to be eating that but I said to my husband 'it is as if I will literally go crazy if I don't eat this food.' So I decided to just eat it. Every day for several weeks I ate yogurt and most every day I also ate sheep's/goat's cheese. I was a bit worried but I was literally miserable if I didn't. And guess what? The cravings stopped almost as suddenly as they started. I saw no bloating, no weight gain, no bad side effects at all (no I don't have labs to prove that). I am convinced I needed something - calcium? K2? - more than I was supplementing/already in my diet. I have occasionally had the same reaction to other foods, such as berries. (I've also had the reverse where a 'perfect' food - lately it's been lamb's liver -makes me sick.)

I realise you are saying it is for anything sweet that you are feeling this craving. But, hell, that can be legit can't it? If you're not fully in fat-burning mode and you're not creating enough energy via the fat-burning pathways, maybe you need something extra? Barry talks about honey to fuel the energy he expends? Maybe you are depleted in some way. I realise this goes against the cardinal rule that we don't ever need any exogenous glucose (though I wouldn't see full fat plain yogurt in that category and find it interesting that it helps). Just thinking out loud. I suppose my bottom line is that I don't think you are pathological and I'm arguing that the symptoms may not be either.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T21:02:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74312>

I had exactly that happen before, when I first allowed myself fat after 15 years without. I craved butter straight, and ate close to a stick a day for a month. After a month, I just didn't need it anymore, and couldn't be persuaded to have even a bite of butter straight.

I think I clearly needed the K2.

But that doesn't seem to be what's going on this time. It's a strong \*sugar\* craving, not a dairy craving. I want cupcakes. Jolly Ranchers. 52 oranges, one after another. I settled for yogurt because a) it was in the house and b) it's not sugar and c) it makes a great vehicle for raw honey.

Same craving I've had for over a decade.

I do believe that there is something physiological driving it. There is almost certainly something I \*need\* that's out there. But no matter how much I eat, it doesn't seem to go away. I can eat a quart of yogurt every day, plus a ton of candy and a ton of ice cream and a ton of granola and brussels sprouts and apples, and still I want to eat more of it, the only thing stopping me being physical pain from having eaten so much. I spent years doing just that, trying to fill myself with nourishing, nutrient-dense foods (CW "healthy", then 3 years of WAP style, then 2 years primal) and no matter how much of a wide variety of foods I ate, I couldn't fill whatever that need is. The only thing that ever worked was the BAB/LR.

So I'm left guessing it's pathological, or a completely misplaced "need" for something not really needed.

Regardless of cause, I can't go on eating thousand sof calories of sugary foods every afternoon and night forever, so I gotta figure something out!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-05T21:06:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74313>

Ah, OK, I can hear the difference in what I've experienced and what you're dealing with now. I still argue that you are not pathological however.

As far as the carnitine goes, I take one in the morning and one at night and it's only been a week but not feeling anything from that yet, good or bad. I am waiting for something to happen but the carnitine is the one mito supp that I haven't felt any changes from it.

### RiverNeighbor — 2013-02-05T22:13:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74322>

I have cravings pop up now and again. After much reflection I believe that for me the cravings come these days when I'm not in "even keel" mode.... i.e., I have an increased level of stress. Yes, sometimes even good stress. Sometimes it'll happen and I won't think that I have any stress and I'll realize that we've been couped up inside several days because it's rainy, or that it really did stress me out when the kids were fighting in the car and I was trying to find my way around 8 lanes of California rush hour traffic, or that I'm really excited about going on a trip. I guess maybe it's not even that one of those things might happen... it's more like if the stress/calm ratio gets skewed more toward stress then I start to experience more cravings. I think body-mind portion of wellbeing can be a big part of cravings.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T22:16:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74323>

The cravings started to peek out in June, after 6mo of none at all.

I added seafood in September - a lot of it. I decreased beef/pork/lamb/goat proportionately to make room, but found that I couldn't have less than 4oz of red meat in the morning or cravings would be out of control.

The cravings have continued to increase. I actually had a few binges in November. I have avoided them narrowly through December, entirely in January, and had trouble again the last few days.

Seafood has been constant since about October, when I found th ebalance that kept cravings from goign bananas.

I haven't done anything about the lab results, since I still don't know how to approach limiting things that may result in increasing other things that could very well also be problems (like lamb, goat, salmon, oysters, cod, etc.).

I totally appreciate all the help brainstorming here! Lord knows my brain ain't exactly up to par these days!

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T22:21:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74324>

My stress is way up, but not objectively. The stressors in my life are not all that high, certainly lower than they've been at other times in the past ten years.

But I can't handle much at all. I'm finding myself curling up in a ball on the couch and telling the kids in low tones what to do - they're cleaning the house, making a lot of the food, and coming one at a time for school. I tell them I just can't yell to call them or instruct or chastise them anymore; it gives me a headache. This has also come in the last few weeks, and precedes the latest increase of cravings.

I don't get a sense that stress is driving the cravings, though. I get a sense that whatever is making me less able to handle stress is also making me crave.

Adrenals? But at the same time, taking my NDT two hours before I wake is doing amazing things for my morning. I no longer wake with a massive headache, feeling like unslept death, after 9hrs of solid sleep.

It's worth repeating that I added this circadian T3 protocol about 5 weeks ago, and started iodine 2.5 weeks ago.

Sigh. Stress used to roll off me like water off a duck's back. I did my whole EE major in 1.5 years, then in three months graduated, got married, started my first job, bought a house, & got pregnant - and felt no stress at all. Easy peasy. Wish I could have those days back. I do not like feeling so weak & vulnerable to what goes on around me.

### Lyndra — 2013-02-05T22:29:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74325>

Just thinking out loud here - cravings got worse as the length of daylight shortened. Have you looked at D3 levels? Do you supplement?

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-05T22:32:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74326>

Lots and lots of D3 - 4,000IU all summer plus sun; 8,000IU all winter. My D3 had dropped from last Mar to last Sept. Strange that it dropped over the summer, but I've been very vigilant about taking it daily ever since.

Really good point!! I feel like this happens for me sometimes too. I will use dates as an example. Something about dates I can get a craving for from time to time and I can eat quite a lot. Once I finish a huge serving though, I don't feel for it again. Example, last week I had extra craving for dates, so I ate a lot. But the next day I was like 'ugh...I couldn't eat another date'.

Perhaps MamaG you are more worried about the 'downslide' and bad consequences of such cravings, like bloat and weight gain. I have to admit that I am stupidly vain (still 27 you know) and honestly if I didn't have to 'worry' about getting fat and disgusting then I would ENJOY life wayyyy more (LIKE THE REST OF THE WORLD). It is a shitty motivator :-( and the only thing that helps support and is more sustaining is when I think of the back end of my life and wish for disease free in my old age. But for my vain self at this point in time...yes I care too much how I look. I DO know that this is not very sustaining of an attitude to have so I am lucky that I can at least see my back end of life too. But yes...I hate having to live what I feel is a 'restrictive' life (and sometimes STILL have 'problems' which makes it even more painful), but I try to convince myself that when I'm 90 and disease free, IT WILL HAVE ALL BEEN WORTH IT!

So again it's how to play that fine line between balance for health and sanity right? Good luck MamaG and thanks Kiwi for your good point.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-06T01:31:38+0200

Do you think JK saw my post above? LOL.

> [Zorica Vuletic said:](/goto/post?id=74339)
>
> Really good point!! I feel like this happens for me sometimes too. I will use dates as an example. Something about dates I can get a craving for from time to time and I can eat quite a lot. Once I finish a huge serving though, I don't feel for it again. Example, last week I had extra craving for dates, so I ate a lot. But the next day I was like 'ugh...I couldn't eat another date'.

yes, I can certainly imagine how this could happen. It has never happened for me, though, with anything sweet. I simply cannot get tired of them. I once discovered dried dates at my local HFS and ate massive quantities of them every week for two years straight. (As just one example.)

> Perhaps MamaG you are more worried about the 'downslide' and bad consequences of such cravings, like bloat and weight gain. I have to admit that I am stupidly vain (still 27 you know) and honestly if I didn't have to 'worry' about getting fat and disgusting then I would ENJOY life wayyyy more (LIKE THE REST OF THE WORLD)....
>
> So again it's how to play that fine line between balance for health and sanity right?

At 36 and with 5 children, I'm still vain, but far more worried about. I do worry about bloating, b/c of what more dire gut issues it may indicate, but for the most part have decided not to make my food choices based on it, b/c EVERYTHING but meat & fat (and even those, if I don't take enough ox bile) causes me to bloat. I intend to add back well-cooked greens, some berries, & occasional sweet potatoes come spring.

Honestly, I've spent enough years believing that I could just willpower my way to a moderate consumption of fruit/starches/FODMAPs/etc. that I know how that works in my body. It doesn't. It leaves me miserable, grumpy, fatigued, bloated beyond imagination, & non-stop craving.

And yes on your last point!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-06T02:10:58+0200

### Eve — 2013-02-06T02:13:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74365>

I'm not sure if this will help but wanted to add my expereince...
For the past several months, I was having insatiable hunger and cravings. I could eat 1.5 lbs of salmon, feel phyically very full in my stomach, but I still wanted to binge on sweets etc. I was putting off having my thyroid retested and finally did so...To make a long story short, I had gone "hyper", although my ft4 looked normal. I am speculating my ft3 was on the high end, but the lab didn't run it that time. I think the feeling hyper was due to adrenal problems, after reading the STTM site. I am speculating there was some "pooling" going on.

During the previous months, I kept upping my NDT, because I felt hypo and didn't clue in on the hyper symptoms (i.e., insatiable hunger, having difficulty falling asleep -- feeling wired but tired). So, I slowly reduced my dose from 3 grains to 2 grains. The insatiable hunger went away for the most part. And then on Sunday I started the T3CM method, and now the hunger/cravings have gone away for the most part. I still have some cravings, but I attribute this to having a terrible leaky gut and having to eat only fish everyday.

I read that you already are doing the T3CM method, but maybe you are on too much NDT and having some temporary pooling going on?

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-06T03:18:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-74375>

UNDOUBTEDLY I have pooling. My T4 is out the top of the range and FT3 is WAY out the top (6 something). RT3 is 450ish. Ridiculous. I had used the STTM protocol to increase my NDT and felt better with every dose, \*never\* hyper, but while the T3 is clearly benefiting me, I'm still collecting RT3 like it's stamps.

Certainly your experiencing is interesting here - I don't have insatiable hunger; it's fine in the morning through to mid-afternoon. I have no trouble falling asleep; that's better than it's been since I started primal (before that, interestingly, I used to fall asleep the moment my head hit the pillow). I have no jitteriness, BP & pulse are still very low, temp is finally 98.6 so yay there, no weight loss (had 3lb one week last month and nothing but gain ever since).

IDK. Could it be hyper signs? It sucks to be hyper and have none of the benefits.

I'm scared to death of reducing my dose anymore. I don't want to lose what I've gained (outside of the 2hr dump inthe afternoon, I'm doing about 7/10, up from 3/10 this tim elast year).

> Symptoms of low adrenal function are **chronic fatigue that worsened as the day progresses, low stamina**, reduced resistance to allergies and infections, cold intolerance, **cravings for sugars and starches, low blood pressure, low body temperature, difficulty concentrating** or working under pressure, decreased appetite, **low blood sugar, depression, excessive need for sleep**, and joint and muscle aches. ### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T14:45:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-76101>

I will never do an all seafood day again.

I knew I shouldn't. I knew that every time I have seafood without land meat and/or land animal fat, I get crazy cravings. But I thought, it's a Friday during Lent; I should at least try.

I got hungry. Really hungry. True hunger, not cravings. I figured, true hunger, I can deal with that. It's part of the fasting.

Then like lightning, before I realized it, it turned into monster cravings. Before I knew it, I was at the store buying yog-raisins, dried fruit, & fudge, and eating it all in the garage.

I will never do an all seafood day again.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-02T18:38:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-78632>

Well, it's been close to two weeks with no infractions. No carbs at all. My goal was to get to Easter, then add back in sweet potatoes & greens & small amounts of berries, very slowly. Update:

\- **No real cravings in over a week**. I'm on close to two weeks sugar-free (dairy, carbs, etc., too).
\- **Still very bloated**. Legs still up 1", wrist still up. **Weighed this morning - up 11.5lb from six weeks ago.** It must be all water, b/c I really don't see fat anywhere. All water & bloat. Maybe a bit of fat on love handles.
\- Still off d-rib. Forgot DHEA the last three days. I'll add d-rib back in a few days or after bloating dies down, depending on what my gut says. I do fear d-rib contributes to cravings b/c of the sweet taste.
\- **Getting eye twitches** despite 3 Mg L-threonate in AM & 3 Mg Orotate in afternoon. ?! What?! Very disturbing. I haven't had these since addressing my malabsorption issues over a year ago. And I've never had them while actively taking Magnesium regularly.
\- Waiting for Cholacol ox bile to come in again. I really don't think the Nutricology is working for me like cholacol & Jarrow did.
\- Physically & mentally feeling MUCH better. The last week I have had sleepy hours each day, but no need to crawl and hide. Had one morning of snapping at everyone, the night after I forgot to turn the router off. Forgot again last night; we'l see what the morning brings.
\- NDT holding steady at 2-2.5 grains/day.

It really is wonderful being able to work every hour of a long, mentally trying day and at the end still have mental & physical energy to spare. Certainly there is still plenty of room for improvement, but wow, what a breath of fresh air. Of course, part of me is waiting for the other shoe to drop, if this is yet another Hashi's attack - I hope and pray it isn't.

But my total results since beginning to implement the Mito Rx one supp at a time = 11.5lb gained. LOL.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T15:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-cravings-beating-me-down.5736/post-79073>

I agree; those are the behavioral steps you can use to distract yourself from cravings, but they only work for a while. When the cravings are bombarding you 24/7, you have to keep coming back to "no" to yourself constantly, and it destroys quality of life to keep avoiding the sugar.

His steps do work for some people with minor cravings.

But I've found that when my body is in balance, the cravings disappear entirely, and there is nothing to avoid at all. That's the place where I love to be. In fact, I'm there now, although I don't really have any explanation as to why I'm there now, but wasn't 6 weeks ago.

Of course, I'm still holding on to the water/bloat/fat weight gained during the three small indulgences of that time. Still have 9.5lb to lose.

# Thread: Dos and Don'ts of Mixing Supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 36
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-04T23:05:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/post-74150>

Suggestions on what supps should be taken with other ones and which should be avoided taking together. We are all taking a few different things combining our personal Rx's and I think we all can learn and share.

Vitamin D needs to be taken a few hours separate from Mg.

Vitamin C needs to be taken a couple of hours later than Iodine.

Anyone else have ideas?

### Lyndra — 2013-02-05T04:40:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/post-74233>

I would appreciate info regarding timing - e.g. don't take d-ribose before bed because it will wake you up.

### TheKid — 2013-02-07T10:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/post-74603>

One thing I would like to note about supplementation is that, as they say, "you can't prove a negative."

I think a lot of people are really searching for a demonstrable effect in some more immediate time frame. As Jack has said, things get better slowly, but tend to fall apart quickly.

I have noticed many people really expecting to "feel" much better taking supplements. As sick as I am, I have had that hope as well. But when it comes to your run of the mill supplements, I don't think that is a realistic expectation. Will they slowly make you feel better? Yes. But that process may move so slowly that you will not be able to track it unless you are journaling consistently over months or even years. Only then would the pattern of improvement become perceptible.

In my experience, the only things that make you feel something with any sort of immediacy are hormones and pharmaceutical drugs designed to be intense and fast acting. I just started hydrocortisone, and it was the first time something changed immediately for me.

As far as supplements go, I am taking D-ribose and a laundry list of other things. My expectation is that they will slowly heal and that they will prevent the onset of further disease. But as I said, you can't prove a negative, so that prevention will never be perceptually felt.

### diane — 2013-02-07T15:22:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/post-74616>

TheKid - you make a good point. Sometimes I don't realize what something is doing until I stop. A symptom or feeling comes back, and then I go back and figure out what I've changed - and often it's stopping a supplement. Some ND's suggest stopping some supplements for a week or so every couple of months so your body doesn't get too used to it. There are some supplements I don't do that for - but I will for new ones so I can assess whether it truly is the "miracle" that someone has professed. LOL.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-07T15:27:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dos-and-donts-of-mixing-supplements.5746/post-74617>

ITA, TheKid, as my health fell apart so slowly that I didn't even realize it was health (as opposed to laziness & gluttony) for years.

I have been journaling consistently for 5 years now, so I have a system of rating how I feel and have been able to see how various foods & supp's do (or don't) make changes.

I'm making note of whether I feel an immediate change with d-rib b/c there are several here who \*have\* felt immediate changes with it, and just adding my experience to the pot. It may not do anything immediate \*for me\*, but may take time, and/or may need to be combined with other things for any of them to have a cumulative effect. There's no telling what will happen! That's why I'm adding the supp's slowly and systematically, journaling the whole while, looking for patterns.

I also look for changes if/when I stop a particular supp or protocol. I've learned a lot from that, too.

# Thread: CoQ10...company's response to my question about Ubiquinol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulaRichards — 2013-02-06T03:14:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/post-74372>

[http://www.aor.ca/products-page/aging/cogni-q-4/](http://www.aor.ca/products-page/aging/cogni-q-4/)

When I researching what and where to buy the mito rx supplements I came across this product but wondered whether it contained Ubquinol or Ubquinone so I emailed the company. Was a bit surprised by their response....(copied & pasted from the email)

"Ubiquinol is a marketing gimmick. No scientist worth his salt even backs this
product.

Ubquinone has multitude of clinical scientific studies, Ubiquinol has two or so
and both were funded by Kaneka the Japanese manufacturer someone who has skin in
the game. The idea behind Ubiquinol is silly if one understands Redox reactions.

Redox reactions occur in pairs- oxidation and reduction. Ubiquinol the reduced
form is highly unstable and as soon as it gets into the stomach it will oxidize
into the ubiquinone form. Why pay 3 times the price for an ingredient that has
the clinical science for over 25 years? Plus even if the ubiquinone is in the
oxidized form it will get reduced as soon as the ubiquinol form gets oxidized.

AOR Tech
visit [www.drnibber.com](http://www.drnibber.com) for indepth and latest issues in nutritional science. An
alternative viwepoint to the celebrity media
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
(End of email)

I didn't end up buying this company's product, mainly because I assumed it didn't contain the recommended ubiquinol and it took them almost three weeks to reply to my question!

So...Given the fact that I don't understand the science behind much of what I read (brain fog for a few years now) I don't know what to make of this. Is there any truth to what is in the email or are they just trying to sell their product by trying to defend the ingredients. Thoughts anyone???

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-06T03:28:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/post-74379>

They are the scammers........there are over 1000 articles on ubiquinol in pub med. That is an embarrassing response.

### PaulaRichards — 2013-02-06T04:24:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/post-74385>

Thanks Dr. Kruse. i didn't know what to make of the "science" they tried to explain but it certainly didn't sound like a very professional response. I'm glad I didn't buy any of their products!!!

### santovin — 2013-08-20T01:53:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/post-93163>

I understand this is a late, but the response you received from the company was in fact quite accurate, and was not any sort of scam. Within our mitochondria, coenzyme Q (ubiquinone) goes through a very simple redox reaction and gets reduced (gains electrons) to form ubiquinol. Its whole purpose is the transfer of electrons to create ATP within our cells. After it transfers these newly gained electrons, it becomes oxidized into ubiquinone all over again to repeat the cycle. This reaction is not catalyzed by any enzymes, and therefore the marketing ploy that we "lose the ability to convert to it" is completely false. If we lost the ability to convert ubiquinone to ubiquinol, our body's would cease to function due to insufficient ATP production needed to survive. Studies nowadays are so skewed and biased that you really cannot trust them unless you know every detail. The only difference between the two is just one simple, easily accessible chemical reaction that our bodies are very capable of doing by themselves.

As one who has a degree in biochemistry, and having worked in a nutrition store for 3 years, I can assure you my confidence in all of the information provided. Ubiquinol is the scam, and is not any bit better than ubiquinone because as your responder said, it gets oxidized right back to ubiquinone the moment it hits your stomach acid. It pains me that doctors who are educated in the subject have not spoken louder against it. My advice is to save yourself the money and just buy ubiquinone, though ultimately it is your decision. Best of luck and take care!

### alanmjones@gmail.com — 2013-08-20T02:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coq10-companys-response-to-my-question-about-ubiquinol.5760/post-93168>

[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/a...far-superior-to-one-typically-being-sold.aspx](http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2011/06/29/this-form-of-co-q-10-found-far-superior-to-one-typically-being-sold.aspx)

[http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2007/jan2007\_report\_coq10\_01.htm](http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2007/jan2007_report_coq10_01.htm)

_As shown in the chart above (Figure 1), one clinical study used 1200 mg per day of ubiquinone CoQ10 to achieve blood concentrations of 3.96 mcg/mL. Based on recently published absorption studies, just 150 mg per day of ubiquinol would provide virtually the same high CoQ10 blood levels as 1200 mg of enhanced-delivery ubiquinone CoQ10._

### MeghanK — 2013-08-20T17:54:19+0300
