Destiny, if you don't mind my jumping in here, but by your quote above I sense your frustration, but I think that you are missing what Dr. Kruse is getting at.
I know that you are probably already aware of the following, but for completeness, let me summarize:
DHEA - Dr. K says 275-400 ug/dL of DHEA for women with 7-keto being preferred for
women. Note that the top range is 400 ug/dL. Dr. Kruse says that a high DHEA level
implies that IL-6 is low, which is a good thing for any type of cancer. A high DHEA level
also implies high melatonin, which is the number one antioxidant in the brain and
major protector against all epithelial cancers (i.e. breast cancer).
So I can understand your focus on DHEA. But here is another quote from Dr. Kruse,

> "Pulsed EMF does the same thing thermodynamically as an asteroid does to water (it evaporates water). \* It does something else tooâ€¦â€¦.it blocks the secretion of melatonin from the pineal gland which alters all circadian cycles too."

IMMHO, I think what Dr. Kruse is trying to point to you is that you are focused on the wrong thing, your focus should be on increasing your electrons/ATP which in turn will increase your DHEA/melatonin/etc.

Go re-read Dr. Kruse's long post beginning with

> "In the reality of Optimal..."

and I think you may realize the golden nugget that he is giving you.

### Psyche — 2015-10-21T21:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-s-and-teststerone-results.5794/post-180092>

Thank you for the response, Jack. My particular N=1, I suppose. It makes me wonder about my unusual reaction to Prozac- the only side effect I had when it was once prescribed to me is intense sleepiness, so much so that I could not continue taking it.

**Circadian rhythms in the eye: the physiological significance of melatonin receptors in ocular tissues.**
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18316227](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18316227)

The circadian signaling molecule, melatonin, is secreted into the circulation from the pineal gland, _**and is also produced within specific ocular cells such as retinal photoreceptors, ciliary epithelial cells, and perhaps cells of the lens.**_ Melatonin appears to entrain many aspects of the biological clock via activation of specific G-protein-coupled integral membrane melatonin receptors. Melatonin receptors have been identified in many ocular tissues, including the neural retina, retinal pigment epithelium, ciliary body, cornea, sclera, and lens.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-08T19:40:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-canadians-handle-snow-storms.5808/post-74899>

On my FB page Mitch Baird from the First nations just posted a pick in his speedo shoveling 8 inches of snow.......I meet Mitch at Paleo Fx last yr and wrote about him here: [http://www.jackkruse.com/my-top-ten-paleo-fx-moments/](http://www.jackkruse.com/my-top-ten-paleo-fx-moments/)

[https://www.facebook.com/notes/mitch-baird/month-3-of-ctketopaleo-whatever/10151168303631856So](https://www.facebook.com/notes/mitch-baird/month-3-of-ctketopaleo-whatever/10151168303631856So) now I’m 3 month in on my winter experiment with fat loss. During the Christmas holidays I stayed really strict to the diet and really tried to focus on the bigger picture of seeing what would happen if I keep going down this path of eating/health/training/cold exposure etc.

1) Cold tub has dropped to 46 degrees as of last night…my days in the tub have been good. I get in 5-6 times a week and when I can’t or I’m away I have turned the shower way down. Temp has dropped from around 50-52 last month to 46 as the temp has followed the weather. At 50-52 I am ok to stay in to 20 minutes without much problem, but now at 46 I am good after about 15 mins. Using Pranayama breathing techniques have been a big help. Ujjayi breath really help to relax in the tub. I have stopped shivering now and seem to be used to it without much in the way of discomfort. Things are progressing well here.

2) Yoga has been going well but I am beginning to teach more which can held back my time for personal practice. My kettle bell work has been increased as well, I am doing 2 sessions a week for 20 mins per session, these have gotten a lot easier and I can tell my strength has increased in my overhead press and squats. I’m using the same 30 lbs bell but I am definitely more controlled. My weight is 181lbs and I’m about 5’6” as of yesterday. No idea what my body fat percentage is but according to the BMI chart I am obese. (ok) here her posts his six pack pics.......LOL
3) Diet has been the same. No change on that front. I do like peanuts and like many in the paleo community I try to avoid them AMAP but similar to wheat they are in so many foods they are tough to avoid. I’ve been a lot more conscious on avoiding these foods. So peanuts are all the way out now as I had some last week and my stomach paid for it. Live and Learn. Loving Liver right now and Beef heart. if you don't eat organ meat start. that is all.4) Body fat seems to be evening out as maybe I have gotten to a place my body is comfortable. But the kettle bells and yoga have helped me with my core strength that now I feel very balanced in the way I feel. Julie’s parents came to visit and her mom was very sick and so was her dad. After going to the doctor her mom had pneumonia and dad the flu and we hung around them for 3 days. I took my vitamins each day ( D3 Magnesium Krill and Salmon oil)and extra vitamin C each day when they visited and I thought for sure I was going to get sick. That plus many sick people at my work you always think something has got to happen. But luckily nothing. (knock on wood)

I attended a few of the Idle No More protests in my area, and not just in the native community but in the overall population there seems to be a dis-satisfaction with government and big corporations. But so many of the people fighting against the government for sovereignty and engaging big corporations for better accountability willingly eat and drink big corporate products which puts them on a beeline course to the biggest government program there is, Health Care. If more people opted out of big corporate food then avoided the big government health care system to me that would be the ultimate form of sovereignty, personal sovereignty that depends only on yourself to make decisions on your health. Not some government bureaucracy. Not sure why we think government and big corporations never have our best interest in mind when it come to the environment, taxes, treaty rights, but are perfectly altruistic when it comes to the food we eat and the health care we receive? If anyone can educate me on why I’d love to know.

You got to see his pics in the link......

# Thread: Kruse is deadly wrong in condemning all high intensity exercise.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LightForTheNight — 2013-02-09T20:00:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/post-75050>

PS this was supposed to get posted in moster thread, not adrenal RX, appreciate if it could be moved.

Mitochondrial redox state correlates with Ca2+ spark activity. Ca2+ sparks develop fastest in fibers poor in mitochondria and slowest in cells rich in mitochondria. The appearance of ca2+ sparks in permeabilized skeletal muscle cells depends on the fibre's oxidative strength and that misbalance between mitochondial ROS production and the fibre's ability to fight oxidative stress is likely to be responsible for unmasking ca2+ sparks in skinned preperations.

Skeletal muscle depends on ATP supply to meet its energy demands. There are three major sources of ATP in muscle: creatine phosphate, anaerobix glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation. The relative contribution of each ATP source varies among muscle fibre types. Type 1 (slow-twitch, oxidative) and type IIa (fast-twitch, glycotic-oxidative) fibres are rich in mitochondria. They rely for their ATP production on oxidative phosphorylation. In contrast, type IIb (fast-twitch, glycolytic) fibres, are MITOCHONDRIA POOR and have very effective glycolytic ATP sythesis.

What this means is that **fast twitch musles correlate with Mitochondrial REDOX state**. If you want to build a strong mitochondrial base FOR YOUR BODY, if you want health then working out type IIb fibres is one of the single best things you can do for yourself (period).

During short intense exercise, glycogen in the muscle is used up (by IIb fibres).
Sugar is then shuttled to these cells and all is well.
However if glycogen stores are never depleted sugar gets shunted into fat instead, resulting in obesity.

Depleting glycogen activates hormone sensitive lipase so that fats are mobilised from body stores and made available for energy supply (good for people trying to lose fat).
If sugar in the blood stream following a meal cannot be taken up because glycogen stres are full, then this excess sugar sticks on to other things creating Advanced Glycation End products or AGE's , which accelerate the normal aging process. One such example is glycosylated haemoglobin which is measured to assess blood sugar control in diabetics.

Good fast twitch fibres = good mitochondrial function = good cardiovascular fitness.

Look, Jack has great ideas, and I've learned a ton about the ETC, mitochondria and circadian biology. And I think, no I KNOW he is right that it is at the ROOT of neolithic disease. But this bullshit he preaches about waiting 2 years to become leptin sensitive is crap. He is probably right that we need electron dense foods and most his advice is good-great BUT HIGH intensity exercise, for a VERY short duration is only going to result in MORE fast twitch IIb fibres, which is going to be positively correlated with mitochondrial REDOX state. This means keeping calcium where it needs to be and exceptional CARDIOVASCULAR health.

Do NOT think that I'm advocating endurance training, or any crap like Cross Fit. I'm saying that very short duration HIGH intensity exercises are ESSENTIAL for sublime mitochondrial function and I don't give a sh\*t what Jack says, he is WRONG, he might be right about limiting carbs, but he is DEAD wrong about avoiding all high intensity very short duration exercises. I fully expect this to be deleted as I'm right and it contradicts Jack who seems to have Alec Baldwin's God Complex. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8g2dkDh4ov4](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8g2dkDh4ov4)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2013-02-09T20:13:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/post-75051>

curious, do you think the genetic base of having more or less fast twitch/slow twitch fibres make a difference? As in if someone does a full genetic profile, and finds out they have lots of fast or Slow fibres - should that play into what exercise is most beneficial for overall health?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-09T20:24:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/post-75053>

Funny that is not what I said.......it is what you thought you heard.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-09T20:25:17+0200

### LightForTheNight — 2013-02-09T22:24:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/post-75071>

@caveman, a person's slow twitch:fast-twitch:super fast twitch is definitely changeable over your lifetime. I don't have the studies off hand but if you want them I'll find them.

@tellmisty, vo2 max is fine to measure but it's not necessarily predictive of mitochondrial function. A marathon runner may score very high on vo2 max because slow twitch muscle fibers are so much denser in mitochondria than fast twitch and will TF give you a higher vo2 max score, this is not predictive of health realistically though. What you want is Efficienct mitochondria, which is indicated by things other than vo2 max, maybe cytochrome - c for example.

Jack has said point blank that HIIT is not for people who are leptin resistant and I disagree ENTIRELY with that statement, HIIT increases insulin and Leptin sensitivity and lowers resting glucose.

@**Jack Kruse** I acknowledged in my thread that **I disagree with both cross-fit and endurance exercises**, as I consider them one and the same. I don't need to be shown why, you do a good job at proving it, but I already know they are bad... And you, **JACK KRUSE**, said the words "**Not if your LR...........it means you need more electrons......before you can lose them again**" in response to "**Aren't anaerobic exercises like HIIT good for mitochondrial function?**". TF telling the VAST majority of obese and overweight individuals **NOT to do HIIT**. What you said leaves little room for misinterpretation and I'm upset that you acuse me of responding to what I heard and not what you said, I am most definitely responding to what you said.

@freesia Okay, I've been following this blog since about the same time as you and have read most of Jack's blogs, enough to know what his public pov on the subject is, I'm not sure what you're implying I missed?

Look, Jack, you are going into great detail with a lot of these processes and that's great, but you are seriously touting these semi-bogus claims which include grounding and basing everything on diet. Sure maybe you eventually say that people who have already become leptin sensitive should maybe try HIIT, but that's bogus, this is information people should hear right away. It immediately will help the vast majority of your followers to improve mitochondrial function and it just really pisses me off that you have so much great information and then come to such seemingly inadequate conclusions.

How about if you want to increase mitochondrial function and live in a reduced state you start incorporating 15-25 second all out bursts of energy followed by a 1-2 minute rest repeated 5-6 times a few times a week? Your information is only as good as the results it gets people, body recomposition is what most people need for health body recomposition without sacrificing their overall health. If anyone seriously wants to improve their mitochondrial function by dramatic figures they should be doing activities that increase IIb fibres, such as a program possibly like Sprint 8 or something comparable.

The things you are recommending people do are a 6 on a scale from 1-10. If you start seriously looking into this and suggesting it to people that'll change to a 9/10. If you seriously care about helping people you'll incorporate some serious protocols instead of this hippy grounding crap.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-09T22:38:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-is-deadly-wrong-in-condemning-all-high-intensity-exercise.5820/post-75074>

> [LightForTheNight said:](/goto/post?id=75050)
>
> PS this was supposed to get posted in moster thread, not adrenal RX, appreciate if it could be moved.
>
> Mitochondrial redox state correlates with Ca2+ spark activity. Ca2+ sparks develop fastest in fibers poor in mitochondria and slowest in cells rich in mitochondria. The appearance of ca2+ sparks in permeabilized skeletal muscle cells depends on the fibre's oxidative strength and that misbalance between mitochondial ROS production and the fibre's ability to fight oxidative stress is likely to be responsible for unmasking ca2+ sparks in skinned preperations.
>
> Skeletal muscle depends on ATP supply to meet its energy demands. There are three major sources of ATP in muscle: creatine phosphate, anaerobix glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation. The relative contribution of each ATP source varies among muscle fibre types. Type 1 (slow-twitch, oxidative) and type IIa (fast-twitch, glycotic-oxidative) fibres are rich in mitochondria. They rely for their ATP production on oxidative phosphorylation. In contrast, type IIb (fast-twitch, glycolytic) fibres, are MITOCHONDRIA POOR and have very effective glycolytic ATP sythesis.
>
> What this means is that fast twitch musles correlate with Mitochondrial REDOX state. If you want to build a strong mitochondrial base FOR YOUR BODY, if you want health then working out type IIb fibres is one of the single best things you can do for yourself (period).
>
>
> During short intense exercise, glycogen in the muscle is used up (by IIb fibres).
> Sugar is then shuttled to these cells and all is well.
> However if glycogen stores are never depleted sugar gets shunted into fat instead, resulting in obesity.
>
> Depleting glycogen activates hormone sensitive lipase so that fats are mobilised from body stores and made available for energy supply (good for people trying to lose fat).
> If sugar in the blood stream following a meal cannot be taken up because glycogen stres are full, then this excess sugar sticks on to other things creating Advanced Glycation End products or AGE's , which accelerate the normal aging process. One such example is glycosylated haemoglobin which is measured to assess blood sugar control in diabetics.
>
> Good fast twitch fibres = good mitochondrial function = good cardiovascular fitness.
>
>
>
> Look, Jack has great ideas, and I've learned a ton about the ETC, mitochondria and circadian biology. And I think, no I KNOW he is right that it is at the ROOT of neolithic disease. But this bullshit he preaches about waiting 2 years to become leptin sensitive is crap. He is probably right that we need electron dense foods and most his advice is good-great BUT HIGH intensity exercise, for a VERY short duration is only going to result in MORE fast twitch IIb fibres, which is going to be positively correlated with mitochondrial REDOX state. This means keeping calcium where it needs to be and exceptional CARDIOVASCULAR health.
>
> Do NOT think that I'm advocating endurance training, or any crap like Cross Fit. I'm saying that very short duration HIGH intensity exercises are ESSENTIAL for sublime mitochondrial function and I don't give a sh\*t what Jack says, he is WRONG, he might be right about limiting carbs, but he is DEAD wrong about avoiding all high intensity very short duration exercises. I fully expect this to be deleted as I'm right and it contradicts Jack who seems to have Alec Baldwin's God Complex. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8g2dkDh4ov4](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8g2dkDh4ov4)

I think he just recommended no HIIT for ppl doing the reset and/or with adrenal problems. I think after that is sorted out he advocates for it once you have gotten to a certain point. I really think for myself based on my personal experiences it would NOT have been good for me to be doing ANY exercise in Nov. 2011. But now I'm ready again. (I'm pretty sure)

D-ribose. I caved and bought some (with money I got from gift card sale haha). I tried 10g w/ drink @ 2pm. My hands actually warmed up. I don't know if it works like this but I have noticed an increase in my appetite in about the hour and half. So I am reckoning that if my cells got some d-ribose and they liked it they are asking for more rather than 'hungry for food'. If that makes sense. I am making some tea and will put more in. I will keep experimenting.

Also when people talked about the taste and smell. +1 on the burnt marshmallow smell and +1 for it providing just the right amount of sweetness to a beverage. I think it will be my new 'sugar'. \*crosses fingers that I will see a continued benefit and not just a one day honeymoon\* lol

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-09T22:49:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-raise-money-too.5822/post-75076>

I did the Vermont Special Olympics Penguin Plunge today. Outside temps were 14degrees and the wind was howling from the remains of east coast storm.

I thought I would post a few pictures:

The group I went in with disappeared pretty quick...so I took a few laps around the water to the amusement of the fireman who were in survival suits. One of my sandals never made it to the dressing room so I had a sock on one foot which promptly floated off so that was another source of amusement for everyone! All in all a fine day and I raised almost 300. for the athletes. I was trying to go for a beach look in my sun hat and glasses, but the wind kind of ruined the effect!

I spoke to as many people as I could about CT and my pond dips. I wouldn't be surprised if the Newport local paper doesn't call. The Firefighters were aware of the practice too...so I did my bit to spread the word...and it was gratifying to show-off just a little. I'm not somebody who likes crowds or big groups, so this was a stretch for me, but well worth it.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-09T22:55:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-raise-money-too.5822/post-75078>

Awesome !!! SEahorse.. LOLing at the firefighters in the cold water survival suits... Yeah for stepping outside your comfort zone, rocking the CT And being a Starfish... you are a rockstar!

# Thread: Overweight toddler?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2013-02-10T04:59:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75174>

Hi guys,

I've not been around much. Life has been busy. I have a question about overweight toddlers. I have identical twin 20 month olds. They weaned completely about a month ago. In the last 2 months they've been gaining a lot of weight... especially one of them. My mom keeps stressing about how much he is gaining. She thinks he is starting to look fat and worries I'm feeding him too many "calorie dense food." He LOVES meat, especially fatty meat like the bacon from the pastured pig we bought.

They had always been on the very small side - not on the height or weight chart at all. They also had been having problems with high fevers every 4-6 weeks for about a year. When they had the fevers they wouldn't eat much. Hoping they are done with that now as it's been 2 months without fevers (yay!)

E, my bigger boy, is now 27 pounds and 31.5 inches, which I guess technically makes him overweight. D is 31.5 inches and 23 pounds. Honestly, I instinctively don't think there is much of a problem. I should probably be doing more seafood each week, but other than that I really think their diet is fine. He does eat a LOT though. Do you have to be worried about a 20 month old overeating? WOuld you be worried?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-10T17:00:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75217>

epigenetics.......and no, you and your mom wont like the implications.......but that is the answer. The kids need to just eat fat and protein until they are 6.......and you have a shot of helping them big time. Cut all their EMF time.......100% of it.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2013-02-10T23:30:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75286>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=75217)
>
> epigenetics.......and no, you and your mom wont like the implications.......but that is the answer. The kids need to just eat fat and protein until they are 6.......and you have a shot of helping them big time. Cut all their EMF time.......100% of it.

What is EMF time? (sorry, feeling out of the loop)
Fat and protein are basically what they eat, they do have veggies and occasionally fruit. I take it, I should cut those out (my littler guy loves veggies and fruit, the bigger one is the one that eats up the protein and fat). I think thats why my mom keeps fussing at me that I need to stop Elliot (the bigger twin) from eating so much fat/protein. I guess I stick pretty much with what I've been doing with them.

### Kiera2 — 2013-02-10T23:40:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75287>

ElectroMagnetic Frequency. In a nutshell, just turn off all the power. No TV, no computers, just no screens at all really. Turn off as much power as you can. Have nothing in their rooms. And ditch all your wifi stuff. Easier said than done, I know. But I'm sure that's what Jack means.

Oh, and don't let them use a cell phone, and replace your cordless phone for an old fashioned one.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2013-02-10T23:42:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75288>

No tv and computers is easy for them, they're only 20 months. We have to have wifi for work. I can ditch the CD player in their room. I used it as background noise for them, but don't think they need it to sleep anymore.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-11T01:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75317>

If you are using a baby monitor at all at 2.4 GHz you might just not like EMF 5...and a CD player wont make them sleep better.....it just makes them sleep less well and may make them gain weight too.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2013-02-11T04:48:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75356>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=75317)
>
> If you are using a baby monitor at all at 2.4 GHz you might just not like EMF 5...and a CD player wont make them sleep better.....it just makes them sleep less well and may make them gain weight too.

No baby monitor. Only time I have used them is summer when babies were down for a nap and I was outside with my older boys. We're not frolicking in the pool now because it's frozen .

I will ditch the CD player. It helps cover up the louder brothers. I guess I'll have to get rid of the older boys CD player too... and they've just stopped fussing about not having the nightlight. Oh well! They'll get over this too.

What about an infrared heater? Does that cause a problem too?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-11T05:09:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overweight-toddler.5825/post-75361>

yes.......infrared is an EMF......it must go.

# Thread: When you can't afford a rolex . . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2013-02-10T18:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75223>

I want to get DH a new watch for Valentine's Day. Obviously the EMF issue has me thinking it's not a good idea to stick a battery next to his skin 24/7, which is how often he wears his watch. So I'm looking for an automatic type, like a Rolex, but that is completely out of my budget!! Any recommendations?

### fitness@home — 2013-02-10T19:09:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75232>

That's a good idea, the windup watch. I really love the look of a pocket watch on a chain but don't see it often in today's society.

Have been thinking itbis time to go back to a Big Ben windup alarm clock. Had one in high school (a long time ago).

### Lyndra — 2013-02-10T19:25:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75237>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=75232)
>
> Have been thinking itbis time to go back to a Big Ben windup alarm clock. Had one in high school (a long time ago).

I used to have several of those clocks but of course got rid of them years ago (like the corded phones - I just hit a bunch of thrift shops to buy a couple). Same thing with cookware - I got rid of anything teflon and am going back to old school Corning ware (the old stuff, made to withstand high temperature variations) and old Pyrex (same thing, new stuff is sensitive to thermal shock).

### prAna303 — 2013-02-10T19:39:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75240>

I have an Quiksilver surfwatch that is powered by motion, no batteries... Check brands that make surfinggears because they often do not use batteries because of pollution in nature  Way cheaper than Rolex.

### Lyndra — 2013-02-10T20:07:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75244>

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-10T20:09:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75246>

Seiko kinetic.........cheap and high quality. I have two. I collect watches........

### BJK77 — 2013-02-10T23:00:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75278>

Thanks for all the suggestions. I had already been looking at a Seiko kinetic on Amazon, so I think I'll pull the trigger now.

### Optimalbound — 2013-02-11T00:01:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75290>

The Seiko Kinetic is still an electronic quartz watch, is it not? I can't quite see how replacing the battery with a capacitor plus what is essentially a dynamo is going to make much difference in terms of EMF.

If my budget were limited, I'd rather go with something like Steinhart if I wanted a non-EMF, self-winding mechanical watch of decent quality.

### caroline — 2013-02-11T00:29:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75296>

In Sydney we have a shop that sells second hand watches - some very high quality and they do have windups and not many people want them these days so prices are good

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-11T01:58:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75318>

Or a used rolex.........I have several of them.

### caroline — 2013-02-11T02:04:51+0200

yes - I have my husband's that I bought him many years ago for an anniversary present - but it costs a fortune to have fixed - but I love it.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-11T04:08:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-cant-afford-a-rolex.5826/post-75348>

I'm using my dad's Caravelle, circa 1960ish, wind up watch. It keeps time beautifully and has a big black face that I can see and read easily. Sometimes you don't have to spend much money. I don't think this thing would be worth more than $2 at a second hand store but it is wonderful for me now! I knew I had saved it for some reason.

### Hope — 2013-02-11T17:28:25+0200

# Thread: Phluffing the Girls
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2013-02-10T19:29:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-75238>

I recently switch to Thermography instead of mammograms. The naturopath's office where I had this done also has a store area and stocks this product:

[http://www.healthygirlsbreastoil.com/healthy-girlds-breast-oil/](http://www.healthygirlsbreastoil.com/healthy-girlds-breast-oil/)

This oil contains jojoba oil and essential oils like citrus, lavender, geranium, frankincense, nerolina,, sweet marjoram, and rose absolute.

My right breast was completely normal, but the left showed areas that might become issues. Ironically, this is the breast that a benign lump was excised from when I was about 19-20 years old. The areas show as blue in color (sluggish lymph?).

The initial thermogram is the baseline and I go back in three months for another. After that it is annually. For my own N=1 I am Phluffing my girls daily. Sometimes my husband assists (he is so thoughtful like that).

You know, I can work in some oxytocin therapy too by tweaking my nipples. Two birds with one stone.

Will post and let you know if there is any discernible difference in the follow up thermogram.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-10T20:10:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-75247>

smart lady...........

### Thor — 2013-02-10T20:16:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-75252>

.
Face Palm  LoL

Here's a link to a great article about this very subject. Can you believe it - nearly 3 out 4 positive mammograms weren't positive at all! Yikes.... In addition, the pressure placed on your "girls" during the exam - causes any would be tumor to only spread deeper into the breast tissue.

[http://www.naturalnews.com/033458\_mammography\_dangers.html](http://www.naturalnews.com/033458_mammography_dangers.html)

### caroline — 2013-02-11T02:42:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-75326>

The things you learn on this forum! I love that - going to try and find some......thanks for sharing.... you have the best husband!

### fitness@home — 2013-04-19T22:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-84496>

Had the second round of thermography (for my baseline) in April. I can note improvement on the right side (less dark blue). The left side shows some improvement, but there is still a lot of darker blue in the picture.

I plan on extending my N=1 to January 2014; when I will go back for my annual test.

### Shijin13 — 2013-04-19T23:21:24+0300

Great success! I will be skipping mamography and getting a thermography done when its time...

### caroline — 2013-04-20T01:19:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-84502>

Dawn - How is the breast Pluffing going? has it made a difference? other than the obvious .........
Good news on the thermography

### fitness@home — 2013-04-20T04:53:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-84531>

It is part of my morning routine now. The oil blend is very light and absorbs quickly. Bonus that it smells good too. The skin on my chest is very healthy and soft.

I can see some visual proof that it is in fact making a difference. My goal is to stimulate the lymph movement in the left breast more. Obviously still have issues there and the tissue is considered "dense".

When my methylation and detox pathways are functioning better, I'd like to give the Iodine protocol a go... Afraid to introduce it right now. Gut tells me it's better to wait on this.

### fitness@home — 2014-04-22T10:52:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-122957>

This year I opted for the Full Body scan. I had full reimbursement from my FSA.

Results were pretty spot on with my history. Although I've never had an official diagnosis of myofascial dysfunction, the description fits the type of pain I feel. I am posting the report so others can see the depth of information obtained from it. Ladies, I highly recommend switching to thermography for at least your annual breast exams.

Not posting the thermography pictures...there are _seven_ pages worth of them in the full body scan.

Password is health1

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-22T13:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-122973>

The previous surgery will change the results but the key is the trend.......and I think the massage is a key point on many levels and the use of oil is fine. I think coconut oil is OK for this too if you want to limit cost.

### fitness@home — 2014-04-22T20:06:38+0300

Very interesting. Love the cat scan!

Recently found an anti-aging doc who does thermography and will make an appt soon. I've long wondered why this is pretty much only available through alternative docs.

### fitness@home — 2014-09-30T21:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phluffing-the-girls.5827/post-142297>

**From Tensegrity #5: Your Magnetic Sense**:

Just looked at my full-body scan results again from this perspective ^^. Wow...

Time to up my game by increasing CT and DHA.

# Thread: Fight over smart meters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2013-02-10T20:01:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/post-75242>

[http://www.theblaze.com/stories/2013/02/09/handicapped-woman-refuses-smart-meter-has-power-cut-in-the-dead-of-winter/](http://www.theblaze.com/stories/2013/02/09/handicapped-woman-refuses-smart-meter-has-power-cut-in-the-dead-of-winter/)

I'm afraid we're all going to have to be prepared for one hell of a fight over smart meters. It seems like there's a new story with a similar theme every week anymore . . .

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-10T20:08:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/post-75245>

Staying cold will keep her alive..........she does not know it.......but I hope you all do.

### Lyndra — 2013-02-10T20:14:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/post-75251>

Shocking story but the comments are really something else. A lot of people think that it's her fault (being without electricity) because she was not willing to go along with what the monopoly power company dictated (e.g. she should have found an alternative power source). The commenters have no concerns whatsoever about the effects of the smart meters. If the power company says it's good and that it will save money, then full speed ahead. When will we learn that there is always a price that comes with "progress?"

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-10T20:25:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/post-75254>

This is why 90% of the population will die after you all read the implications of EMF 5. No, I am not joking either.

### Inger — 2013-02-10T20:45:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fight-over-smart-meters.5829/post-75257>

I have not gotten rid of the smart meter yet either. 3 phone calls where they always try to explain why it is safe and the sending me papers about it. I am not giving up, tuesday they will call me again and I know what I am going to say. I am just irritated that it takes this long. Grrr... it is more struggle than I thought in the beginning...
But they have sent me papers about a special filter too. I am going to demand removing the meter and then I will install the filter too. They said it is no help to remove our meter as all the neighbours got one installed too at the same time! I do not care one bit. I will demand ours beeing removed and then buy the filter to get rid of the neighbours EMF posion too..lol

# Thread: Another Win - Research Shows What You Eat Determines Your Sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-win-research-shows-what-you-eat-determines-your-sleep.5831/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2013-02-10T20:23:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-win-research-shows-what-you-eat-determines-your-sleep.5831/post-75253>

Well almost a win - the researchers were on the right path but couldn't connect the dots. Somebody should direct them to the forum & blog postings  Interesting to see an entire research team devoted to nothing but Circadian Neurobiology. It would be interesting to see if they can grasp the latest posting on here & understand it in the context of healing. The team of researchers just need a wee bit of nudge to get in the right direction.

**Diet Affects Sleep Patterns, Study Finds**

Posted: 02/07/2013 8:15 am EST

It's no secret: We are what we eat. And turns out, we might sleep how we eat, too.

A new study from the University of Pennsylvania's Perelman School of Medicine shows an association between what we eat and how we sleep. The research is published in the journal Appetite.

"In general, we know that those who report between \[seven to eight\] hours of sleep each night are most likely to experience better overall health and well being, so we simply asked the question, 'Are there differences in the diet of those who report shorter sleep, longer sleep, or standard sleep patterns?'" study researcher Michael A. Grandner, Ph.D., of the Center for Sleep and Circadian Neurobiology at the university, said in a statement.

Researchers examined the daily calories and foods consumed -- down to a glass of water -- by people who were part of the 2007-2009 National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey. They also gathered information on the amount of time the study participants slept, putting them into four categories: "very short" sleepers, who slept fewer than five hours a night; "short" sleepers, who slept five to six hours a night; "standard" sleepers, who slept seven to eight hours a night; and "long" sleepers, who slept nine or more hours a night.

And researchers did, in fact, find an association between the number of calories consumed and how long the study participants slept. Those who consumed the most were more likely to be "short" sleepers. Interestingly enough, "normal" sleepers were the next type to consume a lot of calories, followed by "very short" sleepers and then "long" sleepers, researchers found.

The researchers also identified different associations between sleep time and the types of nutrients the participants ate. For example, very short sleepers consumed less tap water, total carbohydrates and a compound found in red and orange foods, compared with the other kinds of sleepers. Meanwhile, the long sleepers consumed less of a compound found in tea and chocolate, as well as the nutrient choline (in eggs and some meats) than other kinds of sleepers, but more alcohol.

Overall, researchers noted that the very short, short and long sleepers consumed a less varietal diet than those who were considered normal sleepers. The question is now whether changing eating habits can actually affect sleep, as the study only showed an association.

Animals that produced increased levels of prolactin and dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) survived the period of mass extinctions at the end of the Cretaceous period. This is the K-T event. The results after K-T was massive global cooling for decades. What else would you know about prolactin secretion? In cold it actually increase electron current flow in animals. This is a way they used to survive the event. It also helps explain what is mentioned in EMF-2 about life increasing electron flow by any means necessary to survive. Here is just another way life found a way.

Even today Prolactin has massive pituitary control over dopamine. DHEA increases thermogenesis directly using UCP's 1-3 and its supported existence through the extended episodes of cold and dark that persisted after the K-T event was naturally selected for in eutherian mammals. Further increases in DHEA and prolactin produced continual physiological and anatomical changes which eventually produced by all of the characteristics of mammals as evolutionary time has increased from the K-T event. Organisms that produce increased DHEA have an advantage during prolonged cold and darkness; they are warmer......and this means they sleep longer make more ATP, and burn calories well.
So ladies here is why a higher DHEA is better and is associated with young folks and not older people.

# Thread: All things Electrical/Magnetic/Fields
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 98
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2013-02-11T16:49:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-75388>

Over the years I have often wished I had never done that Electrical Engineering degree. I thought more biology background would serve me way better at figuring out how to fix myself, and issues my kids have. Over the past year, I have developped a bit of biology understanding (high, not low level), and can better read the blogs now. In the beginning they were a foreign language! But now, now we're moving into an area where I am at least somewhat literate, and I live with someone who is extremely literate! What a relief! But, as a result I have more questions, and some of my understandings of JK's writing don't mix well with my EE knowledge. I'm hoping to discuss and get more clarification.

1\. Schumann Resonance
From what I can understand, the cavity between the ground and ionisphere acts as a waveguide. Lightning emits all kinds of different frequency EM waves, but most are filtered out by the ionisphere. The frequencies that are able to be reflected back towards the earth are called the Schumann resonant frequencies. So, Schumann frequencies are a E-M wave that are able to propagate in the the waveguide formed by the Earth's cavity between the ionisphere and the ground. This would not be unique to earth (I'd bet other planets can have a similar waveguide phenomenon, and then all they need is a signal source (lightning) to get a resonant frequency - which would probably be different from Earth's). It seems JK says our brains are tuned to listen for this frequency, and it's used by our brains to help tell time. I can totally see how artificial EMFs might interfere with the Schumann wave.

2\. Earth's DC magnetic field
This is where my understanding falls apart. I can't see how the DC field of Earth has anything to do with our ability to plug into the Earth, and ground effectively. A DC magnetic field does not cause, or impede current flow. Right? Also, why does he keep talking about the DC field and Schumann as if they are inter-related. Are they? I can't see it. I'd think a planet without a DC magnetic field could set up a standing wave (described above) just fine. So, the heart of my question is, **How does the Earth's DC magnetic field affect my body (brain/SCN)?** I've read and re-read, and looked at other sources and it's not coming together for me.

I have more questions, but I'll start here.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-75404>

Thanks BKW! Your comments and questions about EMF are helping this non-scientific all-business woman understand these concepts much better. You put things in a way that I can understand and thus comprehend the answer. Right now I currently am still playing catch up with the EMF understanding but wanted you to know you really have helped.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-11T19:53:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-75413>

I dont think you have a real clue how the ionosphere really works.............and the magnetic field of the Earth. All this has been very worked out by thousands of physicists.......you need to read about quantum computing in cells. This is no longer speculation.
when the
Human brain waves are not paced by the thalamus, allow the brain's field to be entrained by external electric and magnetic rhythms,
either natural or man-made.

What is the source of natural electric and magnetic rhythms? The magnetic field of the earth, called the geomagnetic field,
causes the compass needle to Point toward the North Pole. However, if you look carefully at a compass needle with a
microscope, you will see that the needle is rarely still - it dances back and forth in a variety of rhythms. Some of these
rhythms are diurnal (24 hour), some are much slower, and others are quite fast (in the ELF range). The last are called
geomagnetic micropulsations. They are caused by a unique geophysical mechanism known as the Schumann resonance.

In the 1950s, a German atmospheric physicist, W. 0. Schumann, suggested that the space between the surface of the earth and
the ionosphere should act as a resonant cavity, somewhat like the chamber in a musical instrument (Schumann 1952). Pressing
the keys on a wind instrument changes the size of the cavity and therefore changes the frequency of the standing waves within
that cavity.

In a musical instrument, tones are generated when the musician blows over an orifice or reed, Energy for the Schumann resonance
is provided by lightning. While you may be experiencing calm weather where you are now, there are, on average, about 200
lightning strikes taking place each second, scattered about the planet. To use the physics terminology, lightning pumps energy
into the earth-ionosphere cavity, and causes it to vibrate or resonate at frequencies in the ELF range.

In the 1960s, Schumann's theory was confirmed (Galejs 1972, Balser & Wagner 1960). Lightning creates electromagnetic
standing waves that travel around the globe. As electromagnetic waves, the Schumann resonance can be detected either as electric
or magnetic micropulsations. The waves are reflected from the ionosphere, back to the earth, back to the ionosphere, etc.
This 'skip' phenomenon has been widely studied, because it is the basis for long distance radio communication.
Radio signals of certain frequencies can travel great distances because they are repeatedly reflected by the ionosphere and the
earth's surface.

The average frequency of the Schumann resonance is about 7-10 Hz- But when the ionosphere gets higher, the cavity gets larger and the resonant frequency drops. Rhythms of terrestrial and extraterrestrial origin alter the height and other properties of
the ionosphere, and thereby alter the Schumann frequency in the range of 1-40 Hz. There are times when solar activity leads to
magnetic storms that disrupt the ionosphere, and Schumann resonances cease.

To summarize, the Schumann resonance is created by terrestrial activities, and is modified or modulated by extraterrestrial
activities. In radio terminology, the signals are frequency modulated (FM).

Actually I haven't seen a benefit yet, but my cat has stopped avoiding my touch at times. It was strange, but while being very friendly, sometimes she would put her back into interesting contortions to avoid being petted. She also likes to go outside in the cold and damp at night. I'm going to stop grounding through an outlet and use an inexpensive grounding stake I just bought, for night use.

### EEDude — 2013-02-12T18:10:07+0200

> [freshveggies said:](/goto/post?id=75533)
>
> I don't always care about studies or want to wait for them. By the time they come around or are bought and paid for by the party trying to prove they are safe, years have gone by.
>
> common sense should prevail. What we can't see or feel can hurt us.
>
> It is hard to get good studies, because there seems to be cover-ups.
>

Please look at this: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy\_medicine](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_medicine)

> There are many, primarily psychological, explanations for positive outcomes after energy therapy such as the placebo effect or cognitive dissonance. A 2009 review found that the "small successes" reported for two therapies collectively marketed as "energy psychology" (Emotional Freedom Techniques and Tapas Acupressure Technique) "are potentially attributable to well-known cognitive and behavioral techniques that are included with the energy manipulation." The report concluded that "Psychologists and researchers should be wary of using such techniques, and make efforts to inform the public about the ill effects of therapies that advertise miraculous claims."\[21\]
>
> There are primarily two explanations for anecdotes of cures or improvements, relieving any need to appeal to the supernatural.\[43\] The first is post hoc ergo propter hoc, meaning that a genuine improvement or spontaneous remission may have been experienced coincidental with but independent from anything the healer or patient did or said. These patients would have improved just as well even had they done nothing. The second is the placebo effect, through which a person may experience genuine pain relief and other symptomatic alleviation. In this case, the patient genuinely has been helped by the healer, not through any mysterious or numinous function, but by the power of their own belief that they would be healed.\[44\]\[45\] In both cases the patient may experience a real reduction in symptoms, though in neither case has anything miraculous or inexplicable occurred. Both cases, however, are strictly limited to the body's natural abilities.
>
> Positive findings from research studies may also be explained by such psychological mechanisms, or as a result of experimenter bias, "methodological flaws"\[21\] or publication bias, and positive reviews of the scientific literature may show selection bias, in that they omit key studies that do not agree with the author's position.\[21\]\[22\] All of these factors must be considered when evaluating claims.

From Wikipedia on Energy Medicine:

"There are many, primarily psychological, explanations for positive outcomes after energy therapy such as the placebo effect or cognitive dissonance. A 2009 review found that the "small successes" reported for two therapies collectively marketed as "energy psychology" (Emotional Freedom Techniques and Tapas Acupressure Technique) "are potentially attributable to well-known cognitive and behavioral techniques that are included with the energy manipulation." The report concluded that "Psychologists and researchers should be wary of using such techniques, and make efforts to inform the public about the ill effects of therapies that advertise miraculous claims."\[21\]

There are primarily two explanations for anecdotes of cures or improvements, relieving any need to appeal to the supernatural.\[43\] The first is post hoc ergo propter hoc, meaning that a genuine improvement or spontaneous remission may have been experienced coincidental with but independent from anything the healer or patient did or said. These patients would have improved just as well even had they done nothing. The second is the placebo effect, through which a person may experience genuine pain relief and other symptomatic alleviation. In this case, the patient genuinely has been helped by the healer, not through any mysterious or numinous function, but by the power of their own belief that they would be healed.\[44\]\[45\] In both cases the patient may experience a real reduction in symptoms, though in neither case has anything miraculous or inexplicable occurred. Both cases, however, are strictly limited to the body's natural abilities.

Positive findings from research studies may also be explained by such psychological mechanisms, or as a result of experimenter bias, "methodological flaws"\[21\] or publication bias, and positive reviews of the scientific literature may show selection bias, in that they omit key studies that do not agree with the author's position.\[21\]\[22\] All of these factors must be considered when evaluating claims."

But now I'm deciding to stick with chronic situation A in lieu of what my current experimentation has shown me.

Also regarding hormones I wonder if there's personal variances for what a body 'prefers'. So even if on a test they might come up to some good levels and even good ratios that it might not have any desired affect on that individual and not help them at all. But in a case where ALL hormones might be on the lower side, that that person actually experiences (paradoxically) better health, or at least a feeling of well being.

I know in the past (or anytime) when my hormones are too high (only evidenced by sx and not a test) that I feel WORSE than when I'm in a situation where it's evident that I have lowered hormones across the board. (Again only by sx). Of course there's little one month exceptions to that rule on either side, but I think overall it's better for me to have NO TOTM than to have one/have it regular.

There's a few things I've learned which help a bit (as simple as Mg for example) which I think I can take away with me. But I think I decide from now to not enter the 'hormones game' and having to worry about dosings, cost, sourcing, dr.s' etc.

I need to get myself back to the state in September. But I think this might be an easier thing to accomplish if I don't have to constantly worry about my hormones. Just keep things simple.

Then again, I could be wrong about the whole thing. lol

### kathylu — 2013-02-13T02:58:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-75644>

Ok, I read the above article. Anecdotal reports are certainly interesting and can be useful. However, I certainly wouldn't infer much of any meaning from this article. Sample size too small, inadequate experimental design (i.e. none), no consistency in measured parameters, no statistical analysis worth mentioning, not single or double blind...jeez, I could go on and on but I have to put my child to bed (on to more important things).

However, it would certainly be easy to conduct your own N=1 experiment with the same level of accuracy they have demonstrated. Let us know how it goes. Oh, by the way...type 3 diabetes has already been taken (Alzheimer's disease). Maybe they couldn't count to 4?

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-13T18:13:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-75706>

There have been a number of posts regarding smart meters. Upon doing some research, I have found there are a variety of smart meter brands and models. They vary in wireless signal , and frequency of signal transmission. This means all smart meters are not equal. People who are concerned about smart meters may consider researching these variables for the model being used in their locale.

I was curious about how the EMF from smart meters compared to other wireless devices. Page 13 of the document on the link below gives an idea. It appears that cell phones, cordless phones, wi-fi routers cell phone base stations and wireless baby monitors are all more dangerous since their EMF is emitted continuously. (vs. smart meters emitting intermittently)

Source: [http://hbelc.org/pdf/Resources/SmartMeter\_Sierck.pdf](http://hbelc.org/pdf/Resources/SmartMeter_Sierck.pdf) .... There are many variables to consider. This article provides an excellent overview of smart meters.

Electric and magnetic fields extend into the air surrounding where they're created (in this case the motor of the treadmill). You are exposed to stronger levels the closer you are to the source of the field (in this case the motor of the treadmill).

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-20T02:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-76595>

> [diane said:](/goto/post?id=76559)
>
> Great questions Lauren. I was also wondering if my rubber cell phone case is at least helping to shield EMF a bit.

The rubber is an insulator from direct current (electricity doesn't easily go through it, and therefore our shoes insulate us from the ground). However, the cell phone communicates by creating an RF signal (and E-M wave at a particular frequency - in the GHz range), that propagates readily through the air. In this case, to properly protect yourself you need a faraday cage (a metal box surrounding the device). However, then your cell phone would no longer work because it wouldn't be able to communicate outside of the metal box.

The best way to deal with dirty electricity is to have an isolated ground installed by an electrican. The isolated ground is an earth ground rod with a wire that runs into the breaker box for the house. The ground wire is then connected to an isolated ground bar. Then you can have the ground for the breakers to the outlets in the bedrooms tied into the isolated ground bar. Any dirty electricity generated by your desk/treadmill, refig, etc will be on a separate ground that the one that you connect your grounding strap into. Depending on how your house is wired, you may have to run a separate power line to your desk/treadmill.

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-20T16:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-76654>

> [vkiernan said:](/goto/post?id=76645)
>
> BKW, I have a wireless internet access point right outside my office door on the ceiling. My wall and door just happen to be metal but the window in the door is some sort of frosted glass. One other note, my wall does not go to the ceiling. I can only see the access point when my door is open. Does the metal door provide me with any protection to that access point? This internet is for customer/sales use when they come visit us. It is not our primary source of internet. That is by fiber. Thanks for your thoughts.

\*\*disclaimer\*\* I can only give my opinion which is based only on a basic understanding - I am NO EXPERT\*\*

The wall would offer \*some\* protection (in theory), but in reality I wouldn't expect it to do much for you, given the close proximity to the antennae, and the fact that the metal wall doesn't reach the ceiling. Imagine E-M waves being released in a growing spherical shape from their point of origin. The wave is fully reflected off a metal (conductive surface), much like a rolling ball "reflects" off a wall as it hits it. A perfect conductor is much like the wall that stops and redreicts the ball in the other direction. Space is sort-of the opposite - a medium through which waves readily propagate from their original source, in a spherical pattern. Other materials do some of each - some reflection, some propagation, and some loss, as the wave hits particles and loses energy to them. So your signal gets weaker and weaker as it gets further from the source, because the sphere is ever growing, and also due to losses along the way.

This is precisely where I don't understand Dr. Kruse saying the EMFs last forever. To me it's like saying a ball rolling is always going to be rolling, and we need to be careful about how many balls we get started rolling on this earth, because once they start, they go on forever and that would become an unstoppable force of destruction. There are several ways that those balls stop rolling (walls, hills, etc), and so we don't worry about them in that way. With the EMF issue, I can definitely see there is cause for concern about those signals on our biology and intense study/scrutiny/caution is warranted, but I don't see how they continue forever once created. I'm pretty open minded to learning more, and hearing more of his argument (I welcome it, because I am no expert at all, and, well, finally I can apply my very rusty BA Sc knowledge lol). I'm not yet convinced that the Titanic is sinking, so to speak...

I also saw something yesterday on the nasa site about Schumann resonance being picked up above the ionisphere. In my mind, this is no surprise, because the ionisphere is not a perfect conductor (sheet of metal), and so some of the signal passes through, and some (most) is reflected back to the earth.

I really don't know... Dirty electricity is several other harmonics on the line other than the regular 60Hz sine wave signal, like you said. The harmonics are generated by the load (appliance) itself, as it manipulates the electricity into a different signal shape (different signal shapes are really the addition of several (clean)sine wave signals at different frequencies.). I would expect something that uses a lot of current (like your treadmill motor) would be far more problematic in this regard since the amplitudes would be bigger. A filter would help clean up the harmonics, but you would still be standing in the midst of field generated by the motor. Being tied to a clean ground would help, I would think... I'm afraid I can't offer much more here! I'm not an electrician, and certainly never learned to wire a house (I was always a little disappointed that we didn't learn too much of the practical, hands-on side).

But as the E-M wave hits a particle (air, tree, whatever), it transfers some of it's energy to that particle - a little bit of the energy from the wave is transformed into movement of the particle, or excitation of it's electrons. So the wave loses some of its strength, and the particle gains. The atmosphere is not a perfect waveguide, because it's full of obstructions like this particle, and the ionisphere is not an absolute perfect conductor, so wave reflection is not complete (more loss here). There is loss, and the signal, over distance becomes weaker and weaker until it's into the noise floor and no longer an organised wave. That's how I see it at the moment... I know you are elluding to something else that negates this explanation, and I'm not pretending to know much about quantum field theory, but I'm trying to tell you that this is the precise place where I no longer understand your argument.

### Holly — 2013-02-22T16:13:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-electrical-magnetic-fields.5847/post-77026>

This is off course, but still interesting:

Magnetic Collar Slows GERD

By John Gever, Senior Editor, MedPage Today

Published: February 20, 2013

Reviewed by Zalman S. Agus, MD; Emeritus Professor, Perelman School of
Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania and Dorothy Caputo, MA, BSN, RN,
Nurse Planner

Action Points

This prospective study evaluated the safety and effectiveness of a new
magnetic device to augment the lower esophageal sphincter in a single-group
of patients with gastroesophageal reflux disease.

The study found that the use of the magnetic device was associated with
decreased exposure to esophageal acid and improvement of reflux symptoms.

A string of magnetic beads placed around the esophageal sphincter helped
most patients reduce acid reflux and cut back on medications for the
condition, results of a small, uncontrolled trial indicated.

With 100 patients having the device implanted via laparoscopic surgery, 64
achieved the primary endpoint for treatment success, defined as either
normalized acid exposure in the esophagus or a reduction in acid exposure of
at least 50% after 1 year, according to Robert A. Ganz, MD, of Minnesota
Gastroenterology in Plymouth, Minn., and colleagues.

More than 90 patients met key secondary endpoints, including a reduction of
at least 50% in the use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), as well as
improvement of at least 50% in quality-of-life scores relative to baseline
while not taking PPIs.

“Studies with larger samples and longer-term follow-up are needed to
confirm these early results and assess longer-term safety,” Ganz and
colleagues wrote in the Feb. 21 issue of the New England Journal of
Medicine.

The device was approved by the FDA in March 2012. It consists of small,
high-strength, titanium-encased magnets wired into an expandable ring.
Placed around the esophageal sphincter, it increases resistance to the
abnormal opening that allows stomach fluids to invade the esophagus, while
still allowing food to pass into the stomach.

Patients in the trial were divided about equally between men and women.
Median age was 53 (range 20 to 75) with a median duration of reflux symptoms
of 10 years (range 1 to 40). Patients had been taking PPIs for a median of 5
years and as long as 20.

Inclusion and exclusion criteria in the trial were such that patients had
relatively uncomplicated and moderate reflux disease. They needed to show
some responsiveness to PPIs and could not have large hiatal hernias,
Barrett’s esophagus, frequent dysphagia, or severe esophagitis, for
example.

During baseline pH monitoring lasting a median of 45 hours, the median
percentage of time with pH values less than 4 was 10.9% (range 4.8% to
25.4%). Patients’ median DeMeester score, a composite measure reflecting
several reflux-related factors, was 36.6 at baseline; a score of 14.7 or
more indicates substantial reflux.

After device implantation, patients were followed for 3 years. Ganz and
colleagues indicated that follow-up is planned to continue to the 5-year
mark. All 100 patients were included in efficacy and safety analyses at year
1\. Follow-ups at years 2 and 3 were analyzed on a per-protocol basis, with
data on 90 and 85 patients, respectively.

The researchers had set a response rate of 60% for the primary efficacy
endpoint as their target for trial success. It was met, with 64 of their 100
patients achieving the endpoint.

Results for the two secondary endpoints were as follows:

Reduction of 50% or more from baseline in PPI usage: 93% of patients

Increase of 50% or more from off-PPI baseline in quality of life scores:
92% of patients

From 13% to 14% of patients were using PPIs at the 1-, 2-, and 3-year
follow-ups, Ganz and colleagues reported, compared with 100% at baseline.

Symptoms of regurgitation were reported by about 90% at baseline, including
10% who rated them severe. At all subsequent follow-ups, symptoms were still
present in roughly 20%, and those were only rarely severe.

Median quality-of-life score without PPIs at baseline was 27 on a 50-point
scale; while using PPIs it was 11. The median score 3 years after receiving
the device was 2 (P

[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?5847-All-things-Electrical-Magnetic-Fields](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?5847-All-things-Electrical-Magnetic-Fields)

I think Big Knitwit asked some good questions. We are all learning here and she is clearly trying to understand what is happening. She was not rude, she is open about her queries, and at least one poster had time to say her questions were good ones. I agree.

Then I see Jack's responses. First he tells her she has 'no clue' how it works. Didn't she already say that she didn't understand? But no clues are offered. She is told to read more. Read more what? She's clearly reading the blogs and the cites therein.

Then, to make me even more confused, is a response from Jack that seems to be really harsh and not really about what BKW was posting. And then the thread is closed. I realise this is Jack's forum, not mine or BKW's, but do we really have to close general threads? And, if so, why?

I have been a long-time reader and follower here. But I don't get this. There is a steep learning curve and much of this information is controversial. Questions will arise, to be sure. Isn't that OK? Isn't that what we claim to all be about?

I value Jack's work and have personally benefited greatly. I simultaneously value openness and I'm concerned about what I see unfolding. I do not want to be a part of a community where open, respectful questioning is not allowed. Can someone explain what's going on? What am I missing? (And please don't say dopamine.)

# Thread: Mitochondrial Efficiency??

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 31

### LightForTheNight — 2013-02-12T19:43:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-efficiency.5862/post-75567>

How do you measure it?

Mitochondrial efficiency should be measured by an individual mitochondria's ability to perform. Powerful mitochondria would seem beneficial here. Mitochondria use 90% of the oxygen used in your body to make atp. VO2 max measures the body's uptake of oxygen, which TF measures the cumulative oxidative capacity of the body (cumulation of all mitochondria). You may not see the flaw here but let me tell you.

Slow twitch muscles are waaaaaay denser in mitochondria than fast/superfast twitch muscles. SO, you can have a very high vo2 max, and have inefficienct (but a lot of inefficient) mitochondria and have a higher vo2 max than someone with MUCH better mitochondrial function. To illustrate my point let me point out that some of the people with highest vo2 max in the world ever recorded are endurance athletes.

So what I'm saying then is how the hell do we measure mitochondrial efficiency and TF have a gauge on whether what we are doing is having a positive or negative effect? I propose measuring cytochrome c, but that's only because that's like the only thing I know is associated directly with energy production inside the mitochondria.

Would it make more sense to measure some other compound inside cells or what? How to measure leakiness, how to measure efficiency, how to measure energy production of the mitochondria?

Summary:
Vo2 max is a relatively BS indicator of mitochondrial function, is cytochrome C a good measure? If not what would be?

# Thread: Close to suicide - adrenal problems ruining my life
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lowbrainpower — 2013-02-12T23:31:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-75606>

I've talked with numerous doctors, gurus, chinese herbalists, you name it. Nothing works. Labs are always fine, vitals are fine, and told 'it's all in your head'. As if I like being a miserable, exhausted, 25 year old male with no libido and decreasing intelligence isn't bad enough, now they're making me out to be the bad guy when I'm really the victim.

I've come across this site because it seems as if Dr. Kruse has his science to back up the adrenal fatigue Rx. After reading his blog I have since bought every supplement on his list and have been following it for about two days now. I understand that this will take some time and am willing to stick this out - this is my last chance as I am unable to work and have nearly emptied my bank account while looking for answers.

I have two questions:
1) Why does my brain fog (fuzzy thinking) get 10x worse after ejaculation?
2) Is brain fog a normal part of adrenal fatigue? I can't remember what I do all day, the world around me is a blur, I have no sense of time and/or space, no sense of self, and can't remember what I read. I used to be highly intelligence and now I feel as if my once highly sharp brain is being destroyed by some sort of unknown entity.

I can deal with the suffering so long as I know it's something that can be treated. I just want to make sure this cognitive dysfunction is an actual part of adrenal fatigue, since physically I seem to be much better off (still exhausted, but not bed bound 100%). It's my brain that I'm worried about: I'm operating at 5% and can't stand this any longer.

I apologize if this offended anyone - I'm not trying to optimize my life but just get back to at least somewhat healthy and do not have anywhere else to turn.

Thank you.

\-Lowbrainpower

\*\*Edit: I should also mention that my symptoms get MUCH WORSE after eating/drinking anything (low-carb/high-carb, low-fat/high-fat, etc.). It feels like I'm being poisoned. Food allergies are negative. Water fasting for 48 hours clears all symptoms. Could this really be adrenal fatigue or something else? I used to never have problems with food and now I feel like I'm on Vicodin 24/7).

### nonchalant — 2013-02-13T00:01:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-75612>

Hi Brainpower, welcome!
Yes, brain fog is beatable! I think you have come to the right place.
I have come back from a fairly low level of function. I am no longer forgetting things that I know I should remember. However, some things that I did a year or so ago are still fuzzy/missing. I don't think I stored the memories right at the time.

I feel like I have taken years off of my age now. It's exciting.

### lowbrainpower — 2013-02-13T01:35:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-75629>

Thank you all for your help thus far.

I have gotten saliva cortisol levels but never ASI, DHEA, or melatonin. I have been having trouble with doctors and their inadequate diagnosis and care methods, not to mention the fact that I have hardly any strength to get dressed in the morning, much less fight for myself against those in charge of my health (who are also very arrogant).

I have recently resorted to stimulants (Adderal, Dexedrine, Ritalin, large amounts of coffee, etc.) to give me the energy to leave the house, but unfortunately this is a lost cause. They have now stopped working and I am left with nothing to aid me in my fight. It seems like one sick joke God would be playing on me, if I still believed in him.

The funny thing is I have never done illegal drugs, never smoked, never drank, and never abused anything prior to all this happening. Good intentions seem to have a punishing effect on me. The only thing I can think of that started this all was a flu-like illness I contracted when I was 14 and slowly recovered from over the course of a month, Accutane use for acne when I was 18, or perhaps 2 years of chronic stress endured while in the Army. I believe the Army had something to do with it but I also believe the stress would have been better handled if I wasn't sick to begin with, which I believe started when I was still in high school/middle school and from the previous two theories mentioned.

Sorry to drone on. I keep getting tortured with these moments of clarity where I wake up for 5 minutes once every couple of months and then sink back into the fog. It's like the light at the end of the tunnel of normalcy that I'm fighting for that never seems to come any closer. Not having complex thought doesn't seem to help either; it's been trial and error, one thing at a time, slowly draining my account.

Thanks for listening and for all your help thus far. I'm searching for that podcast now.

\------------

Post your weight, height, body fat %, body temperature under the tongue, blood pressure.

Make inventory of electrical devices in your environment.
Cell phones, wireless routers, microwave, microwave towers, power lines, smart meter.
Avoid blue light after sundown.
Provide high exposure to blue light from sunrise/wakeup to sundown.
