### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-04T18:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133168>

**Accutane side effects**
Get emergency medical help if you have any of these signs of an allergic reaction to Accutane: hives; difficulty breathing; swelling of your face, lips, tongue, or throat. Stop using Accutane and call your doctor at once if you have any of these serious side effects:

-   depressed mood, trouble concentrating, sleep problems, crying spells, aggression or agitation, changes in behavior, hallucinations, thoughts of suicide or hurting yourself;

-   sudden numbness or weakness, especially on one side of the body;

-   blurred vision, sudden and severe headache or pain behind your eyes, sometimes with vomiting;

-   hearing problems, hearing loss, or ringing in your ears;

-   seizure (convulsions);

-   severe pain in your upper stomach spreading to your back, nausea and vomiting, fast heart rate;

-   loss of appetite, dark urine, clay-colored stools, jaundice (yellowing of the skin or eyes);

-   severe diarrhea, rectal bleeding, black, bloody, or tarry stools;

-   fever, chills, body aches, flu symptoms, purple spots under your skin, easy bruising or bleeding;

-   severe blistering, peeling, and red skin rash; or

-   joint stiffness, bone pain or fracture.

Less serious Accutane side effects may include:

-   joint pain, back pain;

-   feeling dizzy, drowsy, or nervous;

-   dryness of the lips, mouth, nose, or skin; or

-   cracking or peeling skin, itching, rash, changes in your fingernails or toenails.

This is not a complete list of side effects and others may occur. Call your doctor for medical advice about side effects. You may report side effects to FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088.

I just found out a month ago I have Lyme after 4 years of hell and like you in 2011 I was suicidal due to symptoms pushing me over the edge of sanity.

I wish someone would have thought to tell me to test for Lyme, no one ever did back then, it would have saved me at least 18k. The test is cheap $99.

Things you said, below in quotes, that scream it worth it for you to get tested for Lyme to rule out this low hanging fruit.

The only place to do it is at IGeneX.com. You will need a doctor to write a RX. The one lab you can start with is called the western blot it costs $99.

You said.

"The only thing I can think of that started this all was a **flu-like illness** I contracted when I was 14 and slowly recovered from over the course of a month"

The above is textbook symptom of what happens after someone gets bitten by a tic with Lyme.

Did you by any chance have any kind of rash at this time that you remember?

"I should also mention that my symptoms get MUCH WORSE after eating/drinking anything (low-carb/high-carb, low-fat/high-fat, etc.). **It feels like I'm being poisoned.**"

Feeling poisoned is EXACTLY what I felt for the last 2 years off and on for no reason at all that made sense.... for me it's the lyme because all my stool tests are negative.

**Nothing works. Labs are always fine, vitals are fine, and told 'it's all in your head'.** As if I like being a miserable, exhausted, 25 year old male with **no libido and decreasing intelligence** isn't bad enough,

What I underlined above is exactly what I experienced and really bad brain fog is also a text book symptom of Lyme.

Here is how it went down for me. Even while I was getting drawn for the Lyme test this past May I remember thinking "this is bullshit I don't have lyme that happens to other people and plus is lyme even real?"

I only took the test because my doc insisted I had lyme based on my symptoms and the test was only $99.

Last thing I guarantee listening to this audiobook you will hear case studies with all your symptoms mentioned and it will feel like finally for the first time there are doctors out there who believe what you are going through and also other people suffering just like you:

**Why Can't I Get Better?: Solving the Mystery of Lyme and Chronic Disease**

You can purchase online at audible for $7.99 or get at a bookstore for around $24 but I prefer audio books.

This test is low hanging fruit $99! It **could** save you years more of suffering and give you your life back in as little as a few years.

If you do have lyme then getting it diagnosed is the first step to being able to apply what you learn here more efficiently.

I have been told that Dr. K said antibiotics are necessary to get rid of Lyme. The hardcore antibiotic protocol treatments are outlined in exhaustive detail in the book I mentioned above. The author Dr. Horowitz purposely outlined the treatment protocol so that if a doctor wants to learn how to do it for a patient they can use his book to help them figure out what to do.

If you get the audio in the beginning of it you will be given a website and password where you can download the treatment protocol and lyme questionnaire.

I hope this helps it is kind of hard to treat something without knowing what your treating wether you have lyme or something else.

5aR---->5-alpha\_reductase

/
5-alpha-reductase inhibitor

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/5-alpha-reductase\_inhibitor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/5-alpha-reductase_inhibitor)

//
Accutane = isotretinoin

The influence of isotretinoin and 5-a reductase inhibitors in metaloproteases of connective tissue in patients with ance (acne).

Kyrakitty, I do not want to discourage you but as someone who has lyme myself I cannot confirm there is a guarantee antibiotics will do any good to you. I tried many things -antibiotics, herbs, supplements etc. I believe now that the only power that could keep this infection controled is in my own body. I follow most of the JK principles though I had to adjust and personalize everything to my own individual needs, I started bioidentical hormonal support and intravenous oxygen therapies. I have a good feeling this brings some progress. I will be starting soon autologous blood injections together with artemisia on top of this. Some people I know have seen remission in some cases complete remission combining antibiotics with intravenous oxygen, I have made zero progress on antibitoics in the past and at this very moment I do not want to disturbe the fragile balance in myslef by any drugs.

### caroline — 2014-07-07T12:38:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133373>

My heart goes out to all of you........xo What a terrible disease this is.....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-07T15:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133386>

When AF is in the final phases it cause a loss of the NMDA receptors in the long distance tracts of the brain designed to have the most DHA......as the DHA is depleted by the ROS and RNS it fast forwards and the ca2+ efflux (better known as excitotoxicity) cause glutamate excess that destroys NMDA. This is when disturbances in thought, consciousness and perception folllow. It is the final common pathway for most prion disease and mental illness and those with poor thoughts tied to low dopamine levels in the brain

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-09T09:01:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/close-to-suicide-adrenal-problems-ruining-my-life.5869/post-133667>

isotretinoin induces [apoptosis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apoptosis) (cell death) in various cells in the body. Cell death may be instigated in the [meibomian glands](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meibomian_gland),[\[58\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Lambert_1989-58)[\[59\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Kremer1994-59) [hypothalamic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypothalamic) cells,[\[60\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Griffin_2010-60) [hippocampus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippocampus) cells[\[61\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Crandall2004-61)[\[62\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotretinoin#cite_note-Sakai_2004-62)

i was put on roaccutane as a teen, worst shit ever...hippocampal apoptosis can accoung for the brain fog/memory issues!

# Thread: Does type of protein matter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Reset 2013
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### andee — 2013-02-13T06:43:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75669>

I'm noticing that certain types of protein seem to keep me satisfied longer - has anyone else had this experience? I could pack away 60 grams of shrimp for my BAB and be hungry an hour later - even adding coconut oil to my coffee and putting butter on the shrimp. 50 grams beef will keep me sated for 3-4 hours. 50 grams Bacon/eggs/lamb will work for 6 or 7 hours. I'm using the heavier proteins for the BAB, which seems to work, but am just wondering if anyone else notices this.

This is a great forum - thanks everyone.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-13T06:52:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75670>

> [andee said:](/goto/post?id=75669)
>
> I'm noticing that certain types of protein seem to keep me satisfied longer - has anyone else had this experience? I could pack away 60 mg of shrimp for my BAB and be hungry an hour later - even adding coconut oil to my coffee and putting butter on the shrimp. 50 mg beef will keep me sated for 3-4 hours. 50 mg Bacon/eggs/lamb will work for 6 or 7 hours. I'm using the heavier proteins for the BAB, which seems to work, but am just wondering if anyone else notices this.
>
> This is a great forum - thanks everyone.

Andee, a lot of people commented on this exact thing when the epi-paleo rx was first released. I tend to do what you do - I eat my saturated fat and meat protein for BAB and (I tend to only eat 2 meals a day) eat my seafood (usually trying to keep it with non-saturated fats) for the second meal. I can't explain why many have this reaction (maybe something to do with the way we burn/use DHA?) but you are not alone.

### andee — 2013-02-13T07:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75672>

KiwiLauren -Thank you! This is a relief. I prefer to eat 2 meals a day as well, and this seems to be the only way to do it.

### caroline — 2013-02-13T08:46:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75675>

Andee I am pretty sure that Dr. Kruse has said the same thing...... If he is having seafood he will also have some meat.
I do that quite often so that I get seafood in at both meals. I, as well, only have two meals. I hope you are doing really well. Keep us posted on your progress ......it helps everyone.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-13T13:01:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75691>

yes types do matter a bit........

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-13T15:28:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75694>

I quite like meat and seafood together.  Even if it's bacon and seafood. Sometimes I accidentally only had seafood for too long of a time. Oops.

Beef heart. Since I discovered beef heart in January I almost never want liver any more. It tastes better and doesn't stink up the whole house (got to be considerate to other people living here who gag from liver).

### andee — 2013-02-13T17:03:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75702>

Thanks, everyone. Bacon and scallops for breakfast, too awesome.

### diane — 2013-02-13T19:44:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75723>

Second (or 3rd) on having the saturated fat for BAB. Although once I've been doing that for a couple of weeks, I find that I can have seafood - shrimp, salmon, etc - for BAB, but I make sure that I have enough fat with it.

Zorica - just an FYI. Heart is not considered organ meat - it's muscle meat. So if you're trying replace liver, it won't be the same. Heart is great - it's just not organ. At least that's what I have learned from years of feeding raw to my dogs.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-13T20:30:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75732>

...4th or 5th on the sat fat for BAB. It's the only thing that keeps me full enough and then I only want one more meal, seafood, to finish out the day. I have plenty of energy now, don't get hungry at night, and really feel, for right now, this is working for me.

### andee — 2013-02-14T03:39:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-type-of-protein-matter.5871/post-75773>

Very true about saturated fat - good reminder. I had a great day, only 2 meals and more energy. Thanks to all for the support.

# Thread: new   where do I start ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### magda — 2013-02-14T00:56:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75757>

Hi,

I believe I was suffering from extreme adrenal fatigue (big move, emergency 5x due to mold exposure, almost lost my marriage, child in hospital) and on top of that I homeschool and worked weekends in a high stress environment. I didn't sleep on the weekends and hardly ate anything and then to find out I was pregnant.

Well...on the 13th of January as I was teaching on the weekend I felt like I have been shot in the head and had the sensation that I will fall down backwards. I was disoriented for a minute and was okay afterwards but low concentration, focus, mental clarity...my digestion was shut...I suddenly couldn't eat nor answer basic questions. I slept 3 days straight after that and ended up in emergency for examination due to the fact that one of my pupils was dialated. I have been recovering slowly since then...cut out sugar, white flour, processed foods since then but mentally and physically I feel exhausted...I go to sleep earlier (used to stay awake til 2am) now and feel like I can't get enough rest. I have an appointment with a naturopath tomorrow but finding a doctor here is very difficult due to shortage and I did find one but since I have just miscarriaged she does not want to check my hormones since it's probably out of whack anyway.

I don't know what my problem is but I really believe that I had extreme adrenal fatigue which caused some nerve damage that day on the 13th ?

Where do I go from here ? Is it sufficient to do the leptin protocol and just paleo diet or is my problem now more serious than that. I had hypgoglycemia but since cutting out sugar I don't think I have any signs of it now. I have cut out working and am completely useless now as I just rest from day to day and am still exhausted.

I need direction.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-14T01:17:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75758>

Welcome.

Start small, and focus on the basics.

Leptin Reset, Epi-paleo (seafood is your primary meat source), focus on limiting artificial light, go out side and ground yourself as much as possible, and CT (Start w/face dunks)

Many of here on the forum have been working on our health for years. its a long road - but you will see improvements. Ask questions, and read (print out the blogs!)

### freshveggies — 2013-02-14T01:44:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75762>

Check out Dr. Shoemaker-----suggests cholestyramine for mold exposure.

### caroline — 2013-02-14T09:35:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75787>

Welcome Magda - it is going to take time.....you didn't get broken overnite. Stick with it very carefully and keep reading - things do become clearer the more you read and pay attention to artificial light - that is really huge.
Keep coming here and asking questions - there are a lot of really smart people here to help....

### diane — 2013-02-14T14:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75808>

If you think it's adrenal fatigue, here is a blog post that is helpful. [http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

Although I don't have it as extreme as you do, the most helpful thing for me has been to start fixing my sleep (increasing melatonin production) by working with meal timing and liminting artificial light after dark.

I like what Shijin said - small steps, start with things that are easy to implement and then add things along the way!

### nonchalant — 2013-02-14T16:36:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-75822>

> [magda said:](/goto/post?id=75757)
>
> I had hypgoglycemia but since cutting out sugar I don't think I have any signs of it now.

Sounds like you have already taken a huge step in the right direction, Magda. Remember that bread is also a sugar. Well, give the body a few seconds and then it's sugar.

### magda — 2013-02-23T02:54:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77193>

I have started feeling better...can sleep at night again...though I go to sleep early around 9-10pm and turn lights down around 5pm and my kids go to sleep around 8pm. I am taking vit C from a whole food source, vit D, adrenal refresh, grounded black seeds and flax seeds, garlic, tumeric in capsules and first and foremost have been praying alot. I still don't eat processed anything but have had a few things once in awhile here and there...today however I feel like I am either experiencing some anxiety and I can feel it all mentally and my pupil was a lil more dilated again earlier this morning or I have some neurological issues...I have no idea...maybe my mental state is still kind of slow and is not there yet...what type of doctor does everyone here go to ? A neurologist or a naturopath ? What kind of testings are recommended ?
Also, I weigh about 150 though I look more like 130 but I have abdominal fat after the birth of my last child when alot of stress has started for me due to life situations and being overwhelmed with the people around me. Is the abdominal fat a sign ? And how do I get rid of it if I can't work out due to adrenal fatigue or have cut out processed foods over a month ago ? I am also 30.

### caroline — 2013-02-23T03:01:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77197>

Have you had an ASI done? any testing at all? testing is important to get a baseline and then go from there. Your hormones are very likely out of wack. Have you had a consult with Dr. kruse? It sounds like you have a lot of demands on you .... keep searching for answers you will get there!

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-23T03:41:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77206>

The 'Getting Started Guide' is a great place to begin. Watch the video on that page and follow the links.
[http://www.jackkruse.com/getting-started-guide/](http://www.jackkruse.com/getting-started-guide/)

### diane — 2013-02-23T04:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-77211>

I've read so many things that are a cause of abdominal fat - excess cortisol, excess estrogen. Getting some of the basic hormone tests and as caroline mentioned an ASI can help you understand.

When I start I find that it can take a few weeks to get over the initial bad feelings - sometimes achy feelings, tired, grouchy - it can vary. I keep focusing on the positive things that have changed for motivation, and then figure out which things I still need to change in order to keep my quest going. I really like understanding that the journey isn't linear - and that has certainly been my experience!

### magda — 2013-03-27T19:50:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-where-do-i-start.5879/post-81975>

I've seen the ASI mentioned a few times but what is it ? Will my doctor understand if I say that I would like ASI testing ? Also from reading the adrenal protocol I realize that I might be really low in progesterone and have estrogen dominance but Jack does not mention how he raised progesterone levels 'immediately' (considering natural methods). How can I naturally raise my progesterone levels and can stress and poor diet be a cause alone for low progesterone. I know I've always had issues with ovulation and upon quitting sugar and exercising I would ovulate instantly and get pregnant but otherwise my body wouldn't do it without these two steps (natural birth control ; ) .

Consults will end 4/21/2013.

### suse1023 — 2013-04-12T18:02:26+0300

i'm reading as fast as i can hoping to get up to speed in time!!

### caroline — 2013-04-14T02:45:24+0300

### caroline — 2013-04-14T08:03:52+0300

### nonchalant — 2013-02-18T16:10:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-5-is-live-get-ready-for-some-windex-on-your-glass-eyes.5887/post-76314>

> [bigknitwit said:](/goto/post?id=76053)
>
> Another energy production mechanism that doesn't involve the mitochondria? Is this is ellusive way in which we can make energy without needing T3?

Our cell membranes produce an electric charge, using sodium/potassium ion pumps. Both ions are positively charged, but the cell pumps out 3 sodium ions and pumps in 2 potassium ions at a time, which results in a net negative charge. The membrane also has leak channels which selectively allow potassium to leak out due to diffusion, but keep sodium out. The pumps use ATP for energy, the leak channels are passive. The cell membrane is an extremely thin insulator, which makes for extremely high capacitance levels between the 2 conductors (the ionic solution inside and outside the cell.)

Hmm, maybe I'm ready for my Bioelectricity 101 test now?

Or is there some quantum effect here (in the energy transfer) that you are elluding to that is not-so-nice?

A cell is not a machine. It is alive and quite dynamic. Each particle in it has its own field it reacts to in the environment. If that magnetic field changes, and ours has in a big way it disorders the order that life creates and a shit storm ensues. What do we find in a living organism you ask? There is organized heterogeneity in life, or dynamic structures on all scales. There is no homogeneity, no static phase held at any level. Even a single cell has its characteristic shape and anatomy, all parts of which are in constant motion (Brownian movement due to water); its electrical potentials and mechanical properties similarly, are subject to cyclic and non-cyclic changes as it responds to, and counteracts environmental fluctuations, like the magnetic field it finds itself in. This determines how well it works. Life is set to work in a set field of harmonic resonance (schumann) to work optimally. This is where a symphony is created from the chaos of chemicals in a cell. So a magnetic field is like a conductor of sorts that sets the tone for how things are done in a cell.
Spatially, the cell is partitioned into numerous compartments by cellular membrane stacks and organelles, each with its own ‘steady state’ of processes that can respond directly to external stimuli and relay signals to other compartments of the cell. Also subject the magnetic field.

With cellular compartments, microdomains can be separately energized to give local circuits, and complexes of two or more molecules can function as ‘molecular energy machines’ by themselves that can cycle autonomously without immediate reference to its surroundings. In other words, the steady ‘state’ is not a state at all but a conglomerate of processes spatiotemporally organised, i.e., it has a deep space-time structure, and cannot be represented as an instantaneous state, or even a configuration of states. This space time structure is subject to precise quantum time. When it is off you get sick and you could die faster than you think. This is where we are today and laid out in EMF-6. Characteristic times of processes range from <10−14 s for resonant energy transfer between molecules to 107 s for circannual rhythms. The spatial extent of processes, similarly, spans at least ten orders of magnitude from 10−10 m for intramolecular interactions to metres for nerve conduction and the general coordination of movements in larger animals like us humans.
The processes are catenated in both **time and space**: the extremely rapid transient flows (very short-lived pulses of chemicals or of energy), which take place on receiving specific signals, are propagated to longer and longer time domains of minutes, hours, days, and so on via interlocking processes that ultimately straddle generations. this is where the quantum dance of epigenetics takes place and if timing is off molecular chaos is the result. Understanding magnetic fields is the domain of quantum biology.

# Thread: Labs for 31 year old male
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stephen — 2013-02-15T12:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-75958>

Any thoughts on my below labs would be greatly appreciated.

As background I'm a 31 year old male, 188cm (6' 2") tall, 80kg (176lbs), and carrying body fat of somewhere under 10%. I've been experimenting with Jack's ideas for quite a while now (at least two winters) with what I feel have been very good results.

Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR): 1mm/hr (1 - 20)

**Full Blood Count**
Hb: 140 g/L (130 - 180)
Total WCC: 4.0 10^9/L (4.0 - 11.0)
RCC: 5.2 10^12/L (4.5 - 6.5)
Hct: 0.44 g/L (0.38 - 0.54)
MCV: 85 fL (80 - 97)
MCH: 29 pg (27 - 32)
MCHC: 336 g/L (320 - 360)
Plt: 193 10^9/L (150 - 450)
Neutrophils: 52% and 2.1 (2.0 - 8.0)
Lymphocytes: 40% and 1.6 (1.0 - 4.0)
Monocytes: 6% and 0.2 (0.0 - 1.0)
Eosinophils: 2% and 0.1 (0.0 - 0.5)
Basophils: 1% and 0.0 (0.0 - 0.3)

Serum Creatinin: 100 umol/L (50 - 120)
eGFR: 76 mL/min/1.73 m2

Fasting Glucose: 5.0 mmol/L (3.0 - 5.5)

Cholesterol: 4.9 mmol/L (3.1 - 5.5)
Triglycerides: 0.5 mmol/L (0.5 - 2.0)
HDL: 2.1 mmol/L (>1.0)
LDL: 2.6 mmol/L (0.0 - 3.5)
CRF (Chol/HDL Ratio): 2.3 (<5.0)

Sodium: 137 mmol/L (135 - 145)
Potassium: 4.9 mmol/L (3.5 - 5.5)
Chloride: 103 mmol/L (95 - 110)
Bicarbonate: 23 mmol/L (19 - 32)
Urea: 8.5 mmol/L (2.5 - 8.0)
Uric acid: 0.36 mmol/L (0.2 - 0.46)
T. Bilirubin: 22 umol/L (1 - 30)
Protein (Total) 69 g/L (62 - 82)
Albumin: 45 g/L (35 - 50)
Globulin 24 g/L (23 - 38)
Alk Phos: 65 U/L (0 - 135)
GGT: 15 U/L (0 - 50)
ALT: 34 U/L (0 - 40)
AST: 25 U/L (0 - 45)
Lactate Dehydro: 120 U/L (120 - 260)
Calcium (Total): 2.39 mmol/L (2.10 - 2.60)
Calcium (Adjusted): 2.29 mmol/L (2.10 - 2.60)
Phosphate (Inorg: 1.29 mmol/L (0.8 - 1.60)

Magnesium: 0.86 mmol/L (0.7 - 1.10)

hsCRP: 0.1 (<0.1 - 8.0)

Iron: 19.1 umol/L (9.0 - 30.0)
Transferrin: 2.2 g/L (2.0 - 3.7)
Total Iron Bind: 51 umol/L (45 - 80)
Saturation: 37.5% (16.0 - 60.0)
Ferritin: 159 ug/L (30 - 300)

Serum Zinc: 12 umol/L (10 - 16)

TSH: 0.76 mIU/L (0.40 - 3.80)

25 OH Vitamin D: 179.5 nmol/L (25.0 - 139.0)

<25 Deficient
25 - 74 Insufficient
75 - 250 Sufficient
\>250 Potential intoxication

Testosterone (Total): 13.58 nmol/L (7.00 - 30.00)
SHBG: 22.4 nmol/L (11.00 - 78.00)
Testosterone Free Calculated: 329 pmol/L (120 - 470)

### Destiny — 2013-02-15T13:59:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-75960>

Stephen, you need to add ranges with your labs.

### Stephen — 2013-02-16T04:54:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-76074>

Hi Destiny, thanks for the heads up. I've added the lab ranges above.

Cheers,

Stephen

### Stephen — 2013-04-09T13:50:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-83455>

Does anyone have any input on my labs?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-04-09T16:21:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-83469>

mitochondrial inefficiency and some early signs of EMF/fake light.......testosterone is low for age.......yet HS CRP IS good. T bili CO2 and BUN ratios show that risk..........you need to dive into more testing as you acclimate your life to see how bad it might be.

### Stephen — 2013-09-13T05:19:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-95758>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=83469)

> mitochondrial inefficiency and some early signs of EMF/fake light.......testosterone is low for age.......yet HS CRP IS good. T bili CO2 and BUN ratios show that risk..........you need to dive into more testing as you acclimate your life to see how bad it might be.

Thanks for responding Jack. Much appreciated.

Do you have any specific suggestions for further testing based on what you see in my labs?

Cheers,

Stephen

### Jack Kruse — 2013-09-13T05:40:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-for-31-year-old-male.5899/post-95759>

I think an ASI and salivary melatonin would be very helpful to really look at fake light and EMF risk.....Also a thyroid panel and full iron work up.

# Thread: small win!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 42
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T02:48:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76055>

We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-16T02:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76056>

Yeah Lauren...awesome.... its the little things that make us feel awesome...

### fitness@home — 2013-02-16T03:17:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76060>

> [KiwiLauren said:](/goto/post?id=76055)
>
> We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

This is great Lauren! I'd like to duplicate your experiment during our summer. Unfortunately, the peak sun time is while I'm at work. I don't think the parents at the park I frequent for my lunch hour each day would appreciate it If I sunbathed semi-naked.

Wonder how many days per week would produce measurable results? I have weekends and every other Friday off.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T03:25:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76061>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=76060)
>
> This is great Lauren! I'd like to duplicate your experiment during our summer. Unfortunately, the peak sun time is while I'm at work. I don't think the parents at the park I frequent for my lunch hour each day would appreciate it If I sunbathed semi-naked.
>
> Wonder how many days per week would produce measurable results? I have weekends and every other Friday off.

LOL. I wonder if you could get away with a tank top and shorts at the park during lunch hours? And then do longer on weekends and your Fridays off. My yard has very little privacy and while I mostly work from home I did have to wear something most days. I think if you could get 80% of your body exposed that would be enough. It was a really great experiment to do, though, to understand how my body is functioning at the moment. (By the way, if you try it, the way we talked about was to re-test Vitamin D with about a month of summer left to go - that way if your levels are falling/not sustaining themselves, you still have ample time to supplement and get them higher before winter.)

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-16T15:15:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76104>

Love this! Would you care to share your before (summer)/now lab numbers?

### BJK77 — 2013-02-16T16:34:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76110>

That's great Lauren! Isn't it such an empowering feeling to be able to take control of your own health? Love it!

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T17:11:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76112>

Were you expecting it \*not\* to work?

### diane — 2013-02-16T17:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76113>

That's great Lauren! Did you go out whether it was sunny or not? Do we still get benefit from the vitamin D on cloudy days, but perhaps at lower levels?

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T17:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76114>

Vit D production varies with time of day, time of year, latitude, cloudiness, clothes, haze, ozone, etc. Clouds block the rays that produce D more or less, depending on thickness & totality of coverage.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-16T17:55:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76116>

not exactly true when quantum time is off.......

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T18:47:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76119>

Sure, there's the possibility that one's body can't make D even in the best of circumstances. That's why I asked the question, was she expecting she \*couldn't\* make D?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2013-02-16T19:06:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76122>

> [KiwiLauren said:](/goto/post?id=76055)
>
> We are winding down our summer here. Before summer began Dr Kruse discussed with me the idea of doing an n-1 test re: my ability to synthesise vitamin D without supplementation. I have done semi-naked grounded sunbathing for at least one hour per day over summer. In addition to being very tan for the first time in my life, my levels are now higher than when I was supplementing. (I still probably need to supplement to get firmly into the optimal range, but according to the NZ lab levels I'm over the top!). Hurray. This is a small win, but seems a really positive sign.

Yippeeee! Love it! Go full monte, broadcast, and then you can afford even more labs!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-16T21:01:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76137>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=76119)
>
> Sure, there's the possibility that one's body can't make D even in the best of circumstances. That's why I asked the question, was she expecting she \*couldn't\* make D?

MG, there seems to be some suggestion that some people cannot make vitamin D on their own. It's way out of my intellectual league, but even my doctor said to me that I might be one of those people. Then of course there is the idea that my current state is/was too 'broken' to do this properly. For me, a year + ago, I wouldn't have lasted 10 minutes without sunburn. I had to avoid peak sun times. This alone makes it pretty damn hard to get sufficient vitamin D. So there were multiple factors I was ruling out. And I was pleased to say that I can now eliminate them all.

BKW, I'm still trying to make sure I've accurately converted my UK-system numbers to the US-system and I'm not sure I have. What I can tell you for certain is that prior to stopping supplementation, I was in the top 1/3 of the New Zealand range for vitamin D. For the past 4 months I have had no supplementation at all, regardless of weather. (I did get outside on cloudy days; I didn't if it was pouring rain. I always tried to be outside at the hottest time of the day. I usually got more than 1 hour a day because I've been trying to work from home outside, too.). My current results are at the very top of the range. (But, take this with a grain of salt because my initial attempts at converting appear to show that the top of the NZ range is bottom of the optimal US one.)

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T21:49:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76150>

That really is awesome! I have definitely improved my sun tolerance in the last few years, but I have no idea which thing did it. And I would certainly like to improve it much more. What do you think was the big key (or keys) for you?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-16T21:54:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76153>

in 3 blogs all to be revealed...........your ride is coming close to end........

### Danco3636 — 2013-02-16T21:59:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76155>

These blogs have really fired me up. Can't wait but I know I still have re-reading, studying and understanding to work on. That and dialing it in on myself.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-16T22:05:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76156>

the next three.......well epic wont cut it

### Martin — 2013-02-16T22:13:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76159>

And, my good doctor, what's after _those?!_

What _other_ discoveries are you sitting on, waiting _way_ too patiently, to spring upon us?

### Dali Dula — 2013-02-17T16:29:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76201>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=76150)
>
> That really is awesome! I have definitely improved my sun tolerance in the last few years, but I have no idea which thing did it. And I would certainly like to improve it much more. What do you think was the big key (or keys) for you?

Within a month of going Paleo back in June 11 my sun tolerance improved dramatically. I have not used sunblock since. I have not sunburned and I spend a lot of time in the sun.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-17T17:10:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76205>

Yes, I'm certain it's connected to my diet, but not totally sure what aspect. Also, perfect diet still didn't get me more than 30m in the noon sun on the equinox without burning, but that's better than the 15 it used to be!

### vkiernan — 2013-02-17T17:33:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76208>

Yeah Lauren, Any win is huge in our game. So happy for you!

### caroline — 2013-02-18T01:59:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76281>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=76201)
>
> Within a month of going Paleo back in June 11 my sun tolerance improved dramatically. I have not used sunblock since. I have not sunburned and I spend a lot of time in the sun.

This has happened to me too. I spend hours in the sun. I do not use sun block at all and don't burn and I am ridiculously brown - never, ever happened before.
People are always asking me about it. A girl in a vitamin/health shop asked me what I was eating - I thought that was pretty intuitive.....

### Danco3636 — 2013-02-18T02:19:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76285>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=76281)
>
> This has happened to me too. I spend hours in the sun. I do not use sun block at all and don't burn and I am ridiculously brown - never, ever happened before.
> People are always asking me about it. A girl in a vitamin/health shop asked me what I was eating - I thought that was pretty intuitive.....

Similar things happen to me with the sun as well. Funny thing my wife and I went to Hawaii last week. After landing and getting settled I went for a quick ocean dip. It was later in the day. It seemed like immediately I started browning. It was as if my body new to start tanning. By the next day I was noticeably darker. My wife and I laughed as we joked that just the mention of Hawaii I get tan. Funny stuff.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-18T02:43:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76290>

I love these stories, so awesome!

### nonchalant — 2013-02-18T03:09:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-76294>

It's still winter here, but when I go out and get some rays, I'm careful to keep my feet at least on the ground. I have put away my reclining lounge chair, yet another relic of the way things used to be...

### amber_tyrrell@hotmail.com — 2013-03-03T10:56:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78731>

I have had a similar experience Lauren. Have just got some blood tests back. I'm not sure what the conversion is, and probably still low for what Jack wants it to be, but at the beginning of spring (september) my vit D levels were 70 nmol/L, in Dec they were 102 nmol/L, and end of Feb (and end of summer) 124 nmol/L. This is with no supplementation at all, just naked sunbathing when I could over the summer, not every day, maybe 2 to 3 days per week.

### Dextery — 2013-03-03T20:49:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78779>

Here is an online Vit D3 conversion table. Plug in your values and calculate. [http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Vitamin\_\_D.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Vitamin__D.php)

Your 124 nmol/L is around 50ng/ml We need 70 to 100ng/ml but your 50 is great. Just more sun is needed.

### amber_tyrrell@hotmail.com — 2013-03-04T05:49:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78873>

> [Dextery said:](/goto/post?id=78779)
>
> Here is an online Vit D3 conversion table. Plug in your values and calculate. [http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Vitamin\_\_D.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Vitamin__D.php)
>
> Your 124 nmol/L is around 50ng/ml We need 70 to 100ng/ml but your 50 is great. Just more sun is needed.

Thank you Dextery. Our range is between 50 and 150 nmol/L here in New Zealand. That is very low compared to the US recommendation! I'll need to keep working on getting mine up before the winter, may have to take some supplements.

### Dextery — 2013-03-05T02:43:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78991>

### nonchalant — 2013-03-05T03:12:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78994>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=76112)
>
> Were you expecting it \*not\* to work?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-05T03:15:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-78996>

deep tans do not mean your D is good.....my wife is close to black.......I am a white boy with freckles.......my D was just 129. Hers 43. I am naked boy......she is my scaredy cat.........today she got naked. Truth in labs.

### Inger — 2013-03-05T08:15:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-win.5910/post-79036>

>

Suppose you only have 15-20 minutes to devote to CT on a very busy day. Do you choose to:

A) Hope in a 50-degree cold tub for 20 minutes; or
B) Spend 10-15 minutes outside in 32 degree weather playing in the snow!

I'm opting for B just to soak up the electrons along with the benefits of the cold, but curious what others think since there would be less time outside and it's definitely not full submersion.

# Thread: One year anniversary for CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2013-02-17T16:07:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76199>

So it's about a year since many of us started the CT protocol. How you all doing? Thoughts, comments, results, transformations? I have had a great time with CT. Truly life changing.
My thyroid function,indicated mostly by normal body temp and energy levels, has improved. I still take synthroid, but dosage has been reduced.
My labs for the most part are not drastically improved, But I feel great.
All of my physical functions have improved; digestion, sexual, brain fog.
Did I mention I feel great? My body comp, energy level, strength, and speed are all greatly improved. I can run really fast.
I love the fact that I can now swim anywhere unless the water is frozen. Playing in the surf in the cold is a blast, it makes me laugh.
I have enjoyed outdoor winter activities more than ever. Hiking and skiing in a t shirt or shirtless in temps I never would have thought possible before.
I now embrace the cold instead of hiding from it.
My weight has not changed for over 8 months 5'9" 165 lbs and I still continue to improve body comp. No weight change, just trading muscle for fat.
I shiver much less than I did at the beginning.
I love the CT high. Endocannabinoids at play.
Did I mention I feel great?

Let us know how this is working for you. Happy Anniversary!

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-17T16:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76203>

I'm loving CT When I do it... Though sometimes its a challenge w/my Pg and Thyroid tanked... but I keep pushing forward... its the one thing that's saving me... a friend is giving us her xtra deep freezer - we're going to use it specifically for making ice...

### PrimalPam — 2013-02-17T16:43:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76204>

I've come to love my time in the tub. I leave my Ipod docking station and thermometer in the bathroom all the time now, so I have no excuses. Just throw my ice in, fill the tub, and get in. I've created a bunch of playlists so I can just lay there until my time is up. Recently, I've bought some ice pack things so I can spot ice while hanging around the house, and I'm finding it's just not cold enough!

I confess that I skipped about 4 months of CT and just recently got back in the tub. I had ice, frozen in plastic containers all over the freezer, for 4 straight months. Don't ask me why I stopped, I just did. Recently, I got back in, and it's like an addiction, where I want to get in every day. The good news is that, after 4 months off, getting back in was a breeze. I was afraid of that "new CT'er learning curve", but it was super easy.

Funny, but when I first started CT last year, I did 5x/week and had no detox symptoms. But after 4 months off (and some holiday non-EP eating), I broke out all over my face. Serves me right.

So, the reason I decided to get my big a\*\* back in? My FBG had gone up. After 4 months of being out of the tub, FBG was in the 115-120 range. Yikes. It had gotten down to the high 80's/low 90's, and here I was, playing with fire. So I'm going to wait a few weeks and take it again. Intellectually, I KNOW CT lowers FBG, so why did I think I was going to keep it down without it??

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-17T18:02:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76211>

I think CT has been the backbone for most of the changes I've experienced this year. I love the feeling that I get while I'm in the water and afterwards. I've gained all kinds of muscle that certainly hasn't appeared because of exercise, I like the way my body looks leaner and more defined (new experience!), I know that CT will brighten my day mentally which is a big plus for me, I've liked the challenge of figuring out how I can CT outside without hurting my hands and feet (Reynauds), I've learned to find the sweet spot that comes with the right temp. and the right amount of time...and how that changes through the seasons. I'm officially in love with our pond!

I think I'm going to be one of those people who will continue to see changes over a really long period of time particularly in the hormone department, but I'm in for the long haul and CT is just part of my life now

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-17T18:06:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76212>

soon you'll see why you feel that way

### caroline — 2013-02-18T01:04:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-year-anniversary-for-ct.5920/post-76274>

I am thrilled for all the amazing results you guys are having and your dedication. What a massive change in a year physically and most important mentally for all of us.

I go in the ocean everyday and can't be without it - it is like something very, very important is missing in my day - so - no matter how late - I still do it. It isn't cold enough and I can't get my bath tub cold enough either so I am hanging out for winter!

# Thread: CT Adaption time
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2013-02-17T21:55:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76240>

So I've been doing CT for a solid week and don't really notice any difference. I know 2 weeks is the given time for adaption and that may vary, but I'm curious as to any signs I can look for to know whether I'm improving.

For those that have successfully adapted....radiating heat and all the various improvements..did you notice it all at once? Or did you notice that you were radiating heat one day and than a few days down the road it increased? Is there some progression here? Or for some people did it just seem to happen all at once. Also what were the list of benefits. Maybe some happened progressively while some just seemed to show up all at once?

For example did it seem like nothing was going on for 13 days and day 14 bam radiating heat etc...? Also for some reason I'm wondering if eating carbohydrates to a great degree could be slowing me down. I'll reread the CT series...but I do find it interesting the amount of time it takes to adapt to fat and get into good Ketosis is 2 weeks...the same amount of time for CT.

Is this related at all...is this part of the reason high fat meals go with CT? Do you need to be running on Ketones at least partially to adapt? I've been struggling with binge eat well cycles..where I binge on carbohydrates and than eat well from 1-4 days and than binge again.

Am I making it impossible to adapt to the cold by binging? Do I need a full two weeks of high fat and relatively low carb for this to work? Has anyone adapted while eating larger amounts of carbohydrates?

Thanks as always...really hope I can get past this binge issue...I'm worried I'm wrecking my body..but so many things set me off. I can't seem to eat anything with sugar including Fruit...and than I binge. Do I need to just stay away from anything with carbohydrates for a good long time to get rid of this?

lkajsdlkfffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffassssssssssssssssssfjalksjdfalksdjf

So frustrating...I hate the cold. It just seems to last forever sitting in cold water....the cold isn't even all that bad to me....afterwards when I start shivering is worse, but the monotony...sitting there frustrating.

When the temperature of the water is around 45-50 or lower my hands after CT feel ...sort of a numb sensation. Is it possible I'm damaging my hands...is damage likely above freezing temperatures? I suppose the warmth goes away from the extremities...Is it perhaps partly a detox reaction in my hands. I'm wondering if I should worry about it or not. It lasts for days...don't like the feeling.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T09:51:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76637>

I don't know if I know enough to help you - but I can try. A lot of people wear caps and gloves and socks when doing C/T wool is the best. Eating carbs won't be helping - high fat is the way to go.

A lot of us take an ipad/ipod or some such into the tub with us and listen to podcasts or webinars or just music. I would go totally nuts without something to do or listen to!

Maybe reread the quick start guides again for C/T and the leptin reset - I think you are missing a couple of things or forgotten. I know I have to keep re reading the blogs.... as Dr. K. says - we get new eyes and understand things better the second and third time around. Sometimes I will swear I have never seen a blog before but then realise that yes I have - I just understand it better now.

I am beginning to realise that fruit is a big NO for me - I love fruit - but I am doing much, much better with none at all. My weight has stabilised much better and the cravings for fruit are much less.

Do you intend to keep eating a lot of carbs?

The BAB is - without question - the most important part of the reset. If you eat enough protein/ fat for your BAB - it will stop the cravings. We have all experienced this. I had to eat a huge amount of protein for breakfast but I quickly went to 2 meals a day and had absolutely no cravings what so ever.

I hope I have offered some help...

Also - maybe don't be so worried about a time frame - everyone is very, very different. Personal Context is everything!

### freesia — 2013-02-21T05:31:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76831>

I was radiating warmth _occasionally_ a few weeks in. But I still had periods where I seemed to go backwards for a bit - ie: baths were harder and recovery took longer.

If you have numbness of any kind I guess there's a chance you could be going at it too hard too fast? I had blue fingers in the beginning...even though I didn't put my hands in (and kept my feet and head insulated). I just slowed down and took it easier. Still inched my way forward. I didn't adapt in 2 weeks, I think because of my particular health challenges and need to pace.

I wasn't eating many carbs last winter when I adapted. I was doing leptin reset.
Winter diet, and winter stimulus to the CNS via skin work _together_. We're designed pretty well actually.

Hope that helps.

### Raiken3712 — 2013-02-21T11:10:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76862>

I don't want to keep eating carbohydrates...its just hard. Its all around and its hard to tackle. None of my family is doing the same thing as me, and I work at Walgreens where there is always some new thing that compels me to try.

I know its not good for me but for some reason I can't seem to tell myself no. I've tried doing things like saying I won't eat whatever..instead of I can't eat it. I've tried eating just small amounts of things...or eating something healthy like Pistachios....but my mind is constantly going back to foods I don't need because they are just too easy to access. I know if I could just stay away from them for awhile maybe this would diminish, but I keep failing time and time again to have any consistent time away from them.

So tired. Will keep trying. Like tonight I ate burritos with Rice, Corn, Tomato, and Hamburger meat....just because it was there and my mom cooked it. No one offered it...I just knew I felt like it because I know it tastes good.

Than I regret it as soon as its over...my brain is screwed up...I know its bad and I ignore myself...than I attack myself for doing it anyway...and than cycle...cycle over and over the same things.

Thanks for the comments...really hope to get this figured out before I end up with some really horrible health...its already not great.

### ashryn — 2013-02-21T15:04:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76872>

For what it's worth, i'll try to help by offering my thoughts too.. Don't stress,too much about the carbs, just for your first week or so.. Do the BAB perfectly.. Within 30 mins of waking and a minimum of 50 grams protein and a bunch of fat.. Eat whatever for the rest of the day, but try to avoid carbs if you feel strong enough. At the same time make sure you are minding the artificial light. Once you have those things under control, other things will be easier. Once you sleep better you will be stronger and make better decisions. Aim for two weeks on this, them go in full with no carbs if you can..

Once you're getting the reset going along nicely, think about adding cold.. Ease yourself into it.. You have time, it is a lifestyle change after all. You have to train your body to do it.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-21T16:34:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-76890>

AFTER you read EMF 7 you'll know why CT is taking a long time to work.......you have lost massive electrons and you are closer to your resting place then a state of health.......no more beating around the Bush.......the cat is out of the bag.

### PrimalPam — 2013-02-23T22:54:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-77333>

I was doing CT 5 days/week, 50F, 45 minutes, for 5 months and did not feel cold-adapted. I got busy over the holidays and have started back up, same schedule, and still do not feel cold-adapted. We are that broken; it is not a quick fix. We are a society of "This pill will work NOW"; well, I don't think one becomes cold-adapted for quite a while. I see tons of benefits from the cold (improved sleep, fat loss, and just pure enjoyment of it), but I do not consider myself cold-adapted.

I lay in the freezing cold tub sometimes thinking, well, I spent decades doing such damage to myself; it's going to take a lot more than a 45-minute bath to undo it.

Be patient, but stay away from the carbs too. Eat your BAB and try to only eat one other time a day, tons of seafood and fat. I do have a "mini-meal" for lunch, which is a large brazil nut, some 99% chocolate, and a few spoonfuls of coconut manna. This keeps any hunger at bay till dinner.

### freesia — 2013-02-23T23:31:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-adaption-time.5923/post-77335>

Pam, just curious...what does your temperature do while you're in the tub?
Mine rises 1/2 - 2/3 degree celsius by 5 minutes in, and actually starts to rise as I'm making trips to and from the freezer to pile the ice in so before I even hit the water. I saw signs of this several weeks in, but it wasn't consistent until much later on.
Glad you're feeling benefits anyway...nothing like a few benefits to keep you motivated

Are you talking body temperature? I don't measure body temperature while I'm in the tub, only skin temp to keep my skin around 50F. I am still confused about body temperature: If one is cold-adapted, should it be higher? 98.6? Lower? I've done searches through the CT series and can't seem to find it.

Hi, yes I had a really low basal body temperature too - for decades actually! I remember a science teacher commenting and getting me to triple check when the class measured temps for some project. My pulse rate was abnormally low too.
My temperature was always low when I checked mid-afternoon too....if it was _too_ low I skipped CT.
So temps were my benchmark of progress here (and sleep & cortisol rhythm improvements).

...Anyway after a few weeks I noticed my temperature would rise in response to the cold stimulus...originally it would hold for 20 mins...then longer.
I still got "after drop" though - still do sometimes but it's not major like it used to be....15 minute recovery usually.

So I am 39 1/2, still hoping for a baby, working through various issues (LR, thyroid, adrenal, etc) and doing CT...not as much as I should but loving whatever I am doing so much I now crave the cold and don't want spring to come (yet). So I was recently told by my acupuncturist that in Chinese Medicine, it is key to stay warm...not hot, but warm, so no raw foods, nothing right out of the fridge, and definitely nothing frozen (so no cold water to bathe in or consume either before CT)....and now I'm torn. What would you do??

Thing is it both makes sense to me....but I guess since I am still inflamed (haven't gotten pregnant is one sign?!) so I should just keep up my CT....

Would love to hear any of your thoughts.....especially because a part of me has been thinking I should up my CT stat...

I have had some successes so far with all I am doing...better sleep/energy, lost 9 lbs, feel better mentally, i.e. more joy, contentment, really loving the cold and dark (which until this winter, my first with Epipaleo, CT etc) so I am happy....but still hoping for that miracle too.

Thanks for responding in advance....

# Thread: Dr Shoemaker asked this question on leptin:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2013-02-18T22:07:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/post-76353>

**"**Daily Anaerobic Threshold Exercises

Patients who like to exercise have long used daily anaerobic threshold exercises to improve the chronic fatigue that comes from biotoxin illnesses. The Chronic Fatigue community often will cite the observed â€œpush, crashâ€￾ phenomenon as evidence for that syndrome but that idea is incorrect. Whenever there is capillary hypoperfusion, and that occurs with uncorrected elevation of inflammatory compounds like C4a, MMP9 and TGF beta-1, delivery of oxygen to capillary beds is reduced, mitochondrial production of ATP is lowered and glycogen reserves are exhausted. â€œPushâ€￾ means demanding that energy be consumed; capillary hypoperfusion means the energy will be consumed inefficiently. â€œCrashâ€￾ occurs when the glycogen reserves are depleted and patients start to burn protein for fuel (Alanine and glutamine are amino acids that are quickly converted to sugar) but that conversion isnâ€™t enough to supply the energy needs of the body. The crash usually lasts more than one day as it takes that long to replenish glycogen reserves.

**I have often wondered what the role of leptin was in this scenario, as leptin resistance, usually seen in MSH deficiency, protects fat reserves, prevents beta oxidation of fatty acids and prevents release of fatty acids from fat cells.** There may be more to the physiology. Leptin also acts to reduce the amount of adiponectin made and also reduces the amounts of the receptor for adiponectin. Adiponectin helps muscles (especially) burn fat directly.

The attached literature search may have some interest as long term anaerobic threshold exercise induces production of activity of the gene for adiponectin and the adiponectin receptor. We know that microRNA can suppress survival of mRNA so it is possible that a microRNA is made in biotoxin illnesses that suppresses both adiponectin and its receptor. We also know that antagomirs block microRNA and if there is a microRNA that we donâ€™t want to see blocking production of fat burners, we could theoretically block the blocker by a medication that would be the antagomir.

Such a compound would theoretically promote weight loss in those with leptin resistance, a phenomenon that occurs in at least 33% of our overweight population.**"**
(end quote)

Last time I saw Dr Shoemaker, he was overweight, and has recently retired due to health issues. He's even written a weight loss book, "Lose the Weight You Hate."

Any ideas as to what he's missing?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-19T00:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-shoemaker-asked-this-question-on-leptin.5934/post-76377>

EMF 7 will lay it out clearly........I promise.

# Thread: Extremes: Life, Death and the Limits of the Human Body by Dr Kevin Fong
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-02-19T17:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76480>

On another thread I mentioned watching a documentary by Dr Kevin Fong about the effects of cold on the body. Well, I just found out he's written a book called Extremes: Life, Death and the Limits of the Human Body which will be released next month

Copied from Amazon

In anaesthetist Dr Kevin Fong's television programmes he has often demonstrated the impact of extremes on the human body by using his own body as a 'guinea pig'. So Dr Fong is well placed to share his experience of the sheer audacity of medical practice at extreme physiological limits, where human life is balanced on a knife edge.

Through gripping accounts of extraordinary events and pioneering medicine, Dr Fong explores how our body responds when tested by the extremes of heat and cold, vacuum and altitude, age and disease. He shows how science, technology and medicine have taken what was once lethal in the world and made it survivable.

This is not only a book about medicine, but also about exploration in its broadest sense - and about how, by probing the very limits of our biology, we may ultimately return with a better appreciation of how our bodies work, of what life is, and what it means to be human.

**Review**
'It would be hard to find anyone better qualified to write a book on the limits of human physiology than Dr Kevin Fong. His experiences in human spaceflight at NASA, in frontline medicine, and his deep scientific knowledge, shine through. If you want to know what the human body can take, and why we must continue to push ourselves beyond the limit in the name of exploration, then read this book.' (Professor Brian Cox

**About the Author**
Dr Kevin Fong is a doctor of medicine with a special interest in human space exploration and extreme environment physiology. He holds degrees in medicine, astrophysics and engineering, and is an honorary senior lecturer in physiology at University College London. He has completed specialist training in anaesthesia and intensive care medicine, has worked with NASA's Human Adaptation and Countermeasures Office at Johnson Space Center in Houston and the medical operations group at Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral. He currently works as a consultant anaesthetist at University College London Hospital, is founder and associate director of the Centre for Altitude, Space and Extreme Environment Medicine. He is a Wellcome Trust Public Engagement Fellow.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-19T19:01:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76485>

He would love EMF 7 then........

### freesia — 2013-02-19T19:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/extremes-life-death-and-the-limits-of-the-human-body-by-dr-kevin-fong.5948/post-76490>

Looks like it could be a good read HoneyChild.
He's got enough interesting credentials to have some valuable insights!
Thanks for the head up.
