# Thread: Coming to terms with dying - other people that is
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 74
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-20T01:10:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76578>

It has hit home quite hard to me this last week that if I am to have better health than most of my peers then I had better get used to seeing friends and family go downhill and die. My sister has a heart condition. She looks terrible and thinks she looks good for someone her age. She had previously renamed all her heart symptoms as other things such as asthma, stress, tiredness, etc and continues to do so to a great extent. Basically she is very ill by my standards although her doctors are happy with the way she has bounced back after 10 days in the intensive coronary care unit.

So if we, as a community here, are going to be healthy and most of the rest of the world aren't then we need to develop a strong sense of acceptance when others choose other pathways to deal with their health issues. My sister is very pleased for me, but she doesn't want to hear what she could do that might make a difference. She is choosing differently to what I would like her to do. She thinks I'm a bore about health. So having said my piece all I can do is to respect her choices and continue loving her as she swallows her blood thinners and all the other 20 odd pills they have her on daily.

We also have to come to terms with people being very ill and dying while we stay healthy and know they could be healthier.

But its hard. I weep a little.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T01:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76585>

Aussie Nana,

Your words resonate with me. I have a very hard time accepting my parent's choices in healthcare. My Mom has many, many health issues and takes so many prescriptions.

Since I don't live in Michigan any longer I am lucky to see her twice per year. The rest of the family doesn't see the very noticeable decline in her health as I do. To me, it's almost shocking.

I have learned to accept their choices, even if I don't agree. What else does one do? I love my parents and want them around as long as possible.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T04:44:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76609>

This is going to be really tragic for all of us I fear. When I look back and think of all the things that may have helped my husband I shed many tears ....but I didn't know better then \[the ironic thing is he would have listened to me\]

I am now looking backwards with new eyes - doesn't stop the incredible sadness though and it is hard to forgive yourself.

On the other hand - my daughter needs these protocols desperately and won't listen to me ......yes , I too am a bore about health it seems.

Ultimately - we can only help ourself. In my case - the changes are very noticeable so why wouldn't that motivate someone you love so much?

Most seem to be stuck in such a CW rut and can't or don't want to put in the work. That is unimaginable to me but I can't change it.

JK always says ....."People don't care what you know until they know that you care" we will see.......

### Kiera2 — 2013-02-20T05:38:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76622>

Yes it's heartbreaking to watch people we love continue to risk their health. I share what we're doing every time someone comments on how well we look, but most don't want to know once they realise it's a lifestyle change, not just a temporary diet.

It's bad enough when it's the long distance in-laws, but my eldest son pretends to think that I'm a nutcase following some voodoo dr off the internet. He knows I'm not stupid, he just doesn't want to give up his lifestyle. He's been an online gamer for over a decade. It breaks my heart to know that he's giving up years of his life for it. And my eldest daughter has moved location with her employer; she's now deep in a bank's secure bunker/call centre. I can't image the EMF in there.

### caroline — 2013-02-20T08:38:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76634>

You are so right Coriander - they may listen if it is a temporary diet ..... but a llifestlye change .... they don't even pretend to be slightly interested. I don't get it ... this journey has been incredibly exciting and positive in so many ways and what did it cost us? some ice and a grocery budget spent on seafood instead of potato chips . We get to look at the sunrise and the sunset and the stars in the night sky. I love that I can't be bothered with TV. I love that it is more important to interact with endlessley interesting people whether it is family or friends or strangers. I love how I feel and I love the joy in my heart.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T12:46:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76642>

> [Coriander said:](/goto/post?id=76622)
>
> It's bad enough when it's the long distance in-laws, but my eldest son pretends to think that I'm a nutcase following some voodoo dr off the internet. He knows I'm not stupid, he just doesn't want to give up his lifestyle. He's been an online gamer for over a decade. It breaks my heart to know that he's giving up years of his life for it..

This! My stepson is the same way. He actually stood behind me for a few minutes while I was watching the Sleep webinar. JK was talking about EMFs and the kid rolls his eyes and actually says, "what rock is he hiding under? Today's world is advanced and uses devices and wifi all the time". He missed the entire damn point... Yes, we have filled our world with these things and are now realizing they are BAD for us as a species.

Seriously, he is 25 years old and has horrible eating habits. Poor diet = poor dental health = poor health for him in general. I fear that by age 35 he will have serious health issues. It was so ironic that it was the Sleep webinar that I was watching because his sleep pattern is so disrupted. There are times he is up all night.

Sorry for the vent...felt good to get it off my chest

### Destiny — 2013-02-20T13:34:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76643>

I think it is a process with the grown up kids. I am always e-mailing them tidbits of information and talking about Dr.K's stuff whenever I see them.

They take some thing and use it and ignore other. I think the problem is that they are young, relatively healthy and feel immortal.

I will keep persisting.... what other choice do I have? To give up on them, NEVER!

### SeaJane — 2013-02-20T16:50:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76660>

I have been walking this path of how much to push Jack's stuff with my mom. She was diagnosed with small cell lung cancer a year ago. She opted for chemotherapy to reduce the metastatic tumor that was causing pain and invading her abdominal cavity. She has always been health oriented and avoids the general medical establishment and their takes on things. She read a couple of books such as Anti-Cancer and another by a Dr. Block that advocate non-ketogenic diets, and opted for that approach even though I sent her info on the benefits of ketogenic diets with cancer. The chemo was successful in reducing the tumors and my mom kept pretty much on her "new" diet and started to add back in some dairy to help gain some weight back. Within 2 months the tumor had regrown and she opted for radiation therapy. This was in December. I was heart-broken after learning what I have from Jack's site. I just felt that the doctor's were killing her with their treatments. Radiation has been much harder for her and currently although she is technically done with treatments, her body is still suffering the cumulative action that is to take up to 4 weeks before it gets better. She has no appetite. She has now down to about 90lbs from her normal weight of 115lbs. Her energy (electrons) have been sucked out of her by the treatment. When I visited at the end of January, I suggested eating more oysters as she likes them and they are nutrient dense. This she did. As they live in St. Pete, I suggested she go and sit at the water's edge and put her feet in the water. This she wouldn't do because she already felt so cold--her body is struggling to generate enough energy to even keep her warm. There were things I said that I could see did not even register--her mind could not allow that to be so. What has become evident to me is that this illness and it's treatment has affected my mom's ability to stand back and question and to be open to new info. I am pretty sure her dopamine levels are in the tank and this is the underlying cause of suboptimal choices and her mind rationalizing shifts in her beliefs to support her behavior. It is challenging and painful to share with someone who has no space for something that may relieve suffering and bring health.

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-20T18:42:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76699>

This is challenging for me b/c in the past two years I've seen the impacts of bad diet, horrible sleep, and an unwillingness to change impact my mom. she turns 70 on 15 March. I've tried to engage her - and she's told me point blank that she's not changing a damn thing, she likes her life, and isn't going to "rock" the boat.
When DH and I had our talk on Saturday night after Jack dropped the MOAB on him, I told him I don't think my mom will make it to 80. I actually think her mom will outlive her. And that's scary. She's on statins, she hasn't had her BHRT adjusted since she started it 20yrs ago. In the past two years - she's suddenly looking far older than her age. I realize now that her in ability to get good sleep is what's doing this. On average I bet she's probably sleeping maybe 3-4hrs/night maybe 3-4 nights out of the week, and sleeping less the rest of the time. The scary thing, is I'm not sure my dad will survive her passing - he's not really on speaking terms with one of his brothers, and his other brother is a total flake. What's crazy is I'm coming to the realization, that while I may not get along well with my Mother-in-law, she's going to be the largest presence in my family - and she's making the same changes we are, she wants to be here with us and the kids, and it saddens me that the choices my mom and dad are making - are telling me they aren't. It makes me sad. It also hits home, that regardless of how MIL and I feel about each other, the two of us must bury the hatchet, b/c she's going to be here, where as my parents won't. I already feel that loss, even though they're not gone.

I'd love it if my mom and dad would willing take on changing their lifestyle like MIL is, if not for me then for Chaos and Havoc. As I look at the conversations I've had w/them over the past years - I realize now that they're already in the process of checking out... it makes me sad b/c I want to fight for them, but they won't fight for themselves, let alone me or the kids.

As much as it makes me sad, I've also realized that my tenacity and determination I have came from my parents, they're not going to change regardless of how much I try to educate them... so I have to let them go. They'll always be apart of my life but they're not present nor apart of where we're going.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-20T20:54:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76734>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=76699)
>
> This is challenging for me b/c in the past two years I've seen the impacts of bad diet, horrible sleep, and an unwillingness to change impact my mom. she turns 70 on 15 March. I've tried to engage her - and she's told me point blank that she's not changing a damn thing, she likes her life, and isn't going to "rock" the boat.
> When DH and I had our talk on Saturday night after Jack dropped the MOAB on him, I told him I don't think my mom will make it to 80. I actually think her mom will outlive her. And that's scary. She's on statins, she hasn't had her BHRT adjusted since she started it 20yrs ago. In the past two years - she's suddenly looking far older than her age. I realize now that her in ability to get good sleep is what's doing this. On average I bet she's probably sleeping maybe 3-4hrs/night maybe 3-4 nights out of the week, and sleeping less the rest of the time. The scary thing, is I'm not sure my dad will survive her passing - he's not really on speaking terms with one of his brothers, and his other brother is a total flake. What's crazy is I'm coming to the realization, that while I may not get along well with my Mother-in-law, she's going to be the largest presence in my family - and she's making the same changes we are, she wants to be here with us and the kids, and it saddens me that the choices my mom and dad are making - are telling me they aren't. It makes me sad. It also hits home, that regardless of how MIL and I feel about each other, the two of us must bury the hatchet, b/c she's going to be here, where as my parents won't. I already feel that loss, even though they're not gone.
>
> I'd love it if my mom and dad would willing take on changing their lifestyle like MIL is, if not for me then for Chaos and Havoc. As I look at the conversations I've had w/them over the past years - I realize now that they're already in the process of checking out... it makes me sad b/c I want to fight for them, but they won't fight for themselves, let alone me or the kids.
>
> As much as it makes me sad, I've also realized that my tenacity and determination I have came from my parents, they're not going to change regardless of how much I try to educate them... so I have to let them go. They'll always be apart of my life but they're not present nor apart of where we're going.

Another well-written response Gretchen. Yes, time to bury the hatchet with your MIL. Who knows, you may gain a fulfilling relationship from it. At least she is open to change.

They'll always be apart of my life but they're not present nor apart of where we're going.

For the sake of your own sanity, flip it around a bit - what if she were in a fatal accident and taken suddenly. I have had the unfortunate luck to have many people killed in my life and around me in sudden and tragic accidents. I think you know of this from your military experience, no? Once you realllllly get inside that life can just stop, and often does - it sure makes all those things you can't seem to change with other people pop into focus - as well as what you really want to be spending your time doing.

I'm someone that has always re-invented and because until recently I never had other people I cared to be around - picking up and moving was a major adventure and always a positive thing. If it's not, then why do it.

The other thing few will say is it really is OK to let go of someone without them actually getting hit by the bus. If they are not your tribe in any other way than name - you really can just walk away. Tribes can be made as well as born into.

Now, whar you going to? Did you hear, my little burb is the 2nd happiest place in the US!

[http://www.dailycamera.com/ci\_22623092/tweet-this-longmont-is-second-happiest-city?source=rss](http://www.dailycamera.com/ci_22623092/tweet-this-longmont-is-second-happiest-city?source=rss)

Come here! Several businesses that I bet are right up the employment ally (Major FAA center, digital globe, ibm, seagate) <---hehe I do see the irony of that.

You can have BOTH here, a connection to the world plus some nature. Plus, there would be an optimal playdate!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-21T02:13:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76792>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=76775)
>
> how we die is up to us.........

This is where you and I disagree. We are one very potent force in creating our health and/or illness. But we are only one force. There are others not under our control. And there always will be.

Illness does not equate with failure. Physical health does not equal success. In my opinion, it is arrogant to think one has all the answers and all those who do not follow what we believe are ignorant or foolish or stupid or deaf or in denial. Isn't this what that Ted talk video in EMF 6 is all about? Everyone is wrong about something(s). I cannot know what is best for anyone else. And feeling sorry for someone is not the same as caring for them. Your external perspective may be that they refuse help or are not going to bat for themselves, but you can never know what is in a person's heart or mind.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-21T04:36:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76820>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=76800)
>
> Respectfully,,,,,,,you have zero idea what I know. Best be ready. I know precisely how 90% of us will die unless they get the message soon........99.9% wont. That is something I already know by reading evolutionary texts. I can help them.......but I can help those who can hold an uncomfortable truth examine it.........tear into it and then realize.........they never saw this aspect of it at all. You can disagree........but on this one.......I am dead right.

I wasn't commenting on what you know. Of course I don't know what you know. I don't pretend to (hence why I read your blog). Nor was I trying to comment about whether you are right or wrong about a particular issue. I am ready to hear what you have to say. I have a good bit of experience holding uncomfortable truths.

I was trying to make the point that no one can know everything. It is ridiculous to think one person can. The learning and growing is in where we are wrong. But as that video pointed out, being wrong feels like being right until you realise it... hence we all have blind spots.

I was also trying to say that each of us has our own knowing, our own path, our own way of making decisions about our life. I was responding to this entire thread, not just your statement. No one can see another's heart fully. And even when another's choices do not match what we believe to be right, that does not mean their choice is the wrong one for them. I do not endorse a hierarchy, with the smartest people being the healthiest or those who survive when others perish. There are just too many other variables. You cannot know what they know. And even when people make choices that seem poor ones, I believe they deserve our compassion, not our pity.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-21T04:57:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76826>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=76634)
>
> ... this journey has been incredibly exciting and positive in so many ways and what did it cost us? some ice and a grocery budget spent on seafood instead of potato chips . We get to look at the sunrise and the sunset and the stars in the night sky. I love that I can't be bothered with TV. I love that it is more important to interact with endlessley interesting people whether it is family or friends or strangers. I love how I feel and I love the joy in my heart.

Caroline, I absolutely agree. I too have these thoughts and feelings. But talking about these things too turns people off. I asked a friend why she turned away when I talked about it. Her reply was that she was so jealous she couldn't cope. She was happy for me but she wasn't going to put herself out that much as she didn't really believe it would work for her as it worked for me. We still talk and share and she is now turning round so all is not lost. It was this love, the joy, the changed priorities, the pleasure that made me want more of what JK was suggesting.

KiwiLauren, I think you missed Jack's point....
I 100% know how we die is up to us. My grandmom died at over 90 yo a few months ago right around Christmas this winter. My aunt was there when she died too and told my dad later about it. Grandma had alzheimer for many years already, but still she was always a very happy person. She died so peacefully, it was just beautiful. But she had a sun shining in her heart always and that made all the difference even if she could have been healthy if she had knewn but she didn't.

Once she said to my dad hugging him as he visited her in the hospital.. Oh Willy.. you are so beautiful!!! Lol. And she barely remebered he was her son.. but she loved him dearly still..
The state of her mind was beautiful. And how she died was too... She loved so much. So even if her body and "mind" were not working well her heart was stronger. This is why I know, love matters and that is what makes all the difference. Love always seek the truth too, and it is not afraid to do..

Inger, I am not sure I understand that how we die is up to us. My grandma in Europe was almost 97 yrs old when she died of colon and uterine cancer. She was great until she was 96 yrs old. Then, they found her colon cancer. They operated on her when she was 96 yrs old because she was in such a great shape for her age, physically and mentally.

She was a very loving and quiet person, she lived in an isolated place with not much of technology at all. At the end, she suffered a lot and did not have a peaceful end at all. I think she did what she could but she died in pain at home.

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-21T22:48:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76931>

I think you may be talking about the same thing at different levels...yes we are all tied together in life and death on this planet, so big events that have no origin of our individual making. i.e. earthquakes, can be seen to have an origin in our collective making i.e. messing up the planet which logically involves us as individuals.

How an individual may face death or dying certainly can be within their control....but not one of us is able to stop an earthquake, so in that regard the deaths in New Zealand were not under their control. My mother chose to stop eating in order to die so her control was direct and absolute, but she didn't know exactly when her heart would stop...

How we face the end of our lives is often chosen for us by our lifestyles decisions...look at all the people with degenerative disorders who can't think or function well at all. Their ability to think well is gone but was taken away by choices they made throughout their lives (unconsciously or consciously). I think KL is reminding us that there is no place for judgement in this discussion and talking about people "choosing" how they die when life is ripped from them in an earthquake isn't useful. Talking about interconnectedness is.

So we have control and no control and perhaps that's the paradox we live in.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-22T07:36:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-76999>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=76922)
>
> maybe hard for you to accept but that is what the data says........it is also why your grandmother can tell when bad weather is coming. It blocks the Schumann.......bad behavior does the same thing.......it limits our spark of life........by generating scalar vectors that disrupt circadian signaling.

I never thought of knowing about the coming of rain before it starts as being due to a lack of something, Schumann or otherwise - all I know is that something changes and I have to get the washing in before the rain happens. I can be deep in a book, or cooking, or anything and all of a sudden I move - something has changed. I have to do things before the rain comes - washing in, other stuff outside put away or windows closed. But if that something I used to put down to change in pressure is the Schumann then I'm OK with that too.

My sister in intensive care found herself out of bed a good second before the old lady in the bed next to her died setting off the alarms. She had been asleep then felt a change - she put it down to a change in her breathing, but the good ex-nurse picked the change up before the alarms started. That was a change in life force. We can feel it. Sitting with a dead person is quite different from sitting by someone who is alive but asleep or in a coma.

But back to KLs point. I prefer to avoid the idea of equating individual responsibility to corporate responsibility. I think that sometimes s\_\_t happens. A mountaineer might choose to go up the mountains and that puts them at risk of being caught in an avalanche. I don't think they are personally responsible for choosing to die under the avalanche if they have taken reasonable care, any more than I don't think that the 8 month old baby chose to die at the hands of a drunk driver while asleep in her own cot in her room (the car ploughed through the wall), or that the people who died in Christchurch as a result of the earthquake.

I don't think the animals that lived under a volcano in prehuman times were responsible for the volcano exploding, nor now.

How far we can push human responsibilty is up for discussion - we are responsible for much more than we ever thought possible - that is now obvious, but volcanoes and earthquakes have been around longer than humans.

I just want to ask.. where does such a thinking brings me, when I think I cannot choose how I die.. I cannot choose how my life will be... it is not up to me...
It takes away my responsibility, my consciousness, the fact that every little thing I do or think has consequenses. small or big. But it has.

So I choose to not think like that. To me it equals stagnation.. death...

I see it around me all the time. It is heartbreaking, and I mean it for real. I cannot express it strong enough but it is in our thinking that I am sure of. IDK why and how like Jack knows, I know only partly, but it is sad for sure.

(about volkanoes and earthquakes.. or tornadoes.. we know today in what places those usually happends.. and we can choose to not live in such an area, or not in a big city where such things get really dangerous.. or we can choose to, and then we know the risks. Think a bit further and you will realize there is a lot more... it just depends how conscious you are..)

Maybe we all need to expand our consciousness BIG time. But it demands hard work now with all those EMFs around.. ha. But impossible? No.

### Dali Dula — 2013-02-25T17:08:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-77576>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=77574)
>
> Well, DD, that was exactly my point. I can't do anything but wait. And by the time they're ready, they're likely going to be dead.

We must not let our frustration turn to anger or impatience. I read a quote years ago from the Dalai Lama. When asked by an interviewer what the most important thing we can do he said, "Greet everyone with a smile." At the time I thought this was simplistic nonsense. Now that I have some understanding of oxytocin and it's mechanism I think he may be right. Bringing a smile to the table is a game changer. A smile creates an oxytocin response in both the smiler and the smiled upon. This creates receptivity. Preaching, warning and telling people what they should or shouldn't do creates resistance. Make yourself beautiful, smile onto it. That smile may save someone's life.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-25T17:14:10+0200

### Birdy — 2013-02-25T18:05:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-to-terms-with-dying-other-people-that-is.5955/post-77589>

I guess I don't have too many problems with friends and family. I am known as pretty out there anyway. Due to my success on a low carb diet, many friends and family members have gone either straight low carb or have been adding more saturated fats to their diet. Of course, my immediate family eats the way I cook, but so far, other than CT, my family, near and far, has not been willing to try any of the other suggestions. I do have some close relatives with some serious illnesses, and they are following the low-fat near-vegetarian approach. It isn't working, but I don't say anything anymore to them because it is too disruptive. But it is hard to read their blogs, beating themselves up for going off plan at a bbq, and then exercising like mad to get back on track. And unfortunately, after initial weight loss changes blood lipids, things go back to the current levels, and they are pressured by their docs to do more. Currently I do not share Kruse's recommendations with my friends. I think they are looking for more weight loss before they jump in. My immediate family has started going to the beach with me, but so far no serious dunking. I am hoping that some family members will take up some interest in the EMF series, in fact, I think they would be perfect in an emf-remediation business. I am also hoping that some sanity will be introduced/restored on the Kruse-ship and I can recommend reading again. Lately, this site has been my guilty pleasure/train-wreck and most people surrounding me have neither the time of the patience to sift through this.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-02-22T06:02:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-76989>

All I can say is, "Wow!!" I love it all. I wish I could channel my physicist Grandfather, whom, I am certain, would have loved reading this post. I am so fortunate to have been exposed to quantum physics as a kid, and to live where I live. I grew up as a minority living amongst native pueblo people, one of only a handful of white people. Karma is definitely a bitch. I think about the native kids I knew who were designated as "troubled" or as having "behavioral" issues. Autism had not yet become a diagnosis. Nor had OCD, or "Oppositional Defiance Disorder" or any of the modern day diagnoses. Yet, many of the native kids I knew were fully oppositional. They hated school, authority, rules, and punishment. The ones who were most troublesome ended up being sent to military school!! It wasn't until I was in my first year of college that I realized what the white "settlers" had done. The level of heartbreak I experienced was overwhelming. I can only repeat what Hope said, "Karma is a bitch."

Jack, kudos! I am astonished, amazed, dumbfounded, and alert with piqued interest. I cannot wait to hear what else you are going to say. The grounding thing resonated immediately. In fact, the whole grounding and CT thing right now has become obsessive with me. I got my hubby hooked, too, and he is out there, in the cold, going to our new spring-fed CT spot several times a week, despite the 1.5 mile hike in. I leave, with an owl who-who-whoing at me, and I can't wait to go back.

Thank you. EMF7 is phenomenal. Nothing like a caldera, shit tons of basalt, ash flows, mountains, snow, ice, and springs to make you feel good, right?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-25T20:47:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175684>

Its not crushing...........Its reality and there is a way out.

The full story of quantum prometheus is in Marino's book, Going Somewhere, read it. Then I want to leave you with A comment Becker wrote in the Body Electric.

"Medicine began as an art. The masterpiece—a cure—resulted from the patient's will combined with the physician's intuition and skill in using remedies culled from millennia of observant trial and error. In the last two centuries medicine more and more has come to be a science, or more accurately the application of one science, namely biochemistry. Medical techniques have come to be tested as much against current concepts in biochemistry as against their empirical results. Techniques that don't fit such chemical concepts—even if they seem to work—have been abandoned as pseudoscientific or downright fraudulent."
\- Robert O. Becker, MD

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-25T20:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175685>

My take: How should the art of medicine merge with the science? Medicine is more art than science but today they want to think it is based upon evidence. It is a fallacy because if you ask the wrong questions in your research you get the wrong results and you believe them and the paradigm freezes it in its dogma. Do you look "at science" or do you look thru science through a lens? I submit that that most of us are prisoners to modern science perspectives because we see it via "their lenses". I look at nature and turn it 90 degrees, 180 degrees, 270 degrees, and degrees in between. Every time I do this, my perspective of a problem changes and I learn a little bit more about what I missed from the basic perspective from which I was taught. I learned to do this from DaVinci and Michelangelo who were masters of altering the ground and foreground in their works. They changed sizes and shapes of proportions on their art to make the visualization of the observer more life like. They were masters at altering the lens that we see the world through to make it more accurate based upon where we were in space and time I view science in the same way. I need to alter the perception of what we currently believe so you can see the parts of science that are unobservable in action. Just because you can't or don't see it does not make it immaterial, a fallacy, or pseudoscience. On the contrary, it turns our what you can't see and do not know are the most important parts of science. Do you look at that lens and observe how it might bend reality? You should, because you'll see the future is not as dark as you think. ----- Dr. Jack Kruse DMD, MD

### Psyche — 2015-08-25T21:03:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-7-is-now-live-quantum-prometheus.5975/post-175693>

Thank you, Dr. Kruse. I am having this reaction because I feel like the situation IS hopeless, and I will take you on your word that it is not. Coincidentally, I ordered Going Somewhere a few days ago after reading the introduction and first chapter of it as a Kindle preview. Marino's website is also very good- especially the blog posts. His very first post is one I especially like:

Concept Not Precept

 [](http://andrewamarino.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2010/09/concept-precept.png)“Hard scientific evidence.” I’ve heard that phrase throughout my career, and I guess I’ve used it myself many times. In the beginning I had just naturally regarded “hard scientific evidence” as if it were more or less a definite thing. There was the Queen Mary, Central Park, the Mona Lisa, and “hard scientific evidence.” It had never occurred to me that recognizing “hard scientific evidence” would be any more difficult than recognizing the ship, the park, or the painting. Only much later did I realize that “hard scientific evidence” was a concept, not a precept. After that realization, the debate about health risks from environmental EMFs made much more sense.

Sooner or later, I think the principle will be accepted that knowledge regarding the link between EMFs and disease is imperfect, now and forever. Really it’s only common sense. The consequences of everything we do, or that is done to us, are imperfectly predictable, and the consequences of EMFs are no exception. “Hard scientific evidence” is not in the world, it is in the mind.

### Hope — 2013-02-21T20:44:30+0200

### nonchalant — 2013-02-21T23:13:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bottled-water-without-fluoride-at-costco.5976/post-76936>

As far as I know, the only filter that can remove fluoride is a reverse osmosis filter.
I stopped filtering my water. I buy bottled water, Kroger purified drinking water. But I'll check out the Costco brand.
I guess we can add some sea salt or minerals to our water...

Reminds me of when I lived in the Texas panhandle. Super hard water. I'd leaving a glass of it on the counter, wait for all the sediment to settle out, and then drink. Left the dregs of course. Then recently I heard that Deaf Smith county water was considered the best in Texas, which is right near where I used to live then.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-22T03:36:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bees-flowers-electric-fields.5983/post-76974>

I'd like to know why the honey bees I saw were consistently landing on the outside (stem side) of a group of flowers, instead of the inside where the nectar was. Poor things couldn't get to the nectar.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-24T20:57:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bees-flowers-electric-fields.5983/post-77479>

My early peach tree is blooming now, and it is ... well, not covered with bees, but there are quite a few buzzing around! Nice to see in the big city.
The bees aren't honey bees, however. Small wild ones, like I see in the country. Nice to see their numbers growing. They should have plenty of blooms to choose from, due to lack of competition.

### kathylu — 2013-02-24T21:11:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bees-flowers-electric-fields.5983/post-77483>

Yup. Last year I had to hand pollinate (with a small artist's brush) my garden veggie plants because there were so few bees around. Seems EMF is a big contender for the cause of CCD (colony collapse disorder) too.

[http://www.electromagneticman.co.uk/index.php/news/115-bees-manmade-emf-a-colony-collapse-disorder](http://www.electromagneticman.co.uk/index.php/news/115-bees-manmade-emf-a-colony-collapse-disorder)

### nonchalant — 2013-02-24T21:26:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bees-flowers-electric-fields.5983/post-77484>

The wild bees must have advantages over the honey bees, because they are living in the same EMF areas. Natural hives connected to the ground or trees maybe their advantage. And they are cold tolerant, unlike their cousins.

# Thread: HELP! I'm a mess!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HeidiHen — 2013-02-22T15:40:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/post-77023>

Ok. So, what gives? I'm up 10 pounds, yes TEN pounds since beginning the BAB and CT in January. The scale just continues to climb. Another two pounds this week.

Right now, since eating very low carb (under 25) and high fat (60-80% of cals) for almost two years (no dairy, no potatoes, no nuts, lots of coconut oil and butter), my weight and moods are my biggest complaint. I have only managed to gain weight over the past 2 years. I'm 50+ pounds overweight and am edgy and irritable quite often. I'm doing CT as much as possible (with 4 kiddos that's only about every other day) for 40-60 minutes at about 55-60 degree water, turning off wi-fi and cell phones as much as possible, burning candles and wearing amber glasses in the evening, going to bed early (8-ish wonderful hours of sleep), and eating a BAB. I've started taking magnesium, ubiquinol, fish oil and D. I got my basic labs back and will schedule a consult for hopefully next week. In the meantime, what on earth more can I do? Soooooo frustrated!

Labs:
Triglycerides: 40
HDL: 120
LDL: 138
DHEA-sulfate: 210 (range 60.9-337.0)
Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy: 44 (range 30.0-100.0)
hsCRP: 1.24 (range 0.00-3.00)

Anyone have any suggestions they can offer???

### ashryn — 2013-02-22T16:49:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/post-77027>

I wish I could help.

### HeidiHen — 2013-02-22T17:21:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/post-77034>

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-22T17:26:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/post-77036>

The answers are all here..........you need to realize issue one and two are pulsed EMF exposure and artificial light........start from there use CT daily, drink lots of non fluoridated water and eat Epi-Paleo Rx.

### HeidiHen — 2013-02-22T17:44:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-a-mess.5992/post-77044>

### BJK77 — 2013-02-22T20:58:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-am-i-becoming.5998/post-77092>

As I'm sitting here having a picnic on my concrete basement floor, I'm pondering who I am these days . . . I'm not sure I know 'me' anymore. Anyone else feel this way? I don't mean this in a negative way and am certainly not insinuating any of this is wrong, but I just:

-   had a picnic on my basement floor (as mentioned) half naked so I could get all those free electrons
-   with a dozen oysters
-   half pound of sashimi
-   fermented daikon radish with ginger
-   wasn't full, so I topped it all with a bit of liver
-   and finished with some kombucha

All the while:

-   reading about EMF and figuring out the easiest way to tell the rest of the family we're cutting out wifi
-   trying to plan for some big future expenses: converting pool to saltwater, a floatation tank, magnetic mattress pads, a tanning booth, etc, etc.
-   trying to figure out how I can afford all of the above when I eventually quit my job since I am pretty much sold on the idea now that the office is full of EMF that is keeping me sick!
-   and fashioning tinfoil hats for DH and I since we're both working from home today and, for now, are still using the wifi
-   also trying to work up a schedule for turning off the breakers more than just overnight

And yet, somehow, I find myself oddly at peace with all of this! I'm feeling so peaceful, in fact, that I don't understand the arguing that seems to be occurring on the forum suddenly nor do I believe in engaging in any of it though I may have previously. My friends and family, I'm sure, would tell me I've truly lost my mind this time, but this feels very right. In my past life I'd be perusing Facebook, going out to lunch with friends, shopping online for the kids, all kinds of pointless activities. I love that my priority now is on keeping my family as healthy as possible and everything else is so trivial.

Forget the fact that our food bills are now on par with our mortgage and any extra spending money we used to enjoy is pretty much nonexistent because none of that matters if we aren't here to enjoy one another . . . this is my new mantra.

The only downside, if there is one, is that I find I can no longer relate to others very well. I've never been one to follow reality TV, or much TV at all, and since that is the hot water cooler topic I haven't "fit in" at the office for a long time now anyway, but things are just so much different now. It goes beyond "fitting in."

Off to finish those tinfoil hats now . . . just felt the need to share with _someone_ how strange I've become

And we spend on supplements what others do on I don't know...fancy car payments? Nice clothes? Fancy overnight hotel trips and restaurants?

I am enduring people laughing at me in our ballroom dance class or monthly get-togethers with friends when I show up in the evening in my yellow sunglasses (over my regular glasses - I look AWESOME in that outfit!) and making silly comments....but I know who will have the last laugh...or giving me funny looks in an airplane when I eat a smelly can of sardines and a bit of sauerkraut or some other homemade food in a container instead of the junk meals they provide....or leaving the tap water glass untouched in a restaurant...but I don't care.

I have been bummed about EMF for years and trying to get my DH to get the significance of it but he doesn't care, still...though when I told him that the "MD I trust so much with all this stuff" is able to base a lot of his theories on Einstein (whom he also admires!!) and E=mc2 he was listening up a bit so I am dreaming some day I'll get him to read the EMF blog series. Woohoo and way to go!!!!

# Thread: Prometheus

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### Inger — 2013-02-22T21:50:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prometheus.6001/post-77102>

Jacks last blog, quantum Prometheus.. I had to check who Prometheus was again..

> Prometheus (Greek: Προμηθεύς) is a Titan, culture hero, and trickster figure who, in Greek mythology, is credited with the creation of man from clay and the theft of fire for human use, an act that enabled progress and civilization. He is known for his intelligence, and as a champion of humanity.\[1\]
>
> The punishment of Prometheus as a consequence of the theft is a major theme of his mythology, and is a popular subject of both ancient and modern art. Zeus, king of the Olympian gods, sentenced the Titan to eternal torment for his transgression. The immortal Prometheus was bound to a rock, where each day an eagle, the emblem of Zeus, was sent to feed on his liver, only to have it grow back to be eaten again the next day. In some stories, Prometheus is freed at last by the hero Heracles (Hercules).
>
> In another of his myths, Prometheus establishes the form of animal sacrifice practiced in ancient Greek religion. Evidence of a cult to Prometheus himself is not widespread. He was a focus of religious activity mainly at Athens, where he was linked to Athena and Hephaestus, other Greek deities of creative skills and technology.\[2\]
>
> **In the Western classical tradition, Prometheus became a figure who represented human striving, particularly the quest for scientific knowledge, and the risk of overreaching or unintended consequences. In particular, he was regarded in the Romantic era as embodying the lone genius whose efforts to improve human existence could also result in tragedy**: Mary Shelley, for instance, gave The Modern Prometheus as the subtitle to her novel Frankenstein (1818).

[http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prometheus](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prometheus)

For whatever it's worth, my family and I thank you for giving us this knowledge. Yes, it is inconvenient and we may wish it weren't true, but as you've said many times science doesn't lie. I can't say I fully 100% understand all of the science yet, but the more I dig the more it all just intuitively makes sense and I begin to embrace this information and become more determined to use it to protect my family. As I said in an earlier post, I have a deep sense of peace and feel so excited about the prospect of getting back to a simpler, quiet life where I appreciate all this world has to {naturally} offer me.

### Glamazon — 2013-02-23T00:48:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prometheus.6001/post-77148>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=77106)
>
> You know Hope.. I was kind of thinking Jack might have ment Prometheus=EMFs....(...**whose efforts to improve human existence could also result in tragedy**)
>
> But yes I see what you see too..

Inger, there is a law out there that just makes me laugh in a bittersweet sort of way. It is called the law of Revenge Effects or the Law of Unintended Consequences. An obvious example: In Brazil, there were a lot of robberies of people at their ATM machines when they were withdrawing money. To be brief, Brazil began requiring that a finger on a pad to read the fingerprint be required to withdraw money. Security professionals thought that would stop the robberies. Not really because the criminals would just cut off the finger.

This article discusses more examples.
[
http://faculty.css.edu/dswenson/web/REVENGE.HTM](http://faculty.css.edu/dswenson/web/REVENGE.HTM)

I think that could be a promethean effect too.

No, do not stop Jack. I love what Hope said. And me and my family are benefitting so much from you!!! I am telling them all I read and I told them about EMF7 too and they all love it!!! I tell at work too...lol

As we learn, I guess people's real face (or state of mind) starts showing. On Internet it is alwasy not easy to see who are the person behind. What are their intentions?
The more light the more we see (you bring on a pretty intense flashlight Jack!). It might not always be pretty what comes to the surface then. I guess I need to concentrate on the light only. Even if Cavemam thinks I am a woodland nympf...lol

@ Glamazon,
thank you for telling me about the promethean law! Always great to learn new stuff!

# Thread: When trees die, so do people - interesting study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-trees-die-so-do-people-interesting-study.6002/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-02-22T21:59:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-trees-die-so-do-people-interesting-study.6002/post-77104>

Came across this...fascinating study on the effect of nature on man, and how a tree-killing bug may also affect people near it....

[http://donovan.hnri.info/Studies/donovan\_et\_al.EAB.pdf](http://donovan.hnri.info/Studies/donovan_et_al.EAB.pdf)

Specifically, the natural environ- ment has been shown to decrease stress,9 increase physical activity,10 and improve air quality.11 In turn, stress,12,13 lack of physical activity,14,15 and poor air quality16 have been linked with cardiovascular and lower- respiratory-tract disease.
The pioneering work in the fıeld by Ulrich6 found that patients recovering from gall bladderremoval surgery in a room with a view of a natural scene recovered faster and took fewer pain medications than patients in a room with a view of a brick wall.

In Holland, Maas et al.19 reported a positive association between greenness and self-reported health. In a later study, Maas et al.20 analyzed the health records of 345,000 people. They found that those living in greener areas were less likely to be diagnosed with 15 of the 24 health outcomes examined. Results were particularly strong for anxiety and depression and for children and those with lower SES. Park and colleagues9 showed that walking in a forest reduced heart rate and cortisol levels. Finally, in New York City, Lovasi et al.21 found that children who lived in areas with more street
trees were less likely to have asthma.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-22T23:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-trees-die-so-do-people-interesting-study.6002/post-77123>

considering trees give us O2 it makes total sense......its non locality in real time.

# Thread: Get Outside
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2013-02-23T18:47:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77279>

A few months ago, someone asked me where am I happiest. I thought for a bit, and realized that almost every time I feel alive and have that special feeling in my heart, I'm outside.

I know from reading here now that there are so many benefits to being outside, and they keep increasing with many of the blogs:
1) CT - from snow, water
2) Grounding - on the ground, in the water (especially ocean)
3) Vitamin D from the sun
4) photosynthesis from the sun (have I got this right? The benefit of the protons from the sun)

I still feel great outside, though, with my shoes on and on cloudy days. Of course the sun can sometimes make my heart sing (and I always comment, which makes my partner laugh. He says I should be a weather girl LOL). What benefit are we getting from cloudy/rainy days?

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-23T20:43:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77311>

I couldn't agree more about being outside, Miss Weather Girl! I love my walks on the beach and think I need a pair of grounding shoes or sandals to ground while I hike as I love the movement of hiking, being out in nature, and the scenery makes me smile.

Rainy days, in a place where it rarely rains, clean and freshen the air. This is just my opinion, although I think there is some science to back up this belief. It always looks better and the air feels cleaner after a nice rain. I find I want to be outside doing just about anything the day after a nice rain.

### caroline — 2013-02-24T01:45:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77358>

I love being outside as much as possible - I think it changes your whole attitude towards everything. It makes me much more mellow ..how life is meant to be lived......

### BJK77 — 2013-02-24T14:48:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77406>

Definitely agree with you all. I've been making it a priority to get outside more often lately and I'm remembering how it always made me feel so much more alive as a child. I wish I could say I was always earthing when I was out, but I still think there's something to be said for just breathing in the fresh air and appreciating the beauty around us.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-24T16:15:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77416>

Undoubtedly. Being indoors crushes my soul.

### PrimalPam — 2013-02-24T17:28:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77427>

Many years ago, I changed careers. I was inside an office with zero windows and lots of computers, and I switched from job to job, each more miserable than the last. I switched to an outside sales job; I actually earned 75% LESS at my first one than in my cubicle-world one, but I was 1000 times happier from Day 1. I eventually switched to different companies/sales jobs to get my earnings back up (gotta pay the bills) and have never looked back. Every single day, I'm outside as I go in and out of businesses. In the summer, I wear a lot of short-sleeve dresses without hose, and just spending time outside getting stuff out of my trunk to bring in, I get Vitamin D.

At that first outside sales job, I didn't understand why I was so much happier; I just knew on the first day that I had made the right decision. It just felt so...good...to be out and about, in and out of the sun and the elements. I'm in Houston, where it's a million degrees in the summer, and I just don't care. Or it's pouring rain, and I think it's lovely. Ever since making the decision to move my career to more time spent outdoors during the day, I'm just so much happier.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-24T18:21:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77447>

I've been trying to figure out a new path for myself, Pam. I think one of the keys is to avoid debt so that we are more flexible, financially. DH is wanting to buy a new vehicle, but I am adamant against it. We are now looking for a used vehicle that we can pay cash for.

All the outside jobs seem to pay less, and that's fine. I just have to juggle things around to swing it. Like using grounding, sunshine, darkness, cold, seafood and good water, instead of continually buying this expensive ubiquinol and other supplements.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-24T18:26:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-outside.6016/post-77450>

water and sun and CT.........critical

# Thread: EMF and Nueroplasticity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-nueroplasticity.6017/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mike NYC — 2013-02-23T18:54:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-nueroplasticity.6017/post-77280>

So I'm about halfway through EMF 7, just re-read the food electrons blog, and I need to take a break and do some other work before I spend my whole weekend reading, but I have so many thoughts going on in my mind that I feel like I need to share.

Since EMF 5 & 6, specifically the paper and the video showing how EMF radiation changes glucose metabolism in neurons and how dark neurons developed in mice after EMF exposures, I have been wondering about neuroplasticity, specifically metaplasticity as in the work of Jeffrey Kleim. It seems to me that the more plastic we can keep our brains and the more plastic and flexible we are able to maintain the ability of our brains to continually rewire quickly and efficiently will help us to continue optimal function using non-damaged neurons to create connections that take over for connections involving a damaged one. In this way it seems that the better our cells can signal with one another, the better they can modulate that signal as needed as well. Perhaps they may even be able to signal in networks that have some sort of quantum quality where parts of the network can be effectively "off" and "on" at the same time? That last part is a bit of a random thought, and I don't have a great handle on quantum theory, but I'm just thinking out loud.

It does seem to me that the better we are at expressing this neuro and meta-plasticity , the better we will be able to navigate the problems modern life throws our way at the neurological and cellular level. This is why continued learning, novel movement, and providing the brain and body proper fuel and activation are essential for life.

If chronic pain can both create cortical plastic changes and can also alter DNA expression; but through movement, mirror box therapies, and other works of Ramachandran and Moseley, we can create cortical plastic change in the other direction that improves the pain, that would change DNA as well. Furthermore, thought, especially conscious critical thinking against the grain seems to require plasticity and an ability to change (dopamine). I think I am really seeing how true that "a thought can change your DNA" quote really is.

It also seems to me that environmental/evolutionary mismatch is a type of sensory mismatch (analogous to Visual Vestibular Proprioception integration problems), then EV mismatch is also likely a chronic nociceptive stimulus to the nervous system and the body.... however it may not have the amplitude to cause an acute pain experience and it takes time to develop into neolithic disease conditions.

Then I saw this post about quantum entanglement - [http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?5786-Quantum-Entanglement](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?5786-Quantum-Entanglement)
That to me says something of the power of this metaplasticity expanded to a society, and type of uber-plasticity maybe, and the role of the starfish in being the change you hope to see in others... and the more people that make positive changes, the faster that change can spread. For a while the EMF series was making me rather sad when I thought about the future, but looking at it this way I see the potential for it to be bright. Though the path is not without challenges.

One thing I do know is that whenever I work with someone as a training client, after asking what their goals are and finding ways I can help them to achieve them, I always ask myself how what I am doing with them is affecting their brain and how their brain is affecting their movement/workout and the change they desire to make. Over the last 3 years or so, neuroscience and neurology has become a huge part of how I have approached physical training, and what I have learned on this blog over the last 1.5 years have had an incredible impact on how I view all of that.

Some cool links that helped these thoughts come together:
[http://charbonniers.org/2013/02/16/the-thalamus-as-conductor/](http://charbonniers.org/2013/02/16/the-thalamus-as-conductor/) - mainly as an idea of how quickly (and possibly temporarily?) brain connections can change, be rerouted, and differ.
[http://www.mcgill.ca/channels/news/chronic-pain-alters-dna-marking-brain-225012](http://www.mcgill.ca/channels/news/chronic-pain-alters-dna-marking-brain-225012)
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15326245](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15326245)

As a side note I found this last month writing a small article about how structure creates function in movement for a course I am doing (EMF2 made me look for a space example I could use). Its about bone loss in space in spite of exercise using resistance training and simulated "gravity" provided by bungee cords on a traedmill. Key point - the cosmonauts lost more bone and lean tissue than the "control" group which was patients on 4 months of bed rest on earth with no exercise! [http://www.ismni.org/jmni/pdf/2/leblanc.pdf](http://www.ismni.org/jmni/pdf/2/leblanc.pdf)

This has been a bit of a ramble, and I may not be correct in my understanding of everything, but that it how it is appearing to me at the moment.
Anyway, signing off as I need to do some work so I can get back to EMF 7 and checking out Jack's book.... if I didn't get these thoughts out of my head I never would have been able to focus on other stuff....
Have a great day everyone!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-23T20:02:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-nueroplasticity.6017/post-77307>

Mike you are spot on the money with it all.......that makes me happy because the message is getting thru to some starfish........awesome.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-02-23T20:05:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-and-nueroplasticity.6017/post-77308>

Most people can not comprehend how something on one side of the planet can affect the biology of someone else......it does and has forever. We are just realizing just how coherent life is. It is coherent with cells, Earth quake faults, Volcanos, weather, and tsunami, solar and lunar cycles.......and your partner. Nothing is not connected. When things go awry world wide we see ripples when we look........just like when you stub your toe we see a ripple in you post central gyrus that shows the pain and sends an perineural injury current to fix it.

### Mike NYC — 2013-02-24T00:15:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/search-for-antennas-in-your-area.6023/post-77347>

I have 28 wifi networks in my apartment right now....... and almost all have at least a 60% or higher signal. I'm excited to see the difference when I move. Conveniently my lease is almost up and it was getting to expensive here anyway.
Given all of that I'm a bit surprised that the labs I had done over the summer weren't worse (though I know they were not ideal) especially after 3+ years here at the time. T was 934, DHEA-S 269, HS-CRP 0.2, vit D 55
I expect those are better now because I am sleeping a lot better and I feel better and have much better energy and cognition. I need to get them done again, maybe when I move and then again a few months later to re-assess.

# Thread: Louisiana style shrimp
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/louisiana-style-shrimp.6036/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2013-02-25T04:17:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/louisiana-style-shrimp.6036/post-77520>

I found this recipe on the bag of shrimp I had thawed for dinner. It is BEYOND yummy:

Louisiana Style Shrimp

JUMBO SHRIMP 21/25 COUNT

20-22 shrimp, thawed, peeled and patted dry
4 TBS Butter
4 TBS Olive Oil
2 TBS + 2tsp Worcestershire sauce
1 TBS hot pepper sauce
Â¼ - Â½ tsp cayenne pepper
1 TBS minced garlic
1.5 TBS chopped fresh rosemary or 2 tsp dry
2 TBS chopped fresh parsley or 1 TBS dry
1 tsp dry mustard.

Preheat oven to 375Â°
Put butter & olive oil into a 9 x 13 inch pan. Put into ove until butter melts.

Remove from oven and stir in remaining ingredients making sure the bottom of the pan is even coated with the sauce. Add shrimp, turning to coat both sides Marinate at room temp for 15 minutes.

Bake uncovered for 14-18 minutes until shrimp turn pink.

The recipe called for leaving the shrimp unpeeled but it was an oily mess to try and do it after so I advise peeling them first.

### caroline — 2013-02-25T08:41:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/louisiana-style-shrimp.6036/post-77527>

# Thread: Longevity of cold-exposed rats, and cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AndrÃ©s — 2013-02-25T15:02:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-77559>

I know [AKMan has already pointed out](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?373-Does-Minimum-Effective-Dose-apply-to-Cold-Adaptation&p=14760&viewfull=1#post14760) [some study about air-CT effects on longevity of mice (none)](http://cbn.eldoc.ub.rug.nl/FILES/root/2009/PhysBichZoolVaanholt/2009PhysiolBiochemZoolVaanholt.pdf). I haven't been able to find mention around here of this other study using water-CT on rats giving rise to an non-statistical significant (I think) increase of almost 5% in mean longevity: [_Longevity of cold-exposed rats: a reevaluation of the "rate-of-living theory" by J. O. Holloszy and E. K. Smith_](http://jap.physiology.org/content/61/5/1656.short). Moreover, also from the abstract:

> The cold exposure appeared to protect against neoplasia, particularly sarcomas; only 24% of the necropsied cold-exposed rats had malignancies compared with 57% for the controls.

### AndrÃ©s — 2013-02-28T16:49:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-78308>

> [Anna K. said:](/goto/post?id=77677)
>
> There is lots of documentation about them, about how healthy they were, but nothing about them living unusually long above 100.

Actually I'm focused on the ["Live long and drop dead"](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/compression-of-morbidity/#axzz2MCgu7sdg) side , so I don't need anything more than the meager 5% average to keep me going.

> [Anna K. said:](/goto/post?id=77677)
>
> Andres and Jesse, do you still do CT and if yes, how often?

I keep driving with full-blast-towards-me AC on whenever alone in the car (something a little short of an hour a day) wearing a T-shirt (everywhere I go), even at -1Â°C. I have developed a chilblain these _freezing_ last days on my finger nearest to the vent, though. I will use gloves beginning this evening on the colder days.

I keep wearing [my homemade ICE-vest](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?5263-DIY-ice-vest-2-0) two or three times a week for an hour and a half.

I used to put on the electrical radiator in the morning for something like a quarter hour before taking my cold shower. Nowadays I open the window and get myself naked first thing entering the bathroom.

I keep my office heating off and window open all day long.

I have passed two colds this fall/winter. [The first one I have already commented on](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?4378-CT-vitamin-C-and-hibernation). On the second one I actually lowered my vitamin C intake to 2g/day on purpose while increasing the ICE-vest sessions to daily for three days. It certainly kept the cold on check, but finally I got rid of it by mixing again CT and vitamin C saturation.

Best,

AndrÃ©s

### hellojtm — 2013-03-20T19:38:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-81202>

> [Anna K. said:](/goto/post?id=77677)
>
> Andres and Jesse, do you still do CT and if yes, how often? thanks.

Hi Anna

I still CT seasonally. My cold season is a self-induced jan-April. I just do random exposures. Lots of 10 minute cold showers. Lesser 30 minute cold baths. These are both between 48-52f. I do the ice vest a couple times a week if freezer space is available (broth takes a lot of space  )

Outside of those four months I still do it for fun to see how my tolerance changes. Well it's only been about 16 months since the first time so.... I can't make any great claims except subjective ones. Subjectively I recover from workouts and sports faster. And also I get a lot leaner, though this year I purposely ate more to prevent weight loss.

### mila — 2013-03-28T18:49:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-82064>

I guess I'm going to have to be the asshole here. While I agree that CT is likely a good idea, we are not rats and what happens in rats may not happen in humans. It is simply an indicator of something interesting that should be further investigated--not FACT with respect to humans. _in vivo_ human testing is more meaningful and more accurate than rat studies. When communities constantly point to rat studies as the basis for their scientific "facts" with respect to humans, the community loses credibility. Is no one else concerned about how the paleo community is presented to the rest of the world? I am. I would like us to be taken seriously and we never will if we keep pushing these kinds of studies.

### endless — 2013-03-28T20:11:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-82072>

> I was wondering if using the A/C blasting while driving would help with the car EMF exposure or would convertible top down for more photons be better?

Well, when I tested my car with the Trimeter, blasting anything caused a huge jump in EMF. The fan was the culprit. I try to avoid using it, and will roll my windows down instead whenever possible.

Inger, I don't think they had osteoporosis until after the introduction of processed foods....but I could be mistaken.

### AndrÃ©s — 2013-04-25T18:16:37+0300

> [mila said:](/goto/post?id=82064)

Of course, but it seems that all that CT is about is activating the Eutherian program still active on everyone of us Eutherians.

> [mila said:](/goto/post?id=82064)
>
> It is simply an indicator of something interesting that should be further investigated--not FACT with respect to humans. _in vivo_ human testing is more meaningful and more accurate than rat studies.

Of course. But I don't have the patience to wait for them. I don't have the patience to wait for intravenous or liposomal-encapsulated vitamin C random controlled trials to begin. I don't have the patience to wait for the optimal level of 25-OH-vitamin-D to be elucidated. I don't have the patience to wait for the poison status of milligram doses of iodine to be revoked.

> [mila said:](/goto/post?id=82064)
>
> When communities constantly point to rat studies as the basis for their scientific "facts" with respect to humans, the community loses credibility.

Well, I am not a community. I consider that experiments about diets on rats are near useless (Petro Dobromylskyj notwithstanding). I consider that experiments about Eutherian cold exposure adaptation on rats are very informative.

> [mila said:](/goto/post?id=82064)
>
> Is no one else concerned about how the paleo community is presented to the rest of the world? I am. I would like us to be taken seriously and we never will if we keep pushing these kinds of studies.

Ok! So let it be clear from now on: I am not paleo. I still drink orange juice and white coffee at breakfast. I gorged on hard cheeses.

I couldn't care less about the paleo movement, if there is such a thing. There are some of its proponents that I respect and some of them that I don't.

I take those contributions that make sense to me (epi-paleo, CT, Schumann resonance / grounding?: I will go swim at the ocean more often just in case) and I am skeptical of the rest (EMF, structured water outside the cell, gaining/loosing electrons and [standard Quantum Mechanics in general](http://www.optimalhealthcave.com/viewtopic.php?f=39&t=1774&start=50#p40788)). Everyone should do the same.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-04-26T16:33:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/longevity-of-cold-exposed-rats-and-cancer.6041/post-84881>

> [mila said:](/goto/post?id=82064)
>
> I guess I'm going to have to be the asshole here. While I agree that CT is likely a good idea, we are not rats and what happens in rats may not happen in humans. It is simply an indicator of something interesting that should be further investigated--not FACT with respect to humans. _in vivo_ human testing is more meaningful and more accurate than rat studies. When communities constantly point to rat studies as the basis for their scientific "facts" with respect to humans, the community loses credibility. Is no one else concerned about how the paleo community is presented to the rest of the world? I am. I would like us to be taken seriously and we never will if we keep pushing these kinds of studies.

Then you better talk to Stephen Guyenet who is in love with rat studies.

# Thread: To Eat the BAB or not....that is my question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Reset 2013
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Claudia — 2013-02-25T20:01:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77602>

So, I'm wondering, would Jack, say eat the breakfast even if you're not hungry or is it ok to wait until I am hungry? Also, if my cortisol is on the lower end in the morning for a morning cortisol, and should be higher, does that also mean maybe I shouldn't be eating the BAB then.

Before Optimal Reset, I've been practicing intuitive eating or eating only when hungry and only until satisfied. I find this at odds with the BAB. I'm just not hungry. Wouldn't it be counterproductive to eat when leptin levels are high and I am not hungry? At the same time, I hear this voice very morning, eat breakfast.....

My hormones need balancing, my t3 is low, and I"m taking t3 only (low dose of 12.5 mcg per day), and I have some adrenal issues, too. I had a recent salivary test of adrenocortex stress profile. Morning is 11.3 (range is 6.0-42.0 nmol/L), noon is 9.2 (2.0-11.0), 4 pm is 9.1 (2.0-11.0), evening is 2.9 (1.0-8.0), cortisol daily, total 32.5 (11.0-76.0), DHEAS profile morning 13.9 (2.5-25.0 nmol/L) and DHEAS/CORTISOL AM is 1.23 HIGH (0.20-0.60 RATIO).

I did the Leptin reset already back in Nov. 11 to January 12, had all the signs, and stopped doing.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-25T20:11:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77605>

I just posted on your other thread at the same time you were posting this! I will say I did the same thing with the Leptin Rx. Started in Jan '12 and ended in March because I did have all the signs or what to me seemed like all the signs. Again i think this is a contextual thing where some signs are more applicable to some and not as much to others. I felt better all around but didn't have the energy. Having Hashis I wondered if I would ever get that back.

After two months of HAB, I decided to go back to BAB and felt a vast improvement in my energy and overall well-being. There are definitely days I don't want to eat in the morning but I force myself and after a couple of bites I'm all in! It's become quite easy for me with my BAB. I have a burger every morning (or some grass-fed beef) since I stopped eating eggs as I wasn't sure if they bothered me or not. But it has become part of my overall morning routine and it seems really strange on those blood draw days when I can't complete my BAB process until later. So moral of the story is to go back and reread the Leptin Rx blogs and maybe think about revisiting the BAB. Then again maybe it's EMF which I don't understand yet so cannot comment there!

### curves64 — 2013-02-25T22:00:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77617>

Hehe, I posted a similar question in the Ask Jack section. It has been my recent experience that there is nothing like the BAB. I ate the BAB last April-June, felt all the clinical signs as Jack outlines, and then went to about 30 grams of protein in the am. When I returned to the BAB again 10 days ago, the results were startling. I lost all cravings again, went down to two meals a day easy, and started to lose weight for the first time since hearing about the Leptin Rx last April. My body comp is even changing. I feel fantastic. Better than ever. At this point, I don't know if I'm ever going to give up the BAB -- I'm on a roll!

### curves64 — 2013-02-25T22:09:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77619>

I should also say that this is in conjunction with about 8 straight weeks of earthing and CTing in the Pacific for about 35 minutes 6 days a week. I dry brush my skin and play in the waves and have been progressively getting more and more of my body into the ocean. It wasn't the cold but the craziness of the surf that held me back. It's hilarious because now I squat down in the shallow water while keeping my feet on the ground. Sometimes a group forms behind me to watch what I'm doing because no one plays in the ocean there without a wet suit usually. Anyway, my point in bring it up is that, by reintroducing the BAB at this juncture, I reached new levels of feeling really really good.

### Claudia — 2013-02-25T22:40:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77627>

Really? Thanks to both of you. Curves64, Did Jack tell you to go back to BAB? I remember him telling me if I had all the signs, not to do it anymore. That if I wasn't hungry, I shouldn't force it. But that was while ago, actually about a year ago. Now with these new adrenal stress profile results, I'm wondering about it again. Actually, I'm thinking that if we have normal high cortisol in the morning that it goes better with the BAB, while low cortisol not so much.

### curves64 — 2013-02-26T01:00:39+0200

Yes. I distinctively and absolutely feel better with 50 or more grams of protein. I thought it would be the same eating 35 grams but eating 50 makes a noticeable difference. It varies but this morning I ate 5 slices of bacon and 4 eggs with fried salami and coconut oil in my coffee. At 35 grams I still had a large appetite. In contrast after eating 50 grams this morning at 9:00 am, I'm not the least bit hungry at 3:00 pm and my energy is great. I had no idea until recently how big a difference those 15 grams of protein would make.

### BJK77 — 2013-02-26T01:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-eat-the-bab-or-not-that-is-my-question.6047/post-77658>
