### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-12T03:37:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sleep-ever.6237/post-80138>

When you eat a ketogenic diet guess what else happens.......we bypass complex one at the inner mitochondrial membrane and enter FADH2. You heard about this in the Quantum electron post.........but what did not I tell you? Why does a ketogenic diet work in T1D, T2D, and to lose weight? When you lose water conduction at the inner mitochondrial membrane you lose the ability of proper nanoscopic protein folding of cytochrome 1. This throws off the nanoscopic precision required for quantum tunneling and this cause increased ROS and metabolic syndrome........This is also why niacin works in these diseases because it is a ketone mimic drug. Why? Niacin also known as vitamin B3, nicotinic acid and vitamin PP, is an organic compound with the formula C6H5NO2 and one of the 40 to 80 essential human nutrients. In fact, when niacin deficiency is present it is one of 5 vitamins that causes a pandemic disease condition called pellagra. But it can be used to bypass a "broken" cytochrome 1 when mis-folding (due to a chronic ATP deficiency think EMF 7). But why kathylu? Why am I tormenting you with this science,.......because you are biochemist........and you will see where QED takes us..........Vitamin B3 can help activate PPP when we are broken for some reason when we are ketotic from a dietary stand point using vast amounts of coherent water as I laid out in the quantum electron blog post. ( ) Yes, kathylu, niacin is a precursor to NAD+/NADH (cytochrome 1) and NADP+/NADPH (the magic of the PPP), which play essential metabolic roles in all living cells as I showed in EMF 4. Niacin cannot be directly converted to nicotinamide, but both compounds could be converted to NAD and NADP in vivo and this information is very valuable when you have liver leptin resistance, metabolic syndrome, or epilepsy. When cytochrome 1 has mis-folded electron transport proteins (quinolone issue of CoEnQ10) you need to bypass it 100% of the time by eating fats predominately because if you don't, your mitochondria begin to make massive ROS that overwhelms the cell lowers vitamin D and all hormones, causes pregnenolone steal syndrome, and eventually shortens its telomeres to cause cellular signaling problems that lead to either senesence or cancer. The reason why? NAD+ builds up in relation to NADH levels on a per unit basis. Increasing the NAD+ in comparison to NADH changes a most of the mechanistic downstream signalling in protein kinase B pathways of cells. Why is that big? The AKT pathway is also known as protein kinase B (PKB). It is a serine/threonine-specific protein kinase that plays a key role in multiple cellular processes such as **glucose metabolism, apoptosis, cell proliferation, transcription and cell migration.** Akt1 is involved in cellular survival pathways, by inhibiting apoptotic processes. Akt1 is also able to induce protein synthesis pathways, and is therefore a key signaling protein in the cellular pathways that lead to skeletal muscle hypertrophy, and general tissue growth. Since it can block apoptosis, and thereby promote cell survival, Akt1 has been implicated as a major factor in many types of cancer. Akt (now also called Akt1) was originally identified as the oncogene in the transforming retrovirus, AKT8. In a mouse which is null for Akt1 but normal for Akt2, glucose homeostasis is unperturbed, but the animals are smaller, consistent with a role for Akt1 in growth. In contrast, mice which do not have Akt2, but have normal Akt1, have mild growth deficiency and display a diabetic phenotype (insulin resistance/ T2D), again consistent with the idea that Akt2 is more specific for the insulin receptor signaling pathway. The role of Akt3 is less clear, though it appears to be predominantly expressed in the brain. It has been reported that mice lacking Akt3 have small brains. This is what causes brain shrinkage in all the neurodegenerative disorders that are linked to cytochrome 1 issues. PCOS is tied to this early one before the more chronic diseases come to the forefront.
Akt2 is an important signaling molecule in the Insulin signaling pathway. It is required to induce glucose transport.

The follow through? AKT1 is involved in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and other signaling pathways. These all cause massive changes in cell cycle signaling and in cellular communication. mTOR is huge in longevity and in cancer progression via the dis-regulation of the p 53 gene.

Now you can see why when this pathway is activated many bad things can happen that are common to T1D, T2D, most cases of leptin resistance with elevations in HS CRP. These steps are stops on the way to either final pathway and we call them neolithic diseases. Yes.......you got an insight into Quantum Metabolic syndrome early.

We are not making it complicated......it is. This response is the proof of just how complicated it is.......and you need to have a lot of science background to understand this complexity......believe it or not I leave a lot of it out on purpose.......but not in this response.

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-12T21:49:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sleep-ever.6237/post-80232>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=80138)
>
> When you eat a ketogenic diet guess what else happens.......we bypass complex one at the inner mitochondrial membrane and enter FADH2. You heard about this in the Quantum electron post.........but what did not I tell you? Why does a ketogenic diet work in T1D, T2D, and to lose weight? When you lose water conduction at the inner mitochondrial membrane you lose the ability of proper nanoscopic protein folding of cytochrome 1. This throws off the nanoscopic precision required for quantum tunneling and this cause increased ROS and metabolic syndrome........This is also why niacin works in these diseases because it is a ketone mimic drug. Why? Niacin also known as vitamin B3, nicotinic acid and vitamin PP, is an organic compound with the formula C6H5NO2 and one of the 40 to 80 essential human nutrients. In fact, when niacin deficiency is present it is one of 5 vitamins that causes a pandemic disease condition called pellagra. But it can be used to bypass a "broken" cytochrome 1 when mis-folding (due to a chronic ATP deficiency think EMF 7). But why kathylu? Why am I tormenting you with this science,.......because you are biochemist........and you will see where QED takes us..........Vitamin B3 can help activate PPP when we are broken for some reason when we are ketotic from a dietary stand point using vast amounts of coherent water as I laid out in the quantum electron blog post. ( ) Yes, kathylu, niacin is a precursor to NAD+/NADH (cytochrome 1) and NADP+/NADPH (the magic of the PPP), which play essential metabolic roles in all living cells as I showed in EMF 4. Niacin cannot be directly converted to nicotinamide, but both compounds could be converted to NAD and NADP in vivo and this information is very valuable when you have liver leptin resistance, metabolic syndrome, or epilepsy. When cytochrome 1 has mis-folded electron transport proteins (quinolone issue of CoEnQ10) you need to bypass it 100% of the time by eating fats predominately because if you don't, your mitochondria begin to make massive ROS that overwhelms the cell lowers vitamin D and all hormones, causes pregnenolone steal syndrome, and eventually shortens its telomeres to cause cellular signaling problems that lead to either senesence or cancer. The reason why? NAD+ builds up in relation to NADH levels on a per unit basis. Increasing the NAD+ in comparison to NADH changes a most of the mechanistic downstream signalling in protein kinase B pathways of cells. Why is that big? The AKT pathway is also known as protein kinase B (PKB). It is a serine/threonine-specific protein kinase that plays a key role in multiple cellular processes such as **glucose metabolism, apoptosis, cell proliferation, transcription and cell migration.** Akt1 is involved in cellular survival pathways, by inhibiting apoptotic processes. Akt1 is also able to induce protein synthesis pathways, and is therefore a key signaling protein in the cellular pathways that lead to skeletal muscle hypertrophy, and general tissue growth. Since it can block apoptosis, and thereby promote cell survival, Akt1 has been implicated as a major factor in many types of cancer. Akt (now also called Akt1) was originally identified as the oncogene in the transforming retrovirus, AKT8. In a mouse which is null for Akt1 but normal for Akt2, glucose homeostasis is unperturbed, but the animals are smaller, consistent with a role for Akt1 in growth. In contrast, mice which do not have Akt2, but have normal Akt1, have mild growth deficiency and display a diabetic phenotype (insulin resistance/ T2D), again consistent with the idea that Akt2 is more specific for the insulin receptor signaling pathway. The role of Akt3 is less clear, though it appears to be predominantly expressed in the brain. It has been reported that mice lacking Akt3 have small brains. This is what causes brain shrinkage in all the neurodegenerative disorders that are linked to cytochrome 1 issues. PCOS is tied to this early one before the more chronic diseases come to the forefront.
> Akt2 is an important signaling molecule in the Insulin signaling pathway. It is required to induce glucose transport.
>
> The follow through? AKT1 is involved in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and other signaling pathways. These all cause massive changes in cell cycle signaling and in cellular communication. mTOR is huge in longevity and in cancer progression via the dis-regulation of the p 53 gene.
>
> Now you can see why when this pathway is activated many bad things can happen that are common to T1D, T2D, most cases of leptin resistance with elevations in HS CRP. These steps are stops on the way to either final pathway and we call them neolithic diseases. Yes.......you got an insight into Quantum Metabolic syndrome early.
>
> We are not making it complicated......it is. This response is the proof of just how complicated it is.......and you need to have a lot of science background to understand this complexity......believe it or not I leave a lot of it out on purpose.......but not in this response.

so If I understand this stuff (biochemist I'm not). but NAD+/NADH when elevated and you have a faulty cytrochorme 1 means that your increased leakiness (regardless of reason for said leakiness) = greater mis-regulation of cellular signaling and directly impacts longevity and can stop cellular death (apoptosis).

however, NADP+/NADPH is the sweet spot for PPP; assuming you are in ketosis, have sufficient Pg and leverage the cold. This is system where autophage works its magic - and why you can reach a zero entropy state. niacin is another way to trick the body into accessing the PPP, however you've got to be aware that if you have a faulty cytochrome 1 (PCOS, Diabetes, Leptin resistance etc) it won't convert to NADP+, but NAD+ instead, which is why its better to get into the PPP leveraging ketosis, Pg and the cold.

### kathylu — 2013-03-13T04:50:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sleep-ever.6237/post-80313>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=80138)
>
> Now you can see why when this pathway is activated many bad things can happen that are common to T1D, T2D, most cases of leptin resistance with elevations in HS CRP. These steps are stops on the way to either final pathway and we call them neolithic diseases. Yes.......you got an insight into Quantum Metabolic syndrome early.
>
> We are not making it complicated......it is. This response is the proof of just how complicated it is.......and you need to have a lot of science background to understand this complexity......believe it or not I leave a lot of it out on purpose.......but not in this response.

Hi Jack. I had to read this a few times to understand where you were going with your comment. I think you may have misinterpreted my intent. When I said "I think we often make things more complicated than they need to be", it was not a criticism of your logic, methods or intent. I do not feel "tortured" by the biochemistry...in fact, I love the complexity. It's just that I am always looking for a way to make things actionable. So, I have to ask myself if it is necessary for everyone to be able to know that the FADH2 production path in the Krebs cycle leads to more ROS and leakiness at cytochrome 1 and the hexose monophosphate shunt-PPP leads to the "magic" of the reductive path, more NADH which gives us higher glutathione levels and all the cellular benefits that brings? Maybe it is enough for most people to have a basic understanding of why ketotic, high fat diets can be corrective for a wide variety of conditions. That it can essentially "cure" type 2 diabetes, prevent cancer, slow or maybe even eliminate neurodegenerative disorders. I also love the connections you are drawing to quantum biology and the coherence of water, quantum tunneling and mis-folded proteins. But again, it always leads me to ask "What do I do now?", which if you think about it is the type of question I have always asked you in consults.

So, I guess I think the magic of being able to bring this critical knowledge to the largest numbers of people lies in simplifying and providing actionable techniques, not in the exploration of intricacies (no matter how much fun that is for geeky people like me). This is such a touchy opinion to express because I do not want to offend anyone, least of all, you. It's a laudable goal for the people who find these subjects fascinating (as I do), to work on acquiring the knowledge needed to grapple with the theoretical millieu, but I think most of us are simply crying out "What do I do now? How do I save myself, my family, my friends? How can I explain this to them in a way they will understand?"
Please understand, I am not asking you to "dumb down" anything you have been writing about. I personally like that you expect us to get with and stay with the program. I just feel that putting as much emphasis on the practical as on the theoretical can be helpful. Thus the genesis of my "reductive" statement!

### Jonathin — 2013-03-13T19:12:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sleep-ever.6237/post-80379>

kathylu

I think it is a real quandary regarding knowledge vs. action. It seems that action without knowledge eventually leads to a breakdown. If you don't know why you are doing something then you eventually will stop doing it or it mutates to being something different. After all the reason that we've all gone off into the ditch during out lives is because we were not really fully aware of our sense of well being and we did not have good intellectual knowledge to help get us on the right track. It was not necessarily because there were not caring people around us just that they were clueless about real health. On the other hand I think that people can start with some action plan but that needs to be followed up at some point with facts, context and explanations. I think we all have the impulse to help those around us but it becomes a problem if we don't approach it correctly.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-09T00:35:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-last-salivary-melatonin-test-crazy.6241/post-79735>

Sleep is huge...........This should have made the sleep Rx. [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/...effects\_n\_2807026.html?utm\_hp\_ref=mostpopular](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/03/06/scary-sleep-deprivation-effects_n_2807026.html?utm_hp_ref=mostpopular)

### HelenaVon — 2013-03-20T17:35:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-last-salivary-melatonin-test-crazy.6241/post-81187>

@Hope, I used genova diagnostics for my test..too expensive but it looks cool.

@Nonchalant, It was a normal night for me at the time of the test..I was having a lot of pains that keep me from sleeping. It is such a bad cycle of disaster ;(
I am always tired in the mornings. I was taking lots of melatonin @6x 6mg per night and I added 2 valariam root pills and mag. Just really wanting to get some sleep.but stopped all supplements 2 weeks prior to this test. So the test was taken with no added supplementation.
I discovered Celebrex and that helped with some of the pains and I got a little better sleep, but then one day it stopped working. I am taking Plaqunel right now I am curious what Dr.K thinks of that drug..I honestly can't really tell if it is helping or not.

Thanks for looking and inquiring about things.

To better health!

### mila — 2013-03-28T19:17:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-rx-links.6247/post-82066>

I think it is a big deal, too. I am usually asleep by 10 pm, up at 4.30 am all year-round. One thing I found interesting in your book is how you change your sleep schedule from season to season. This is something I am going to seriously consider. I do notice that in the winter time when we have fewer hours of daylight and here, in the southern hemisphere it is light earlier due to that dreaded daylight savings and dark earlier too, I feel sleepy a great deal earlier than 10 pm. I was actually thinking about going to sleep earlier during the winter and getting up earlier. After all, I don't have a clock to punch anymore and can pretty much do as I please.

### janagram — 2013-03-09T07:24:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79778>

ever since I got off sleeping pills about 1 and a half or 2 yrs ago I have taken several supps before bed. Melatonin I have used for years and then I added GABA and magnesium. sometimes I add valerian. Plus over the counter progest cream which my gyno says is placebo. This to get off Ambien. I do ok on this protocol....with some patchy nights. I get up 2-3 times per night to pee.....(not a good sign).
Please hack my sleep! Is it bad to take these supplements for extended time? Which ones should I quit? Or what labs should I have done?

### caroline — 2013-03-09T08:23:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79782>

Have you done a melatonin test? I take melatonin before bed as well. I think JK doesn't like melatonin and I keep forgetting to ask. Are you really paying attention to light cycles? Have you done a cortisol saliva test?

### nonchalant — 2013-03-09T16:00:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79800>

I believe Jack likes melatonin supplements only for a few reasons, jet lag is one of them, and I think for a short term to recover from something else (injury?). Not long term use. I had purchased some melatonin just before I heard him say that, and they have been sitting, unopened, in the cabinet for a long time.

### diane — 2013-03-09T16:01:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79801>

Janagram do you know your DHEA levels as well? And have you done any grounding outside? My best sleep comes when I've been outside for large parts of the day - even before I knew the benefits of grounding.

### janagram — 2013-03-09T17:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79803>

No, I don't know these levels. good place to start. thank you....I do not get enough sleep. ever. 5 hrs is about it.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-03-09T20:02:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-79826>

While Valerian is often considered a sedative, for some people it has the opposite effect, and works to stimulate rather than sedate. Other, more reliable sedative nervines include Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), California Poppy, Hops, and Pulsatilla. You can sometimes find them in combination. I have found herbal sedatives to be very useful for some people and situations.

### vkiernan — 2013-03-11T19:50:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-80071>

> [diane said:](/goto/post?id=79801)
>
> Janagram do you know your DHEA levels as well? And have you done any grounding outside? My best sleep comes when I've been outside for large parts of the day - even before I knew the benefits of grounding.

Me too, always sleep better after a day outside whether summer or winter. I have not read on my laptop in the evening for quite awhile now and my sleep is getting awesome. I don't have internet until my dh brings it home (that is usually late too) plus I live in the country where it has been tough to get internet in the first place. I only take my oral progesterone 1/2 my cycle and 1 mag malate now at bedtime and I actually sleep and could sleep some more if I had more time in the am. I am a true believer in the wrong light and emfs screwing us up.

Have you consulted Jack yet? With sleep like that, I think it would be a good investment. Without quality sleep, we are nothing. I really wonder how much autophagy you really have going on if you have to take all that stuff to keep your eyes closed at night? I feel for you and hope you find some good answers to get some real sleep soon.

### Hope — 2013-03-11T21:02:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-80080>

janagram, Do you ever CT in the evening? Even face? What's the temps in your room when you sleep? The cooler the better...

Would your MD be willing to give you 'real' PG (in pills) for your luteal phase at least? Do you notice your sleep getting worse with different phases in the cycle or is it always bad? Dr K has said oral PG pills may help with sleep, and it does wonders for me....of course that's my N=1 and YMMV but could be worth a try??

Have you tried drinking less starting in the very early evening to see if that helps with getting up for the bathroom at night? Or is it all the same?

Who gave you the sleeping pills? A sleep specialist? If so could you do a sleep study? I did one and learned I had pretty bad sleep apnea.

Have you always had sleep issues??

What about meditation, a calming routine at night? And no stress either in addition to darkness...if I get upset it's harder for me to relax well at night.

Good luck!

### Hope — 2013-03-11T22:49:13+0200

### fitness@home — 2013-03-12T14:23:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-80175>

> [janagram said:](/goto/post?id=79778)
>
> ever since I got off sleeping pills about 1 and a half or 2 yrs ago I have taken several supps before bed. Melatonin I have used for years and then I added GABA and magnesium. sometimes I add valerian. Plus over the counter progest cream which my gyno says is placebo. This to get off Ambien. I do ok on this protocol....with some patchy nights. I get up 2-3 times per night to pee.....(not a good sign).
> Please hack my sleep! Is it bad to take these supplements for extended time? Which ones should I quit? Or what labs should I have done?

Janagram, I had a bad experience with Ambien about 3-4 years ago. My doctor prescribed it because my sleep was so horrible at that time. Even melatonin wasn't having the desired effect. I loved it at first because my sleep was so much better. At six months, I had to talk her into a refilling the prescription...was pretty dependent on it by that point.

The bad stuff started happening when I went through a horribly stressful period in my life. IBS was acting up and had meds for that and Xanax. The combination of stress with the meds was not good. I started having short-term memory issues - had stickies everywhere!. Also very foggy thinking, and even had a hard time driving in the morning (like driving from home to work and thinking, where did the time go in between the two points?). That was my wake up call.

I weaned off of them by breaking in half and then quarters. I was not happy to lose the "sleep" but it wasn't a natural sleep. Things are better now. I sleep well most of the time.

Glad you are off the Ambien.

ayyyyy......yi...well, it's a slippery slope with drugs. Been there....had to get off Xanax and Ambien, but sleep has never been as good. ( a period of extreme anxiety got me into it). I'm interested in the sleep rx. I'll try to order it.

### SarahS85 — 2020-02-03T04:45:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-285514>

Hi Guys,

Just wondering if anyone knows which is the safest melatonin supplement to take for children and adults?

### Jack Kruse — 2020-03-03T03:48:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-286985>

When you take supplemental melatonin more times than not Nature will punish your circadian mechanism around PER1/PER2 NAD+/SIRT1 levels to favor chronic mitochondrial colony failure. Acute melatonin administration in humans impairs glucose tolerance in both the morning and evening. When you take melatonin as a supplement long enough be ready for retinal thinning and redox collapse of your mitochondria in many organs.
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4173928/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4173928/)

### Beristere — 2022-08-09T14:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-314937>

I also had trouble sleeping and took similar medications to help me fall asleep. But then I realized that giving them up would return sleep problems. It is also impossible to take such medicines constantly because, in addition to side effects, the effect of the medicines will weaken. Therefore, I gave up everything except the complex vitamins I buy in Online Pharmacy. What helped me was proper nutrition in combination with a vitamin complex, and sports also play a very important role. About 3 hours before bedtime, I go outside and work out on the horizontal bar for up to an hour and a half, then I come home, eat, wash, and I’m so tired that I can fall asleep much easier, and my sleep has become more qualitative.

### JanSz — 2022-08-09T15:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ever-since-i-got-off-sleeping-pills.6249/post-314939>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=286985)
>
> When you take supplemental melatonin more times than not Nature will punish your circadian mechanism around PER1/PER2 NAD+/SIRT1 levels to favor chronic mitochondrial colony failure. Acute melatonin administration in humans impairs glucose tolerance in both the morning and evening. When you take melatonin as a supplement long enough be ready for retinal thinning and redox collapse of your mitochondria in many organs.
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4173928/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4173928/)

I suspect that this study is suspected.
They used 5 mg melatonin.
And that is almost nothing when compared to 180mg melatonin.

>
> Energy Flow Technologyâ„¢
> Plugs in the heel and toe, made from a proprietary copper alloy with an antiqued finish, pass through the footbed and outsole of each sandal, connecting the wearer directly to the earth. Most people are surrounded daily, everywhere they go, by dynamic electrical fields and charges that the body absorbs: There is evidence that allowing the body to seek a level state of charge is health-beneficial to numerous biological processes; Juil's proprietary Energy Flow Technologyâ„¢ is designed to help the body discharge excess electricity, and along with it, damaging free radicals, achieving electrical homeostasis and improving health.
>

Got my DH a pair of Juli flip flops last year. The leather top part was a little stiff and he had to work them in but now he loves them! They have the copper conductors that go from all your toes and heel to the ground for grounding. They are the nicest pair of flip flops for men that I have ever seen. Almost like dress up flip flops!!!

### jen — 2013-03-22T14:46:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-81428>

I found some nicely priced sheepskin ballet flats here: [http://www.sheepskinfactory.com/sheepskinslippers.cfm](http://www.sheepskinfactory.com/sheepskinslippers.cfm) I asked before buying whether they were soft soled and they said yes, but when they arrived I found they did have a small rubber piece on the sole. I was able to remove it easily to just have the leather bottoms (they are unlined).

I am planning to ground some of my other shoes with copper craft wire, threading it through the soles (might need to drill a hole) and forming it into flat coils on the top and bottom.

### cinnamon — 2013-03-22T17:21:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-81452>

The other day a woman stopped me in the grocery store to tell me she loved my shoes. They were a pair I had bought from a thrift store years ago. Italian leather. The heel pad is some kind of composite material, but the soles are leather. When I rifled through the back of my closet to assess my shoes, I found that I have 3 different pair with leather soles! All were ancient thrift store purchases. And all Italian leather!

Hey, maybe the "mediterranean diet" success isn't just about red wine and olive oil? Maybe they've been wearing leather soled shoes?

### scghsv70 — 2013-04-05T20:48:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-83003>

Looking for a pair of all leather slippers to just knock about in at home and only $20? Check out adult Full Sole ballet slippers. I just received mine and they have full sole, sides, and innersole that are all leather....cotton lining on inside. I feel like I am learning how to walk differently in them..more like barefoot. Sizing is tricky. I wear a 9 1/2 S shoe and ordered a 7 1/2 A and it fits perfectly. they come in 4 widths. Sizing chart [http://www.tutu.com/dwear\_sizes\_balletsh.html](http://www.tutu.com/dwear_sizes_balletsh.html)

### Lahelada — 2013-07-06T17:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-89602>

This is a thought that had crossed my mind that shoewear could have an influence and that the right material could extend grounding time. I presume that this is what it is about . What materials are good apart from leather. Wood, cork,yute ?

### SeaHorse — 2013-07-07T17:42:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-89655>

Wood doesn't conduct well...espadrilles are ok if the soles aren't covered in plastic, I don't know about cork. I'm not sure it would conduct very well. I've been wearing juils, moccasins, leather soled boots and leather soled sandals all the time....I feel a difference right away if I get into anything else.

As for wool and cotton...I think wool keeps you dryer so that would mean less ability to receive the magnetic field, but it wouldn't block it completely. Cotton would probably be the best sock. Just my thoughts, I don't really know...

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-07-07T18:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-89657>

That's what I think, too, Sea. I also have been wearing leather-soled sandals, moccasins, and Juils...or I'm just barefoot 99% of the time. I sometimes wear my Chacos for ease of becoming barefoot as much as I can, and for rough-terrain protection when needed. Cotton socks are most conductive, wool might be conductive when feet sweat and they become moist. I saw an advertisement some time ago for conductive socks that were mostly cotton with a silver and copper thread sewn into them.

### NaturalHealthForLife — 2013-07-15T06:19:08+0300

### Tanager — 2013-08-07T23:17:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-91758>

I've been looking for grounding shoes. I think Juil shoes are beautiful, but I could never go walking in slip-on shoes -- they just don't stay on my feet.

I found this link to Soft Star shoes. You can get leather soles that work for grounding. They've been tested for grounding and for running by a barefoot runner-blogger, and there are youtube videos of them. They are handmade to order, so you can also get different widths.

Here's the link to their earthing shoes page: [http://articles.mercola.com/sites/a...&utm\_content=artTest\_A2&utm\_campaign=20130807](http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2099/12/31/superbugs-antibiotic-overuse.aspx?e_cid=20130807_DNL_artTest_A2&utm_source=dnl&utm_medium=email&utm_content=artTest_A2&utm_campaign=20130807)

Here's a video: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0uKwd6ewDcc](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0uKwd6ewDcc)

I'm thinking about ordering some. For now, though, I think I'll stick with my Hopi boot mocassins with rawhide soles I picked up on Arizona.

These "Teepee Boot Moccasins" also look interesting -- don't know if the "cushion insole" is removable, but they look comfy and very attractive. Too small for me, though. [http://www.indianvillagemall.com/footskin/teepeeboots.html](http://www.indianvillagemall.com/footskin/teepeeboots.html)

### Tanager — 2013-08-07T23:23:57+0300

### Tanager — 2013-08-07T23:45:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-91761>

Here's a link to some all-leather traditional Navajo moccasins. I find them quite stylish, but that's a matter of preference. I have some that are 2-button, but they are slow to put on, so I'd recommend 1-button or no-button if you like to take your shoes off indoors.

The sole is quite thick, one piece of solid leather. I don't know exactly how conductive they are, but I expect if they're damp they conduct pretty well, especially after you've worn them for a while and they have absorbed some salt.

[http://nativeleather.com/index.php?main\_page=index&cPath=3](http://nativeleather.com/index.php?main_page=index&cPath=3)

Here's a video on them. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XISKkhprnWE&list=UU-arcGjSLC7P\_7fGnFRPbEg](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XISKkhprnWE&list=UU-arcGjSLC7P_7fGnFRPbEg)

### caroline — 2013-08-08T02:52:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-91779>

Does anyone have any thoughts on Birkenstock Sandals? I would love to try the Juils but it cost $50.00 to deliver them here and then another $50.00 if they have to go back..

### Lahelada — 2013-08-08T17:03:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-91822>

It depends on why you are asking.If it is in terms of grounding and the materials used I have no worthwhile opinion to share as of yet.
However I do have experience in treating an old injury with them. I injured something in my knee riding a bike uphill while heavily overweight and it simply would not heal. I bought Birkies and they killed me as far as pain are concerned. For two weeks in I could not use them for more than two hours at a time. Then they became so comfortable that I wore them night and day until they wore out. The knee ? No more issues even after returning to normal shoes. In essence they are great posture correctors and take teh stress of misused joints. The wearing in period has to hurt as the cork adapts itself to your foot and makes you use muscles that you were unused to using in more modern footwear. If you order them ensure yourself that they have not been preworn even by someone for 2 hours . The bed should be pancake flat and have not even the slightest indentation. People unfamiliar with the principal idea of Birkies ( they have to hurt to be comfortable and useful thereafter) send them back saying they hurt them. That shoe should ideally not be reused as you then cannot leave your own print in the cork. Don't let anyone else use them not even to try.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-08-14T01:39:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-92427>

Bare feet. I've been wearing shoes that are easy to remove and I walk barefoot as much as possible when I'm out and about. If the terrain is rough, or the establishment might be offended, I slip them back on. Most people don't even notice. But the ones who do are always interested in talking about it! Moccasins would be a great investment! I love mine and with elk skin soles, they will last a long time.

### larawarnick — 2015-05-06T12:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-shoes-with-leather-soles.6273/post-163438>

# Thread: Side Effects of Tanning

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15

### BJK77 — 2013-03-11T19:26:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80068>

I know quite a few of us have been hitting the tanning beds and I'm wondering if anybody else is getting super dry skin from it? My stomach is looking pretty nasty with all its flakiness right now!

Do you also find that you could totally take a nap in the bed if you could just stay in a bit longer? I find earthing to make me super sleepy too. I just tend to feel so calm and relaxed that I could doze right off. I almost fell asleep in the front yard the other day while earthing, but my neighbor ended up ruining it for me by shouting across the street to ask if I was "sun bathing." My reply: "No, I'm getting free electrons!" I don't think he'll ask me again . . .

### DarleenMB — 2013-03-11T20:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80073>

Nope. My skin has been dry-ish (esp my legs) all winter in spite of coconut oil, CT and lots of dietary fats.

Nope. The noise of the fan and everything has a kind of white noise effect but there's no way I could fall asleep.

### shilohman — 2013-03-11T20:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80077>

Dr. K also said somewhere on the forum that you need to be supplementing with V D3 when you start to tan.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80096>

### HeidiHen — 2013-03-17T01:35:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80819>

My skin seems to be fine so far. But then, I've only gone three times so far, all last week. What I have noticed is that on the three nights after going tanning, my sleep has been awful! I've woke up all three nights at about 2 am and laid in bed until 4 or 5 before falling back asleep. I wonder if maybe I should only tan in the morning? All three times I went between 3-5 in the afternoon. I have a feeling that's too intense of light for that late in the afternoon. Any one else experience this? What time do you tan?

### endless — 2013-03-17T01:45:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80820>

I think earlier is better...I haven't experimented but I sort of instinctively felt that tanning late afternoon might affect my sleep.

### HeidiHen — 2013-03-17T02:26:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/side-effects-of-tanning.6288/post-80822>

I'm honestly a bit nervous to go again for fear that I'll have another sleepless night. I'll try early morning and see if it makes a difference. Hopefully morning will not affect me so much.... As an aside, I encountered an older gentleman at the local HFS trying to convince the nice store employee that he was so strong and healthy because he did not eat any meat, and that nobody should ever eat pork. Ruby, thanks for the tidbits. This one below is close to my heart since my father in law was sentenced to a hard labor in this radioactive mine Joachimstal (Jachymov) during the communist regime.

Radium Bread

Radium water from Joachimstal was used in the production of loaves manufactured by the Hippman-Blach bakery in what is now the Czech Republic. Although the production technique would have led to an increase in radium levels, the amount present in the bread was not dangerous.
Written by: Thomas Davie
Read more at [http://www.environmentalgraffiti.co...ctually-used/1388?image=3#Js7yCv0vEeMsLk3A.99](http://www.environmentalgraffiti.com/offbeat-news/10-radioactive-products-that-people-actually-used/1388?image=3#Js7yCv0vEeMsLk3A.99)

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-14T00:25:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-tri-field-readings-batman.6313/post-80425>

so the trifield meter came today ...turned it on.. low and behold the kids room is off the charts - literally w/the exception of the wall they share with the bathroom... so tonight is furnature re-aranging time and it looks like emf paint is the 1st house hold project we're going to do...

havoc's bed lays along the highest measurements... changing that tonight...

### SCRN2007 — 2013-03-14T00:52:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-tri-field-readings-batman.6313/post-80427>

Yikes, I tremble to think what my house is like. Still saving up for the meters. An engineer friend told me that the Tri-field is only useful for detecting magnetic fields, like from an electric motor or home wiring, but that it is next to useless for quantifying radiofrequency waves. The description from the manufacturer regarding the Tri-Field seems to say the same thing. For an RF meter, he said you have to spend $300 to get an accurate amateur version. Given all of my neighbors' WiFi, DECT phones, and smart meters, I think I will need both types of meters. We can't decide whether to spend money on shielding or the meters first. I have my daughter's bed as far from outlets as possible, but I also have it away from the outside wall because there is a cell tower 1380 meters away from that side of the house. When I mapped out all of the cell towers in our area, we really are about as far away from them as you can be without worrying that someone is going to put one up next door next week--and we live in a rural area! They are literally in a circle around our house with the 1380 meter-away one being the closest.

At least smart meters weren't invented at that time.

Glad you got one! Don't forget to check the ceilings...I had hot wires running through mine. And the electric range...off the meter, even when I am not using it!

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-03-14T03:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-tri-field-readings-batman.6313/post-80464>

Just got my Trifield meter. Very interesting. Focused on my computer desk area since I work there. Currently have 3 monitors (2 LCD and 1 LED). The oldest LCD (manufacture date 2006) was high off the charts .... even when both cables were unplugged ... guess it really retains a charge. That monitor was retired today. The LED monitor barely registered, and the other LCD monitor was mid-range.

But the most interesting discovery was with the mouse. .... it registered very high, and I use the mouse alot more than my keyboard. Guess that explains why I have more inflammation in my right hand than left. Apparently all mice manufactured currently have either optical or laser sensors; so that seems to explain the high EMF of a mouse.

Solution for that seems difficult. Either find an old 2 button mouse w/ no optical, or find a good way to ground myself. The latter seems more feasible ... anxiously awaiting the EMF webinar so I can learn more about safe and effective grounding.

our bedroom is really good - except for the alarm clocks - the cool thing is the tri-field meter brought everything home for dh....

### jenaf — 2013-03-14T18:44:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strength-gains-in-two-weeks.6324/post-80516>

Hi everyone,

Just wondering if anyone, since cutting back EMF exposure and drinking 3-4 litres of spring water a day has noticed any differences? I've been using epi-paleo, mmm oysters!!, for the last year to try to recover from massive adrenal fatigue (some CT but I'm going to hit it up more soon), and in the last month introduced grounding in the ocean 2-3 times a week. But in the last two weeks I haven't grounded at all, but I've made a huge effort to drink 2-4 L of local non flouridated spring water everyday and shutting off the breaker to my bedroom, washer/dryer, microwave and turning off WiFi when not using it. (and FINALLY getting a full nights sleep!!!)

and in the last two weeks I've gained 6-7 lbs but my clothes are loose (i can comfortably wear a pair of pants I haven't worn in about 8 months - so dropped close to a dress size). Today I also decided, hmmm I'll try to do a turkish get up with my 16kilo kettlebell - haven't done that in almost a year, and the last time i did one was about 3 months ago with a 12k bell, and that was difficult. Bam - easy up, both sides.

Anyone else notice any differences like that?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-15T00:23:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strength-gains-in-two-weeks.6324/post-80581>

### Inger — 2013-03-15T07:58:12+0200

### Rachelle — 2013-03-14T21:30:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-able-to-use-magnesium-now.6327/post-80543>

Not sure what happened but I mentioned before that when I used mag malate or theronate my limbs got got, extremely, heavy and I was fatigued the next day. I just tried it again 3 weeks ago and it did the same, and the only thing I changed was to stop drinking fluoridated water. Have only been drinking spring water now for 2 weeks and decided to try both mag malate and theronate again for the last few days and have no heavy limbs or fatigue at all.

I am very tuned into my body and have been for many years. I normally add one protocol at a time for this very reason. Been sleepiing much better also and I now believe sleep more important than food.

Anyone know what is up with that?

I've never drank fluoridated water though, so that's definitely not my issue. I have really been increasing my water intake though and getting at elast 3/4 of a gallon daily now. Maybe I'll have to try the Mg again too.

# Thread: Fasting or not?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2013-03-14T21:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80549>

I find this so frustrating. Every doctor I deal with has a different opinion on whether or not I should be fasting for these tests, soooo I've decided I trust all you experts much more than I do the 'so-called' experts

Do you fast for the following:

Thyroid panels
Sex hormones (PG, DHEA, E2, T, etc)
hsCRP

I'm also getting a cholesterol panel and glucose, which I always fast for and a vitamin D that I've never fasted for.

### caroline — 2013-03-15T00:51:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80588>

I don't think so - but I am certainly no expert. I just learned that you can be fasted for too long before Glucose testing. I usually eat dinner around 4:00 or 4:30 pm .... so now I really have to think about having some food around 6 or 7pm or even as late as 8pm. I usually get tests after seeing my Doc and if he is running behind I can be over fasted.

### caroline — 2013-03-15T00:52:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80589>

I usually call the lab myself now and ask about requirements ....

### diane — 2013-03-15T01:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80592>

When I had mine done they didn't require fasting for thyroid and sex hormones. I did for the hs crp, but that's because I got it done with all my other labs.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-03-15T02:08:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80612>

Answer seems easy ... if all labs are drawn at the same time, and even one lab requires fasting (i.e. glucose), then you fast.

I always fast and always test at the same time in the morning .... With consistent fasting, and draws at the same time, I can rule out one variable.

### caroline — 2013-03-15T04:56:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80638>

>
> Answer seems easy ... if all labs are drawn at the same time, and even one lab requires fasting (i.e. glucose), then you fast.
>
> I always fast and always test at the same time in the morning .... With consistent fasting, and draws at the same time, I can rule out one variable.

Great advice - thanks....

### Destiny — 2013-03-15T12:44:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80657>

My GP doesn't mind to draw my cholesterol when I am not fasting. He says that actually you see what is happening in your day to day that way.

That makes a sense to me.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-15T12:50:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-or-not.6328/post-80658>

The best way to test cholesterol is fasting but after 32 oz of water........todays blog shows you why. This is why so many people have elevated LDL on testing.......and guess who tells you not to drink before the test? The guys who make statins.......just another truth bomb.

>

I'm trying to be smarter about it this time  Sooo if I can have some done in the morning and some in the afternoon (preferably in the afternoon so I don't have to mess up my BAB), that's what I'm hoping to do!

I'm on 4 grains of NDT (was diagnosed a few years ago after over 10 years of trying to GET diagnosed) and will be getting sex hormones tested this next month. I'm 100 pounds overweight despite my best efforts and have the energy of a slug. A 32 year old, female, dead slug.

Anyone have anything to say about this? I'm all ears (so to speak). I can post the other labs if necessary.

**TSH 0.043 (0.450-4.500)**
Thyroxine T4 5.0 (4.5-12.0)
T3 Uptake 30 (24-39)
Free Thyroxine Index Free T4 1.5 (1.2-4.9)
Triiodothyronine, Free Free T3 2.9 (2.0-4.4)

AM Cortisol 26.3 (2.3-19.4)** (I know, I need to get the saliva test)

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-15T11:37:26+0200

And I have NO idea what it means to have zero DHEA. That seems ... bad. You need a really good doctor, stat!! I suspect your life may depend on it.

### Slone07 — 2013-03-15T22:59:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-dhea-hashis-thyroid-oh-my.6330/post-80701>

LOL - Well, that doesn't sound hopeful, y'all.

My doc started me on DHEA, some cortisol lowering/calming supplements, vitamin D3 (yes, he wants it up, up, up) and wants to try some anti-virals to try to modulate my autoimmune issues (I'm still looking into this, but what he told me about it sounded promising). I took a DHEA on the way home from his office and within ten minutes, the mind-numbing headache I've had for the past couple of weeks vanished. It didn't wander off...it just disappeared. Made the hour long drive back home much more pleasant. It's currently a few hours later and I still don't have a headache.

As for D3, that one's nothing new to me. I've always been low, trying to get into the upper half of the range. Hey, at least this time it wasn't 13 with the same range...  I've been out of it for quite a while and between that and the apathy that takes over when I feel bad, I just never ordered more. I do live in the deep south, so plenty of sunlight here, but I do need to try to get outside more. I tend to avoid it, because I burn so easily (and I hate sunscreen). It's also exhausting, for some reason.

I'm on Thyroid-S from Thailand. I've been self-treating since my old endocrinologist retired a few years ago and left me with her replacement who was appalled I was on "that old stuff" (Armour) and wanted me immediately on Synthroid. Been looking for a non-TSH doctor and three days ago...I finally found one. It's been at least 3 years, though, of slowly increasing my dose based on symptoms. It kept going up and up and 4 grains was kind of a point that I wasn't willing to go past on my own. Instinctually, I knew that there was way more wrong with me than just my thyroid and Hashi's. Anyway, the new doc recommended not going up to 4.5 grains given that he's starting me on the other stuff and said that my body may begin dealing with the thyroid hormone a little better soon.

I'm getting thyroid (and antibodies) and cortisol retested in 6-8 weeks and DHEA and other hormones in 3 months after DHEA has had some time to work.

I knew there was a reason I felt like I was 95. It's because I am! On the inside, anyway. LOL

ETA: I must be feeling a little better...I used two emoticons.

### caroline — 2013-03-16T00:17:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-dhea-hashis-thyroid-oh-my.6330/post-80707>

Think about doing a saliva/ cortisol test now so you have a baseline. Progesterone is really important too.....

### Inger — 2013-03-15T08:07:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/may-be-harder-than-we-think-to-get-away-from-emf.6332/post-80643>

Yesterday at work a guest asked me what wifi he can use (because we have our own wifi at the restaurant for the guests) and he showed me he's laptop and all the different wifi's showing up there... I got SHOCKED. I bet there was at least 20....

A 25 yo coworker told me yesterday he just had brain surgery because of a brain tumor... he is vegetarian too... and a other young lady coworker told me she has gotten really bad panic attacs and have to take heavy medication to be able to work at all.. they got so much worse after around Christmas this year (when almost all got a smart meter installed).. She has wifi too at home she told.. and all her neighbours has too.
I told her it could very well be the radiations and I told what she could do to protect her.
I tell all my coworkers what they can do. They do think I am nuts...lol but I do not care one bit. They know I love them and they see how I am doing and maybe one day they will not want to suffer anymore too. This is totally getting out of hand recently.... I mean.. huh it is getting worse FAST!

### TheKid — 2013-03-15T10:10:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/may-be-harder-than-we-think-to-get-away-from-emf.6332/post-80644>

Yeah, I think the race is on. Now you see why Jack ain't waiting around to hear more bad news. I can see it in myself.

The problem is, I think in most cases EMF's effects aren't as noticeable. You don't realize it until it's too late. And the biggest concern with EMF is cancer. You don't feel Oncogenesis. By the time you feel the cancer, you're in deep trouble.

So people who downplay EMF do so, I believe, based on a lack of clear symptoms. They don't realized that when you start getting palpitations, GI manifestations, migraines, cancer ... you're already pretty far down the road.

Day after day I read about people with cancer diagnoses that seemed to pop out of thin air. Young people. Guess what, it should take you years of diet-driven metabolic derangement to come up with a cancer diagnosis. And before that, you would see a helluva lot of other medical conditions that should be warning signs. So when I see kids showing up with Leukemia and other cancers and other exotic diseases, I'm not thinking diet. I'm thinking acute toxin exposure and I'm thinking emf.

# Thread: Stressed out by 'stuff'
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-03-16T22:17:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80811>

Just came across this great article.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/10/o...ith-less-a-lot-less.html?pagewanted=all&\_r=3&](http://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/10/opinion/sunday/living-with-less-a-lot-less.html?pagewanted=all&_r=3&)

I resonated particularly with this:

In a study published last year titled â€œLife at Home in the Twenty-First Century,â€￾ researchers at U.C.L.A. observed 32 middle-class Los Angeles families and found that all of the mothersâ€™ stress hormones spiked during the time they spent dealing with their belongings. Seventy-five percent of the families involved in the study couldnâ€™t park their cars in their garages because they were too jammed with things.

Although I've never lived in more than 1,200 sq ft of space, I do feel every day like we have too much 'stuff'. I've been getting rid of 'stuff' little by little but I sure would love to get rid of most of my stuff. I find books are the hardest....ugh.

I do remember when we moved, and the movers had packed up everything except what we would take, I felt so much lighter and....free. Maybe that's cause our ancestors had so little too

### caroline — 2013-03-17T00:18:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80813>

I think that is so true Hope....I hate all the "stuff"

When we moved to Australia we sent everything by a slow route so we wouldn't be in a position to just take any place we found because we had to. We found our place a few days after we got here and lived without our stuff foor 4 months.

We bought a bed and a patio set and a TV and a couple of plates and glasses etc and that is how we lived. It was heaven!

### Hope — 2013-03-17T01:56:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80821>

THat's so cool....where did you move from? I'd love to hear!

We went without our stuff for three months, too, though in our case it was in storage all that time....and I didn't miss anything except for my favorite kitchen knife. I would live that way again, too.....put all our stuff in storage, live in a furnished room till we figure out the next step. It's just so so nice to have much less stuff around.

### diane — 2013-03-17T03:40:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80824>

6 years ago we had to move out of our condo into a temporary condo. Insurance put all of our stuff into storage and we just took our day to day stuff. Then when we moved back (which was 9 months later) we cleaned everything out as we realized over that time we didn't need as much stuff. Now, 6 years later, stuff has accumulated again. Blech. Makes me want to purge it!

### caroline — 2013-03-17T06:51:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80844>

Yep - same here.....a bunch more stuff has accumulated...... I think I would love to live in a caravan and just travel around. This country is great for that.

Hope - we moved here from Canada.

### caroline — 2013-03-17T06:53:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80845>

I understand that you can get a caravan and part of the roof can slide away and you are sleeping under the stars! How amazing would that be???

### Inger — 2013-03-17T07:12:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80846>

Oh yes.. and then one could just drive to sleep in a place far from all Wifi's..yay!

You all made me want to start sorting out and purging stuff.. I like to have only few things and the things I do have must be stuff of high value to me. When I go shopping I often say to myself.. hmmm, that I do not need, and that not either, also not that.. wow is it amazing to need so little. I have made it to a habit to really think twice if I need something. That has resulted in me buying almost nothing the last years. IDK but it feels so blessed.  Like i have really started to value stuff I have instead!

### prAna303 — 2013-03-17T08:15:09+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-17T16:59:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80864>

Reach for the inner reaches of your outer spaces. A sculptor finds their inner Michelangelo in a hunk of marble by asking the marble what it wants to become. Today begin to remove the soot covering you one chip at a time to reveal the exquisite you buried inside by bad thinking. Transformational change never happens by eureka discoveries, but it begins by removing the excess that blinds us. You don't find yourself.......you create yourself.

### Rachelle — 2013-03-17T21:56:23+0200

Sure makes ya think.

I always feel freer when I weed stuff out. It's important to me to feel free . I really need to delve into that someday.

A work of changing myself from caring about anger and resentment to caring about nothing in particular and then letting that empty space fill with some blessed things. ### Hope — 2013-03-17T23:46:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80903>

Very well put, Diane.

I wonder too how our history - upbringing - shapes us too. I am so thankful to have made more progress on this, letting things go. My mother - who grew up in Germany in WWII and its aftermath, who had nothing forever and used to have to eat potato skins with candle fat (there was nothing else for some time in the deepest years of the war, in the winter etc) - has an especially hard time giving things up, though I admire her incredibly for her courage and going through WW II hell, losing her mother at age 7 and many uncles in the war....and I think it must stem partly from that. She has clothes that are 30 years old. Still in great shape. Out of style, but in great shape. She just can't let them go....

### Rachelle — 2013-03-18T15:27:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-80970>

@Hope...Those clothes she won't give up I would wear. The clothes they make today are so cheaply made--Ugh!!!. I would rather be out of style than wear what they are offering today for a big price. ### Hope — 2013-03-19T02:12:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stressed-out-by-stuff.6363/post-81035>

Rachelle, how sad about the songbirds. You really have none? That is awful. I get such joy from watching and listening to the birdies, even the sparrows etc. So sweet. oh - I think my mom still wears most of her clothes. She says the same thing about hers.

That is so great you buy mostly from thrift shops. Some of my favorites come from there too  I never heard that about hemp. But I personally also LOVE all natural fibers. Especially organic...but how cool!!

Kathylu, that is sad about the slogan. I try so hard to get away from chemicals, LOL. I am so sensitive now to even smells that are artificial, I hate when the neighbor dries clothes in the drier and the steam comes out next to our front door - those artificial fragrances, yuck.

# Thread: How Mitochondria Make ATP - Tossing Out The Old Theory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-mitochondria-make-atp-tossing-out-the-old-theory.6367/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bogman — 2013-03-17T07:40:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-mitochondria-make-atp-tossing-out-the-old-theory.6367/post-80848>

.
I haven't seen anything on here about the updated research on how Mitochondria make ATP. Seems like Jack is still using the old method that was taught a long time ago? I'm still reading through all the info being new here.... In the old days; high school biology taught the Sodium Pump within cells - which is now very outdated theory.

**Looking at the amazing work of Dr Ling:** Oxidative Phosphorylation and Mitochondrial Physiology: A Critical Review of Chemiosmotic Theory, and Reinterpretation by the Association-Induction Hypothesis

The concept that the basic unit of all life, the cell, is a membrane-enclosed soup of (free) water, (free) K+ (and native) proteins is called the membrane theory. A careful examination of past records shows that this theory has no author in the true sense of the word. Rather, it grew mostly out of some mistaken ideas made by Theodor Schwann in his Cell Theory.

Revolutionary findings cast a new and different light on the subject of ATP synthesis. The emphasis is shifted from ionic or "osmotic" gradients, central to the membrane-pump theory, to ion adsorption on proteins, central to the Al hypothesis. XCH2CHEOOH

After the major discovery of ATP and its pivotal role in cell metabolism and function, ATP was believed to contain a high-energy bond in which an unusually large amount of potential energy was stored. Since the 1950s it has become clear that this concept is not correct. The enthalpy of hydrolysis of the ATP phosphate group is not unusually high; the free energy of hydrolysis of ATP is largely due to the liberation of H+ in an environment maintained at physiological pH as well as to other extraneous factors.

ATP acts as a cardinal adsorbent

This all ties into how, who and what cells function. The liquid inside a cell is structured. The old theory was it was some where between water & ice - only some what structured. Digging deep into the work of Dr Ling is amazing - starting in the late 1950's. Interesting work - I've found it fun to follow the extensive research.

### freesia — 2013-03-17T09:34:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-mitochondria-make-atp-tossing-out-the-old-theory.6367/post-80855>

[http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-7-quantum-prometheus/](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-7-quantum-prometheus/)

Try this one

PS Agreed here, Ling's work is fascinating!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-17T13:50:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-mitochondria-make-atp-tossing-out-the-old-theory.6367/post-80858>

freesia.......bogman is not staying up......and Ling preceded Mitchell's work........to be clear. Biology ignored it because they could not fathom we were made this way.......and then Becker showed definitively we are made of semiconductors.

Used to live there and knew people who would drive up to the spring and would haul huge amounts back to their homes down the mountain....so I know the source.

I have been taking my FBG every morning and in the last week or so, I notice that when I prick my finger my blood lays flat on my finger and the uptake on the strip is very quick compared to a month ago. That was when my blood would form a bubble on my finger and seemed stickier. It seems that the flouride free spring water has entered my blood supply.

Am I correct? Is this to be expected with flouride free spring water? And is this a good sign that my nanotubes are starting to work properly...not that I ever even knew I had nanotubes!

### nonchalant — 2013-03-18T03:46:58+0200

### Inger — 2013-03-18T06:48:56+0200

### Dextery — 2013-03-18T12:50:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flouride-free-spring-water-and-blood-viscosity.6372/post-80947>

This is getting interesting to me. [http://www.herbprof.com/prof\_notes.htm](http://www.herbprof.com/prof_notes.htm) Be sure to follow the link as there is more about the blue zone of Seventh Day Adventists around Loma Linda Hospital in California

_Reducing Heart Attack Risk

What does this mean for those concerned with minimizing heart attack risk? According to the researchers, “…this is the first study to record the association between high water intake and reduced risk of coronary heart disease.” As Dr. Chan had stated in the press release and during conferences on her findings, “…by drinking more plain water, healthy people—without any history of heart disease, stroke, or diabetes—reduced their risk of dying from a heart attack by half or more. This is as much or more than if they had adopted any other well-known preventive measure, including stopping smoking and lowering cholesterol levels, increasing exercise or maintaining ideal weight.” Dr. Chan continues, “…the degree of benefit from drinking plain water surpasses that of drinking a moderate amount of alcohol intake and aspirin with none of the adverse side effects \[social or physiological\]. Because drinking more plain water is a simple lifestyle change that anybody can do, this simple practice has the potential of saving tens of thousands of lives each year with minimal cost.”_

_It is imperative to distinguish pure water from other beverages. As Dr. Chan puts it, “Neither total fluid intake, nor intake of other fluids combined showed this reduced risk. Instead, for women, high intake (5 or more glasses a day) of other fluids showed a greatly increased risk of coronary heart disease.” To emphasize a critical point, Dr. Chan adds, “People need to be made aware that there is a difference, at least for heart health, whether they get their fluids from **plain water** or from sodas.”_

Is this nature's coumidin or warfarin for protection against another heart attack?

Why have we not heard about the role of water in heart health? Maybe because big Pharma cannot make any money prescribing water.

Nothing was said specifically about flouride free water but I'll take Dr K's rec and stick with my flouride free spring water.

# Thread: Input Wanted - Member Projects
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-03-18T14:26:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80961>

I have had this idea for a while, but yesterday I was watching a youtube video and a structure came to mind. I imagine many of you have had a similar idea. Member research projects.

Here's how it could work. A member wants to know more about a specific topic and is willing to work on researching the topic.

Post a project proposal and ask 1) is there interest in the topic and 2) is anyone willing to volunteer to work on the project.

If there are both interest and volunteers, form a project team, define the project, assign tasks, etc, and publish the final report.

I'm purposely leaving out detail so we can build the concept together (if there is interest).

Here's the [video](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=62cNtvx6P8E) that got me motivated. I think if we had a few volunteers, we could develop a map of best and worst places to live, and why.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-18T14:33:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80962>

I like it........and when the research is done.......a collaborative blog can be written by the project leader.........I would be willing to post it. We could call it the Member Series.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-18T14:55:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80966>

Sounds great! I'd like to participate.

### kathylu — 2013-03-18T15:09:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80967>

I'm in! I love research....

### Dali Dula — 2013-03-18T15:27:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80971>

I have lately been envisioning a similar concept. I think it would be useful to try to prove Jack wrong on a given subject. This would not be to trash Jack. I am obviously a fan as a result of actionable protocols producing tremendous results for me. This would tie in well with the project concept. By trying to refute a given theory we would come to understand it in greater depth than when we just accept it. Ideally this process would not be emotionally driven. Although, given the amount of data in all subject areas that apparently conflicts with itself this may be a futile exercise.
A group research project of any type sounds like fun. I'm in.

### Huck — 2013-03-18T19:48:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80991>

I have an idea for our initial project. It's one that is timely and requires varying degrees of expertise.

Title: Magnetic, Electric, and Radio/Microwave Measurement Using a Trifield(T) Meter

1) How to use the meter
2) Readings for varying devices/appliances at specific distances

For those who have a meter or want to buy a meter, we would develop a how to tutorial. I have a meter but not really sure how to use it or the data from it. At this point, I'm not getting my money's worth out of it. I can point it at something and read the needle, but what the heck does it mean?

Build a database or spreadsheet of readings. Probably use distances like 0, 1, 2, 3 meters. For people who can't afford or choose not to buy a meter, this would give them ballpark readings they could use to reposition furniture. It would also let people compare their appliances to the average range from data we collect. We would establish the parameters and then get as many people as possible to send us their readings.

This would have a relatively short time requirement and hopefully be available soon after the EMF Webinar.

What is available (free) for collaboration? What tools do we need? Is email and google docs good enough?

I think a project team of 3 to 5 people is right for the 1st part of this project. Send me a PM if you are interested and we can start planning the project.

### Huck — 2013-03-18T19:51:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-80993>

My above post wasn't meant to stop anyone else from starting a project. My point was that the 1st project will hopefully be able to define tools, etc that other projects can use without having to start from scratch.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-18T20:48:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81004>

This is a great idea. I would like to participate in some fashion as well. Sounds like we need a project manager to manage the whole thing.

### Hope — 2013-03-18T23:19:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81021>

Yes, count me in too. Love research and studying. Just have to print and read it all OFFline. LOL.

### Destiny — 2013-03-19T00:42:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81025>

I am trying to limit my time on computer but I would like to help as well.

### BJK77 — 2013-03-19T01:01:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81026>

What a fantastic idea. I'd love to help too, but unfortunately I'm in the midst of some huge projects at work right now so I wouldn't be able to help on this particular project. I'd definitely love to help out on a future project though!

### cantweight — 2013-03-19T01:12:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81027>

I'd be in for a summer project. Nursing school kicking my ass till mid May

Christi

### Huck — 2013-03-19T19:30:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81111>

Need 1 or 2 more people for Magnetic, Electric, and Radio/Microwave Measurement Using a Trifield(T) Meter Project.

EE background would be helpful. If you don't have time for research, but could answer questions and be a technical reviewer, that would be a big help.

### Martin — 2013-03-19T20:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81116>

Great idea, Huck. I'm looking at making body voltage measuring device using inexpensive parts and a decent multi-meter. I'm wanting to post pics so others can build it as well and save money and learn something.

I'm looking at gauss meters and how to get the most for my money. I love good tools and have made my living with the best I could afford, which means compromising somewhat if it will only be used a few times. On tools that I use constantly, getting a great one was an investment.

I like the Tri-Field, but according to inspectors who go out and measure for clients, it has limitations. But, like I said, we ain't going into the biz of getting hired and going out into the field. Then again... it could be an emerging occupation!

I'm looking at ones in the $250 range. Pricey compared to many others, it's inexpensive compared to professional models that cost thousands! I want accuracy, reliability, repeatability, and some tech support.

I have an idea of offering basic preliminary on-site measuring for the people in my town for two reasons - spread the awareness and help pay for the meter. I don't know how much people want this investigated, but I'd think it would be fun to go and show people this.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-20T15:49:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81178>

Everyone who is interested, please PM Huck if you haven't done so already.

### CarolMorris — 2013-03-20T17:23:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/input-wanted-member-projects.6380/post-81185>

Wonderful idea...I would love to contribute...there are so many great people on this forum that really "get it" and I struggle constantly with the science. I found my way here  that's a good thing and I am extremely grateful for all of you and Dr. Jack!! I'm willing to give it my best...give me the grunt work Anyone else feel this way?

**Posts:** 25

### Dextery — 2013-03-18T18:10:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-structured-water-grounding-interview-by-dr-mercola.6384/post-80981>

Dr K has mentioned elsewhere that Dr Pollack's work on structured water is groundbreaking. I found this interview very enlightening along with the article.

[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2011/01/29/dr-pollack-on-structured-water.aspx](http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2011/01/29/dr-pollack-on-structured-water.aspx)

### diane — 2013-03-18T20:15:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-structured-water-grounding-interview-by-dr-mercola.6384/post-80998>

### GlenPDQ — 2013-03-19T03:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-structured-water-grounding-interview-by-dr-mercola.6384/post-81052>

Maybe that explains why exposure to my quartz tube infrared heater has helped a trouble spot on my skin.

Also, I can get bottles of water down below 32F in my freezer. It freezes when I take it out of the freezer and give it a hard bump. The bump must shift the proton bonds.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-03-19T04:10:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-structured-water-grounding-interview-by-dr-mercola.6384/post-81054>
